pages.title: IRS Letter 6612: Your ERC Claim is Being Audited
pages.description: Received IRS Letter 6612? Learn what it means, your 30-day deadline, and how to respond to protect your ERC claim.
pages.h1: IRS Letter 6612: What It Means and How to Respond

content.urgency: You have 30 days to respond. Missing this deadline can result in full disallowance of your ERC claim.
content.intro: If you've received IRS Letter 6612, your Employee Retention Credit claim is officially under examination. The IRS has sent over 84,000 of these audit notification letters to businesses across the country. This letter marks the beginning of the ERC audit process and comes with a strict 30-day deadline that you cannot afford to miss.

sections.heading: What is IRS Letter 6612?
sections.content:
Letter 6612 is the IRS's official notification that your ERC claim has been selected for audit. Unlike a simple inquiry, this letter signals that the IRS intends to thoroughly examine your claim to determine if you legitimately qualified for the credit.

The letter typically arrives with Form 4564 (Information Document Request), which contains 13 multi-part questions requesting specific documentation about your eligibility. This is not a form letter you can ignore—it's the first step in a formal audit process.

sections.heading: Why Did You Receive This Letter?
sections.content: The IRS is targeting ERC claims for several reasons:

list.title: High-risk indicators
list.description: Your claim may contain elements the IRS considers suspicious, such as claiming full quarters or large amounts without clear eligibility documentation.

list.title: ERC mill involvement
list.description: If your claim was filed by an aggressive promoter, it may have been flagged for review.

list.title: Random selection
list.description: Some claims are selected randomly as part of the IRS's compliance efforts.

list.title: Industry patterns
list.description: Certain industries have higher audit rates due to patterns of improper claims.

sections.heading: The 30-Day Deadline: Why It Matters
sections.content: The response deadline on Letter 6612 is not flexible. If you fail to respond within 30 days:

list.title: Automatic disallowance
list.description: The IRS may deny your entire claim without further review.

list.title: Loss of appeal rights
list.description: Failing to respond can limit your options for challenging a denial.

list.title: Penalties and interest
list.description: If you've already received your refund, you may owe it back plus penalties and interest.

sections.warning: The 30-day deadline is calculated from the date on the letter, not the date you received it. Check your letter immediately and count backwards to ensure you have enough time.

sections.heading: What Documents Does the IRS Want?
sections.content: Form 4564 included with Letter 6612 typically requests:

list.title: Government orders
list.description: Documentation of federal, state, or local orders that suspended your business operations or significantly impacted your ability to operate.

list.title: Gross receipts records
list.description: Quarterly financial statements showing the decline in gross receipts compared to 2019.

list.title: Payroll records
list.description: Detailed payroll records showing qualified wages paid during eligible quarters.

list.title: PPP documentation
list.description: Records of PPP loans, forgiveness applications, and how you allocated wages between PPP and ERC.

list.title: Business impact evidence
list.description: Documentation showing how government orders affected your specific business operations.

sections.heading: How to Respond to Letter 6612
sections.content: Responding properly to Letter 6612 is critical. Here's the process:

steps.title: Gather your documentation
steps.description: Collect all records related to your ERC eligibility before responding.

steps.title: Review your original claim
steps.description: Understand exactly what was claimed and why you believed you qualified.

steps.title: Prepare written responses
steps.description: Answer each question on Form 4564 thoroughly and accurately.

steps.title: Consider professional help
steps.description: For claims over $50,000, professional representation significantly improves outcomes.

steps.title: Submit before the deadline
steps.description: Send your response via certified mail or fax to document timely submission.

sections.tip: Keep copies of everything you submit and note the date and method of submission. You'll need proof that you responded on time.

sections.heading: What Happens After You Respond?
sections.content: After submitting your response, the IRS examiner will review your documentation. Possible outcomes include:

list.title: Claim approved
list.description: If the examiner is satisfied with your documentation, your claim will be allowed.

list.title: Additional questions
list.description: The examiner may request more documentation or clarification.

list.title: Partial disallowance
list.description: Some quarters or amounts may be denied while others are approved.

list.title: Full disallowance
list.description: If the examiner determines you didn't qualify, you'll receive a disallowance letter with appeal rights.

takeaways: Letter 6612 means your ERC claim is under formal IRS audit
You have exactly 30 days to respond—missing this deadline can result in automatic denial
The letter includes Form 4564 with 13 multi-part documentation requests
Professional representation is strongly recommended for claims over $50,000
Keep copies of everything you submit and document your response date

relatedPages.title: When to Hire an ERC Attorney
What If Your Claim is Denied?

content.ctaText: Received Letter 6612? Don't wait until the deadline. Get expert help preparing your response and protecting your ERC claim.

faqs.question: What is IRS Letter 6612?
faqs.answer: Letter 6612 is the IRS's initial audit notification for ERC claims. It means your claim is under examination and includes Form 4564 requesting documentation to support your eligibility.

faqs.question: How long do I have to respond to Letter 6612?
faqs.answer: You have 30 days from the date of the letter to respond. Missing this deadline can result in full disallowance of your claim without further review.

faqs.question: What documents do I need to provide?
faqs.answer: The letter includes Form 4564 with 13 multi-part questions covering eligibility documentation, payroll records, government orders, gross receipts calculations, and PPP coordination.

faqs.question: Should I respond myself or hire a professional?
faqs.answer: For claims under $25,000, you may be able to respond yourself. For larger claims, professional representation significantly improves your chances of a favorable outcome and ensures proper documentation.

faqs.question: What happens if I don't respond to Letter 6612?
faqs.answer: Failing to respond typically results in full disallowance of your ERC claim. You may also face penalties and interest, and you'll have limited options for appeal.

pages.title: Letter 105-C: Your ERC Claim Was Denied
pages.description: Received Letter 105-C? Your ERC claim has been fully disallowed. Learn your appeal rights and the critical 2-year deadline.
pages.h1: IRS Letter 105-C: Your ERC Claim Was Fully Denied

content.urgency: You have 2 years from this letter to file a lawsuit. This deadline cannot be extended, even by filing an appeal.
content.intro: Letter 105-C is the IRS's official notice that your Employee Retention Credit claim has been completely disallowed. No credit will be paid unless you successfully challenge this decision. While this is a serious setback, it's not the end of the road—you have appeal rights that must be exercised within strict deadlines.

sections.heading: What Letter 105-C Means for Your Business
sections.content: When you receive Letter 105-C, it means:

list.title: Complete denial
list.description: Your entire ERC claim for the quarters specified has been disallowed.

list.title: No refund forthcoming
list.description: You will not receive any ERC refund unless you successfully appeal or litigate.

list.title: Clock is ticking
list.description: Your 2-year statute of limitations for filing a refund suit begins from the date of this letter.

sections.heading: The Critical 2-Year Deadline
sections.content: Unlike most IRS deadlines, the 2-year period to file a lawsuit in federal court is absolute. It cannot be extended by:

list.title: Filing an appeal
list.description: The appeals process does not toll (stop) the 2-year clock.

list.title: Requesting reconsideration
list.description: Audit reconsideration requests don't extend the deadline.

list.title: Correspondence with the IRS
list.description: No amount of back-and-forth communication extends this deadline.

sections.warning: If you received Letter 105-C more than 18 months ago, consult with a tax attorney immediately. You may be running out of time to preserve your rights.

sections.heading: Your Options After Receiving Letter 105-C
sections.content: You have three main paths forward:

steps.title: Request an Appeal
steps.description: Within 30 days, you can request a conference with the IRS Independent Office of Appeals. For claims over $25,000, this requires a formal written protest.

steps.title: File Suit in Federal Court
steps.description: You can file a refund suit in U.S. District Court or the Court of Federal Claims. This preserves your rights but involves litigation costs.

steps.title: Accept the Denial
steps.description: If you don't believe you can successfully challenge the denial, you may choose not to pursue further action.

sections.heading: How to File an Appeal
sections.content: To appeal Letter 105-C, you must:

list.title: Act within 30 days
list.description: Your request for appeal must be submitted within 30 days of the letter date.

list.title: Submit a written protest
list.description: For claims over $25,000, you need a formal written protest explaining why you disagree with the disallowance.

list.title: Include supporting documentation
list.description: Provide any evidence that supports your eligibility that wasn't previously considered.

list.title: State the facts and law
list.description: Your protest should clearly explain the factual basis and legal authority for your position.

sections.tip: Even if you plan to file an appeal, monitor the 2-year deadline closely. You may need to file a protective lawsuit before the appeals process concludes.

sections.heading: Common Reasons for ERC Denial
sections.content: Understanding why your claim was denied helps you decide whether to appeal:

list.title: No qualifying government order
list.description: The IRS may have determined that no government order significantly impacted your operations.

list.title: Insufficient documentation
list.description: You may not have provided adequate evidence of eligibility.

list.title: Essential business exception
list.description: If your business was deemed essential, you may not qualify unless you can prove partial suspension.

list.title: PPP wage overlap
list.description: Wages used for PPP forgiveness cannot also be used for ERC.

list.title: Gross receipts calculation errors
list.description: Your quarterly comparisons may not show the required decline.

takeaways: Letter 105-C means your entire ERC claim has been denied
You have exactly 2 years to file a lawsuit—this deadline is absolute
Filing an appeal does NOT extend the 2-year deadline for litigation
For claims over $25,000, you need a formal written protest to appeal
Consult a tax professional immediately to evaluate your options

relatedPages.title: How to Appeal Your ERC Denial

content.ctaText: Don't let the 2-year deadline pass without action. Get a professional evaluation of your appeal options today.

faqs.question: What does Letter 105-C mean?
faqs.answer: Letter 105-C is the IRS's official notice that your ERC claim has been completely disallowed. No credit will be paid unless you successfully appeal or file a lawsuit.

faqs.question: Can I appeal a Letter 105-C denial?
faqs.answer: Yes. You have 30 days to request an appeal to the IRS Independent Office of Appeals. You also have 2 years to file a refund suit in federal court.

faqs.question: What is the 2-year deadline?
faqs.answer: You must file a lawsuit within 2 years of receiving Letter 105-C, or you lose the right to recover your ERC forever—even if your claim is valid. This deadline is not extended by filing an appeal.

faqs.question: What's the difference between an appeal and a lawsuit?
faqs.answer: An appeal is an informal process within the IRS. A lawsuit is filed in federal court and involves formal litigation. You can pursue both, but the 2-year lawsuit deadline runs regardless of the appeal status.

pages.title: Letter 6577-C: The IRS Wants Your ERC Refund Back
pages.description: Received Letter 6577-C? The IRS is demanding repayment of your ERC. Learn your options and the 21-day response deadline.
pages.h1: IRS Letter 6577-C: ERC Clawback Notice Explained

content.urgency: You have only 21 days to respond—much shorter than other IRS notices. Act immediately.
content.intro: If you've received IRS Letter 6577-C, the IRS is demanding that you repay the Employee Retention Credit you already received. Over 30,000 businesses have received these "clawback" notices, totaling more than $1 billion in demanded repayments. You have just 21 days to respond—and you have options.

sections.heading: What is an ERC Clawback?
sections.content:
A clawback is when the IRS determines that you received an ERC refund you weren't entitled to and demands you pay it back. Letter 6577-C is the official notice initiating this process.

Unlike a denial of an unprocessed claim, a clawback means you already received money and the IRS wants it returned—plus interest from the date you received the refund.

sections.heading: Why Did You Receive This Notice?
sections.content: The IRS issues clawback notices for several reasons:

list.title: Post-payment audit findings
list.description: The IRS reviewed your claim after payment and determined you didn't qualify.

list.title: ERC mill flags
list.description: Claims filed by known problematic promoters are being systematically reviewed.

list.title: Documentation failures
list.description: Your eligibility documentation doesn't support the claim that was paid.

list.title: PPP coordination errors
list.description: Wages were used for both PPP forgiveness and ERC, which isn't allowed.

sections.heading: The 21-Day Deadline
sections.content: Letter 6577-C has one of the shortest response deadlines of any IRS notice—just 21 days. Within this time, you must decide whether to:

list.title: Agree and pay
list.description: Pay the amount demanded (plus interest) to resolve the matter.

list.title: Request a payment plan
list.description: If you can't pay in full, request an installment agreement.

list.title: Dispute the clawback
list.description: Challenge the IRS's determination that you didn't qualify.

list.title: Request an appeal
list.description: Ask for your case to be reviewed by IRS Appeals before collection begins.

sections.warning: If you don't respond within 21 days, the IRS may begin collection actions—including levies on your bank accounts and business assets.

sections.heading: Interest and Penalties
sections.content: When the IRS demands repayment, they also charge:

list.title: Interest
list.description: Interest accrues from the date you received the refund, not from the date of the notice. Current IRS interest rates are significant.

list.title: Accuracy penalties
list.description: A 20% penalty may apply if the IRS determines you were negligent or substantially understated your eligibility.

list.title: Fraud penalties
list.description: In cases of fraud, the penalty increases to 75% of the underpayment.

list.title: OBBBA penalty
list.description: New legislation added an additional 20% erroneous refund claim penalty for ERC.

sections.heading: How to Respond to Letter 6577-C
sections.content: Your response strategy depends on whether you believe the clawback is justified:

steps.title: Review your eligibility
steps.description: Before responding, honestly assess whether you actually qualified for the ERC you received.

steps.title: Gather documentation
steps.description: If you believe you qualified, gather all evidence of government orders, gross receipts decline, and qualified wages.

steps.title: Calculate your exposure
steps.description: Understand the total amount at stake, including interest and potential penalties.

steps.title: Consult a professional
steps.description: Given the short deadline and high stakes, professional guidance is strongly recommended.

steps.title: Submit your response
steps.description: Send your response via certified mail or fax before the deadline.

sections.heading: Can You Fight an ERC Clawback?
sections.content: Yes, but your success depends on the strength of your eligibility case:

list.title: If you legitimately qualified
list.description: With proper documentation, you can challenge the clawback and potentially keep your refund.

list.title: If your claim was questionable
list.description: You may be able to negotiate a settlement for less than the full amount demanded.

list.title: If you didn't qualify
list.description: Your best option may be negotiating a payment plan to minimize immediate financial impact.

sections.tip: Even if you think you may need to repay, responding properly can help you avoid additional penalties and set up a manageable payment arrangement.

takeaways: Letter 6577-C demands repayment of ERC refunds you already received
You have only 21 days to respond—one of the shortest IRS deadlines
Interest accrues from when you received the refund, not from the notice date
You can dispute the clawback if you have documentation proving eligibility
Failing to respond can trigger immediate collection actions
content.ctaText: Received a clawback notice? With only 21 days to respond, every day counts. Get expert help protecting your business.

faqs.question: What is Letter 6577-C?
faqs.answer: Letter 6577-C is an ERC recapture notice. The IRS is demanding you repay the Employee Retention Credit you already received because they've determined you weren't eligible.

faqs.question: How much time do I have to respond?
faqs.answer: Only 21 days from the date of the letter. This is a much shorter deadline than other IRS notices, so immediate action is critical.

faqs.question: Do I have to pay back the full amount?
faqs.answer: Not necessarily. You can dispute the recapture if you have documentation proving eligibility, negotiate a settlement, or set up a payment plan if you can't pay in full.

faqs.question: What happens if I ignore the clawback notice?
faqs.answer: The IRS will assess the amount owed, add penalties and interest, and begin collection actions including levies on your bank accounts and business assets.

pages.title: ERC Claim Denied? Here's What to Do Next
pages.description: Your ERC claim was denied by the IRS. Learn the reasons for denial, your appeal options, and critical deadlines you can't miss.
pages.h1: ERC Claim Denied: Your Complete Guide to Next Steps

content.intro: Having your Employee Retention Credit claim denied is frustrating—especially when you believed you qualified. But a denial isn't necessarily the final word. Understanding why your claim was denied and knowing your options can help you decide the best path forward. Many businesses successfully overturn ERC denials through the appeals process.

sections.heading: Common Reasons for ERC Denial
sections.content: Understanding why your claim was denied is the first step in deciding whether to appeal. The IRS denies ERC claims for several reasons:

list.title: No qualifying government order
list.description: The most common denial reason. The IRS may determine that no federal, state, or local order actually suspended your operations or significantly impacted your business.

list.title: Essential business exception
list.description: If your business was classified as essential, you may not qualify unless you can prove a partial suspension through capacity limits, supply chain disruption, or other documented impacts.

list.title: Insufficient documentation
list.description: You didn't provide adequate evidence to prove eligibility, such as the specific government orders that affected you or how operations were impacted.

list.title: PPP wage overlap
list.description: You used the same wages for PPP loan forgiveness and ERC, which isn't allowed.

list.title: Gross receipts calculation errors
list.description: Your quarterly revenue decline didn't meet the required thresholds when properly calculated.

list.title: Recovery startup business issues
list.description: You claimed as a recovery startup but didn't meet the specific requirements for this category.

sections.heading: Which Denial Letter Did You Receive?
sections.content: The type of denial letter you received affects your options:

list.title: Letter 105-C (Full Disallowance)
list.description: Your entire claim was denied. You have 30 days to appeal and 2 years to file a lawsuit.

list.title: Letter 106-C (Partial Disallowance)
list.description: Some quarters or amounts were denied while others were allowed.

list.title: 3853-C Letter
list.description: A preliminary examination letter requesting your response before formal denial.

sections.heading: Your Appeal Options
sections.content: You have several paths to challenge an ERC denial:

steps.title: IRS Appeals
steps.description: Request a conference with the IRS Independent Office of Appeals within 30 days. This informal process often resolves disputes without litigation.

steps.title: Audit Reconsideration
steps.description: If you have new information or documentation that wasn't previously considered, you can request the IRS reconsider its determination.

steps.title: Federal Court Lawsuit
steps.description: File a refund suit in U.S. District Court or the Court of Federal Claims within 2 years. This is the most formal (and expensive) option.

steps.title: Taxpayer Advocate Service
steps.description: If you're experiencing significant hardship, TAS may be able to help expedite your case.

sections.heading: When Should You Appeal?
sections.content: Not every denial is worth appealing. Consider these factors:

list.title: Strength of your eligibility case
list.description: Do you have solid documentation proving you met the ERC requirements?

list.title: Amount at stake
list.description: Larger claims justify the cost and effort of appeals or litigation.

list.title: Reason for denial
list.description: Some denial reasons are easier to overcome than others.

list.title: New evidence available
list.description: Can you provide documentation you didn't include in your original claim?

list.title: Cost-benefit analysis
list.description: Professional representation costs should be weighed against potential recovery.

sections.tip: If your claim was denied because you couldn't prove a government order impacted your business, but you actually have documentation of that impact, an appeal may be very worthwhile.

sections.heading: Preparing Your Appeal
sections.content: A successful appeal requires thorough preparation:

list.title: Gather all documentation
list.description: Collect government orders, business records, gross receipts statements, payroll records, and any evidence of operational impact.

list.title: Understand the denial reason
list.description: Your appeal should directly address why the IRS said you didn't qualify.

list.title: Present a clear legal argument
list.description: Cite relevant IRS guidance and regulations supporting your position.

list.title: Consider professional help
list.description: For claims over $25,000, professional representation significantly improves outcomes.

takeaways: A denial isn't the final word—many businesses successfully appeal
Understanding the specific reason for denial is crucial for your appeal strategy
You have 30 days to request an appeal and 2 years to file a lawsuit
The strength of your documentation determines your appeal's success
For claims over $25,000, professional representation is strongly recommended
content.ctaText: Don't accept a denial without understanding your options. Get a free review of your case and learn whether an appeal makes sense.

faqs.question: Why was my ERC claim denied?
faqs.answer: Common reasons include: claiming as an essential business without proof of suspension, insufficient documentation, calculation errors, PPP wage overlap, or filing through an ERC mill that used aggressive positions.

faqs.question: Can I appeal an ERC denial?
faqs.answer: Yes. You can request an appeal to the IRS Independent Office of Appeals within 30 days, or file a refund suit in federal court within 2 years of your denial letter.

faqs.question: Should I hire a professional?
faqs.answer: For claims over $25,000, professional representation significantly improves your chances. The IRS requires a formal written protest for larger claims, which involves legal and tax expertise.

faqs.question: What's the success rate for ERC appeals?
faqs.answer: Success depends heavily on the specific facts and documentation. Claims with strong evidence of eligibility that weren't properly considered initially have good prospects for reversal.

pages.title: How to Appeal an ERC Denial: Step-by-Step Guide
pages.description: Step-by-step guide to appealing your ERC denial. Learn about formal protests, IRS Appeals, and your federal court options.
pages.h1: How to Appeal Your ERC Denial

content.intro: If your ERC claim was denied, you have the right to appeal. The appeals process gives you a chance to present your case to an independent IRS officer who wasn't involved in the original decision. This guide walks you through exactly how to file an appeal, what deadlines apply, and how to give yourself the best chance of success.

sections.heading: The 30-Day Appeal Deadline
sections.content: From the date of your denial letter, you have 30 days to request an appeal to the IRS Independent Office of Appeals. This deadline is firm—if you miss it, your only option is federal court litigation.
sections.warning: Count your days carefully. The 30-day period starts from the date on the letter, not when you received it. If you're close to the deadline, submit your appeal immediately, even if incomplete.

sections.heading: Types of Appeals
sections.content: The type of appeal you file depends on the amount of your claim:

list.title: Small case request (under $25,000)
list.description: You can submit a brief written statement explaining why you disagree with the denial. No formal format is required.

list.title: Formal written protest ($25,000 or more)
list.description: You must submit a detailed written protest following IRS requirements. This is essentially a legal brief arguing your case.

sections.heading: How to File a Formal Written Protest
sections.content: For claims of $25,000 or more, your protest must include:

steps.title: Your identifying information
steps.description: Name, address, EIN, and a daytime phone number.

steps.title: Statement of intent to appeal
steps.description: A clear statement that you want to appeal the denial to the Office of Appeals.

steps.title: Copy of the denial letter
steps.description: Attach the letter you received from the IRS.

steps.title: Tax periods at issue
steps.description: Identify the specific quarters for which you're appealing.

steps.title: Statement of facts
steps.description: A complete description of the facts supporting your eligibility.

steps.title: Statement of law
steps.description: Cite the relevant statutes, regulations, and IRS guidance that support your position.

steps.title: Arguments
steps.description: Explain specifically why you believe you qualified for the ERC.

steps.title: Signature under penalties of perjury
steps.description: Sign the protest declaring that the facts are true and correct.

sections.heading: What Happens in IRS Appeals?
sections.content: After you file your protest, here's what to expect:

list.title: Case assignment
list.description: Your case will be assigned to an Appeals Officer who will review your file.

list.title: Appeals conference
list.description: You'll have a conference (usually by phone) to discuss your case with the Appeals Officer.

list.title: Document submission
list.description: You may have the opportunity to submit additional documentation.

list.title: Decision
list.description: The Appeals Officer will issue a decision sustaining, modifying, or reversing the denial.

sections.tip: The Appeals process is meant to be less adversarial than an audit. Appeals Officers have authority to settle cases and often look for reasonable resolutions.

sections.heading: Timeline for Appeals
sections.content: The ERC appeals process typically takes:

list.title: Initial assignment
list.description: 1-3 months for your case to be assigned after you file.

list.title: Conference scheduling
list.description: 2-4 months to schedule an Appeals conference.

list.title: Decision
list.description: 1-3 months after the conference for a final decision.

list.title: Total time
list.description: Typically 5-10 months from filing to resolution.

sections.warning: Remember: The 2-year deadline for filing a federal court lawsuit is NOT extended by the appeals process. Monitor this deadline carefully while your appeal is pending.

sections.heading: What If Appeals Denies Your Case?
sections.content: If the Appeals Officer upholds the denial, you still have options:

list.title: File in federal court
list.description: You can file a refund suit in U.S. District Court or the Court of Federal Claims (if within 2 years).

list.title: Request reconsideration
list.description: In limited circumstances, you may be able to request Appeals reconsider.

list.title: Accept the decision
list.description: If you've exhausted your options or lack strong grounds, accepting may be the practical choice.

takeaways: You have 30 days from your denial letter to request an appeal
Claims over $25,000 require a formal written protest
IRS Appeals is independent from the examination division that denied you
The appeals process typically takes 5-10 months
The 2-year litigation deadline continues to run during appeals
content.ctaText: Need help with your ERC appeal? Our team has extensive experience navigating IRS Appeals and can help you build the strongest possible case.

faqs.question: What is the appeal deadline?
faqs.answer: You have 30 days from your denial letter to request an appeal to IRS Appeals. The 2-year deadline for filing in federal court is not extended by the appeals process.

faqs.question: What is a formal written protest?
faqs.answer: For claims over $25,000, you must submit a detailed written protest including your eligibility arguments, supporting documentation, legal authority for your position, and a signature under penalties of perjury.

faqs.question: How long does the appeals process take?
faqs.answer: Typically 5-10 months from filing to decision. This includes time for case assignment, scheduling a conference with an Appeals Officer, and receiving a final decision.

faqs.question: Do I need a professional for my appeal?
faqs.answer: For claims over $25,000, professional representation is strongly recommended. The formal protest requires legal analysis and proper presentation of facts and law.

pages.title: Why Is My ERC Refund Taking So Long?
pages.description: 597,000+ ERC claims are still unprocessed. Learn why your refund is delayed, current timelines, and options to expedite.
pages.h1: Why Is My ERC Refund Taking So Long?

content.intro: If you're waiting for your ERC refund, you're not alone. As of 2026, the IRS still has over 597,000 Employee Retention Credit claims in its processing queue. Average wait times exceed 548 days, and many businesses have been waiting much longer. Here's what's causing the delay and what options you have.

sections.heading: The ERC Processing Backlog
sections.content: The IRS is dealing with an unprecedented backlog of ERC claims:

list.title: 597,000+ claims pending
list.description: Hundreds of thousands of businesses are still waiting for their ERC refunds.

list.title: Average wait: 548+ days
list.description: Most claims are taking well over a year to process, many much longer.

list.title: Moratorium effects
list.description: The September 2023 moratorium on new claim processing compounded the backlog.

list.title: Enhanced scrutiny
list.description: Every claim now receives individual review, slowing the process further.

sections.heading: Why the IRS Slowed ERC Processing
sections.content: In September 2023, the IRS announced a moratorium on processing new ERC claims. Here's why:

list.title: Fraud concerns
list.description: The IRS identified widespread fraud and improper claims, particularly from "ERC mills" that aggressively marketed to ineligible businesses.

list.title: Enhanced review process
list.description: The IRS implemented new procedures to individually review claims instead of batch processing.

list.title: Criminal investigations
list.description: Over 545 criminal investigations were opened, covering billions in suspected fraud.

list.title: Political pressure
list.description: Congressional scrutiny of improper payments led to increased caution.

sections.heading: How to Check Your ERC Refund Status
sections.content: Unfortunately, the IRS doesn't have an online tool to check ERC refund status. Your options:

list.title: Call the IRS
list.description: Contact the IRS Business & Specialty Tax Line at (800) 829-4933. Be prepared for long hold times.

list.title: Check your transcripts
list.description: Request your business tax transcripts to see if any action has been taken on your claim.

list.title: Authorized representative
list.description: A tax professional with Power of Attorney can access your account information more easily.

list.title: IRS.gov account
list.description: Some information may be available through your business's IRS online account, if you've set one up.

sections.tip: When calling the IRS, call early in the morning (8am ET) or late in the afternoon to reduce hold times. Have your EIN and the quarters you claimed ready.

sections.heading: Can You Expedite Your ERC Refund?
sections.content: While you can't directly force the IRS to process your claim faster, there are some options:

list.title: Taxpayer Advocate Service
list.description: If you're experiencing significant financial hardship, TAS may be able to intervene on your behalf.

list.title: Refund lawsuit
list.description: If it's been more than 6 months since filing and you've received no refund, you can file suit in federal court.

list.title: Congressional inquiry
list.description: Contacting your Representative or Senator may help draw attention to your case.

list.title: Professional assistance
list.description: An enrolled agent or CPA with IRS relationships may be able to get updates more effectively.

sections.heading: When to Worry About Your Pending Claim
sections.content: Some pending claims face additional scrutiny. Watch for these signs:

list.title: IRS correspondence
list.description: If you've received letters requesting information, respond promptly—delays here extend your wait.

list.title: Transcript shows holds
list.description: Certain codes on your transcript indicate your claim is under review.

list.title: No movement
list.description: If you filed before the moratorium and have heard nothing, consider reaching out.

list.title: Promoter concerns
list.description: If you used an ERC mill, your claim may be in a higher-scrutiny queue.

sections.warning: If you've received a letter from the IRS about your ERC claim, respond immediately. Delays in responding can result in denial of your claim.

takeaways: Over 597,000 ERC claims remain unprocessed with average waits exceeding 548 days
The September 2023 moratorium and enhanced scrutiny caused massive delays
There's no online tool to check ERC status—you must call the IRS
Taxpayer Advocate Service may help if you're experiencing financial hardship
Respond immediately to any IRS correspondence about your claim
content.ctaText: Concerned about your pending ERC claim? We can help you check your status, respond to IRS inquiries, and protect your refund.

faqs.question: How many ERC claims are still pending?
faqs.answer: As of 2026, over 597,000 ERC claims remain unprocessed. The average wait time exceeds 548 days.

faqs.question: Can I expedite my ERC refund?
faqs.answer: Options include contacting the Taxpayer Advocate Service (if you're experiencing hardship), filing a lawsuit after 6 months of no action, or having a professional representative follow up on your behalf.

faqs.question: Why is the IRS taking so long?
faqs.answer: The IRS implemented a moratorium on processing new claims in September 2023 due to fraud concerns. They're now reviewing claims individually with enhanced scrutiny, which takes significantly longer than previous batch processing.

faqs.question: How do I check my ERC refund status?
faqs.answer: There's no online tool for ERC status. You must call the IRS Business & Specialty Tax Line at (800) 829-4933, check your business tax transcripts, or have an authorized representative access your account.

pages.title: My ERC Company Went Out of Business - What Now?
pages.description: Your ERC preparer disappeared? Learn how to get your documentation, find your claim status, and protect yourself during an audit.
pages.h1: My ERC Company Went Out of Business - What Do I Do?

content.intro: You filed an ERC claim through a company that promised expert service, and now they're gone—phone disconnected, website down, emails bouncing. Unfortunately, this scenario is increasingly common as many ERC mills have closed their doors, leaving clients without documentation, support, or answers. Here's what you need to know and do.

sections.heading: You're Still Responsible for Your Claim
sections.content:
This is the most important thing to understand: You, the business owner, are legally responsible for the accuracy of your ERC claim—not the preparer. The IRS holds you accountable regardless of who filed the paperwork.

This means if the IRS audits or denies your claim, you're the one who must respond, defend your eligibility, and face any consequences.
sections.warning: Even if your preparer made false claims or errors, you may still face penalties. The IRS can pursue you for repayment, interest, and penalties.

sections.heading: Immediate Steps to Take
sections.content: If your ERC preparer has disappeared, take these steps right away:

steps.title: Check your claim status
steps.description: Call the IRS at (800) 829-4933 to see if your claim is pending, processed, or under review.

steps.title: Request your IRS transcripts
steps.description: Get copies of your business tax transcripts to see what was actually filed in your name.

steps.title: Gather what documentation you have
steps.description: Collect any paperwork, emails, or contracts from the ERC company.

steps.title: Contact your payroll provider
steps.description: Request copies of the Forms 941-X that were filed on your behalf.

steps.title: Review your bank records
steps.description: Check if you received any ERC refund payments and when.

steps.title: Assess your eligibility
steps.description: Honestly evaluate whether you actually qualified for the ERC based on the real criteria.

sections.heading: Reconstructing Your Documentation
sections.content: If the preparer took your documentation with them, you'll need to reconstruct it:

list.title: Government orders
list.description: Research the federal, state, and local orders that were in effect during 2020-2021. These are public record.

list.title: Gross receipts
list.description: Pull your accounting records to calculate quarterly revenue for 2019-2021.

list.title: Payroll records
list.description: Your payroll provider should have complete records of wages paid.

list.title: PPP documentation
list.description: Contact your bank for PPP loan and forgiveness records.

list.title: Business impact evidence
list.description: Gather any records showing how your business was affected by COVID-19 restrictions.

sections.tip: Even without the preparer's files, you can reconstruct most eligibility documentation from your own business records and public sources.

sections.heading: Should You Withdraw Your Claim?
sections.content: If your claim is still pending and you're unsure about your eligibility, you may want to consider withdrawing:

list.title: When to withdraw
list.description: If you realize you didn't actually qualify or your preparer made false claims on your behalf, withdrawing avoids penalties.

list.title: Withdrawal benefits
list.description: No penalties, no interest, the claim is treated as if it was never filed.

list.title: When not to withdraw
list.description: If you legitimately qualified and have documentation, don't withdraw a valid claim out of fear.

sections.heading: Finding New Representation
sections.content: A new tax professional can help you navigate this situation:

list.title: ERC expertise
list.description: Look for someone who understands ERC eligibility rules and IRS audit procedures.

list.title: IRS representation
list.description: Choose a CPA, Enrolled Agent, or tax attorney authorized to represent you before the IRS.

list.title: Case assessment
list.description: A qualified professional can review your claim and tell you honestly if it was valid.

list.title: Documentation help
list.description: They can help reconstruct files and prepare responses to IRS inquiries.

takeaways: You're legally responsible for your ERC claim, even if the preparer disappeared
Check your claim status and get IRS transcripts immediately
Your documentation can be reconstructed from your own records
Consider withdrawing if you realize you didn't actually qualify
A new qualified representative can help assess and defend your claim

relatedPages.title: If You Receive an Audit Notice

content.ctaText: Left without support by your ERC preparer? We can help you understand your situation, assess your claim, and prepare for any IRS action.

faqs.question: Am I still responsible for my ERC claim?
faqs.answer: Yes. You are legally responsible for the accuracy of your ERC claim regardless of who prepared it. The IRS holds the taxpayer accountable, not the preparer.

faqs.question: How do I get my ERC documentation?
faqs.answer: Request copies from your payroll provider, accountant, or reconstruct from your business records. You can also request your IRS transcript showing what was filed in your name.

faqs.question: Can a new representative help me?
faqs.answer: Yes. A new tax professional can step in as your representative, reconstruct your eligibility documentation, and defend your claim if audited.

faqs.question: Should I withdraw my claim?
faqs.answer: If your claim is still pending and you realize you didn't qualify, withdrawing avoids penalties. But don't withdraw a valid claim just because your preparer disappeared.

pages.h1: When to Hire an ERC Audit Attorney

content.intro: Not every ERC issue requires professional representation—but many do. When the stakes are high, deadlines are tight, and the IRS is demanding answers, having an experienced professional on your side can make the difference between keeping your refund and losing it all. Here's how to know when it's time to get help.

sections.heading: When Professional Help is Essential
sections.content: You should strongly consider hiring a tax professional when:

list.title: Your claim exceeds $50,000
list.description: Larger claims warrant professional defense. The cost of representation is often far less than the potential loss.

list.title: You received a disallowance letter
list.description: Letters 105-C or 106-C trigger strict deadlines and complex appeal procedures.

list.title: The IRS alleges fraud
list.description: Any suggestion of fraud requires immediate professional representation to protect your rights.

list.title: You received a clawback notice

list.title: Criminal referral concerns

list.title: Your preparer disappeared

sections.heading: Who Can Represent You?
sections.content: Three types of professionals are authorized to represent you before the IRS:

list.title: Enrolled Agents (EAs)
list.description: Federally licensed tax practitioners who specialize in tax matters and can represent you in all IRS proceedings.

list.title: Certified Public Accountants (CPAs)
list.description: Licensed accountants who can provide comprehensive tax services including IRS representation.

list.title: Tax Attorneys
list.description: Lawyers specializing in tax law who can represent you in IRS matters and federal court litigation.

sections.tip: For most ERC audit matters, an Enrolled Agent or CPA with ERC experience is sufficient. You need an attorney if litigation is likely or criminal issues arise.

sections.heading: What an ERC Professional Does
sections.content: A qualified ERC representative provides:

list.title: IRS communication handling
list.description: All contact with the IRS goes through your representative, reducing stress and mistakes.

list.title: Response preparation
list.description: Professional drafting of audit responses and formal protests.

list.title: Documentation review
list.description: Assessment of your eligibility evidence and identification of gaps.

list.title: Appeals representation
list.description: Advocacy during IRS Appeals conferences.

list.title: Litigation support
list.description: If you need to go to federal court, an attorney can handle the lawsuit.

list.title: Settlement negotiation
list.description: If full defense isn't possible, they can negotiate the best possible outcome.

sections.heading: When You Might Not Need Help
sections.content: Professional representation may not be necessary when:

list.title: Small claims (under $25,000)
list.description: For smaller amounts, you may be able to handle a simple appeal yourself.

list.title: Clear-cut eligibility
list.description: If your documentation is solid and straightforward, you may be able to respond without help.

list.title: Pre-filing questions
list.description: For basic eligibility questions before filing, general guidance may suffice.

sections.heading: Cost of ERC Representation
sections.content: Understanding representation costs helps you make an informed decision:

list.title: Initial consultation
list.description: Many professionals offer free initial consultations to assess your case.

list.title: Flat fee services
list.description: Some offer flat fees for specific services like responding to audits.

list.title: Hourly rates
list.description: For complex matters, hourly billing allows flexibility as the case develops.

list.title: Contingency arrangements
list.description: Some may offer contingency or hybrid fee structures for strong cases.

list.title: Cost vs. risk
list.description: Professional fees are typically far less than the penalties and interest at stake.

sections.tip: Ask about fee structures upfront. A good professional will be transparent about costs and help you understand the value of representation for your specific situation.

takeaways: Professional help is essential for claims over $50,000 or when fraud is alleged
Enrolled Agents, CPAs, and tax attorneys can all represent you before the IRS
For litigation or criminal matters, you specifically need a tax attorney
The cost of representation is usually far less than potential penalties
Free consultations let you assess your options without commitment
content.ctaText: Not sure if you need professional help? Our free consultation will assess your situation and explain your options—with no obligation.

faqs.question: Do I need an attorney for an ERC audit?
faqs.answer: If your claim exceeds $50,000, you received a disallowance letter, or the IRS is alleging fraud, professional representation is strongly recommended. An attorney is specifically needed for litigation or criminal matters.

faqs.question: What does an ERC attorney do?
faqs.answer: They handle all IRS communications, prepare your formal response, represent you in appeals, negotiate settlements, and if necessary, litigate your case in federal court.

faqs.question: How much does ERC audit defense cost?
faqs.answer: Costs vary based on complexity, but professional defense is typically far less expensive than the penalties and interest on a disallowed claim. Many professionals offer free initial consultations.

faqs.question: What's the difference between an EA, CPA, and attorney?
faqs.answer: All three can represent you before the IRS. Enrolled Agents specialize in tax matters, CPAs are licensed accountants, and attorneys can also handle court litigation and criminal matters.

pages.title: IRS ERC Clawback: What to Do If They Want Your Refund Back
pages.description: The IRS is recapturing $1B+ in ERC refunds. Learn what a clawback means, your rights, and how to fight back.
pages.h1: IRS ERC Clawback: They Want Your Refund Back

content.intro: The IRS is aggressively pursuing recapture of ERC refunds it believes were improperly paid. Over 30,000 businesses have received clawback notices demanding repayment of more than $1 billion combined. If you've received a demand to return your ERC refund, you have options—but you need to act quickly.

sections.heading: What is an ERC Clawback?
sections.content:
An ERC clawback occurs when the IRS determines that you received an Employee Retention Credit you weren't entitled to and demands repayment. This is different from a denial of an unprocessed claim—a clawback means you already received money and the IRS wants it back.

The official clawback notice is Letter 6577-C, which gives you only 21 days to respond.

sections.heading: Why the IRS is Clawing Back ERC Refunds
sections.content: The IRS is targeting ERC refunds for several reasons:

list.title: Post-payment audits
list.description: The IRS is now reviewing claims that were paid during the initial processing surge before enhanced scrutiny began.

list.title: ERC mill targeting
list.description: Claims filed by known aggressive promoters are being systematically reviewed and challenged.

list.title: Information matching
list.description: IRS systems are identifying claims that don't match other reported business information.

list.title: Fraud indicators
list.description: Claims showing signs of fraud or improper eligibility are prioritized for clawback.

sections.heading: Can You Fight a Clawback?
sections.content: Yes, you can dispute an ERC clawback if you believe your claim was valid. Your options include:

list.title: Challenge the determination
list.description: If you have documentation proving eligibility, you can contest the clawback directly.

list.title: Request Appeals consideration
list.description: Ask for your case to be reviewed by IRS Appeals before collection begins.

list.title: Negotiate a settlement
list.description: In some cases, you may be able to settle for less than the full amount demanded.

list.title: Request a payment plan
list.description: If you owe the money but can't pay in full, an installment agreement may be possible.

sections.tip: The strength of your defense depends on your documentation. If you can prove you legitimately qualified based on government orders or gross receipts decline, you have a strong case.

sections.heading: What Happens If You Don't Respond
sections.content: Ignoring a clawback notice has serious consequences:

list.title: Automatic assessment
list.description: The IRS will assess the full amount owed without further review.

list.title: Interest accrual
list.description: Interest continues to compound from the date you received the original refund.

list.title: Collection actions
list.description: The IRS can levy your bank accounts, garnish wages, and file tax liens.

list.title: Business impact
list.description: Collection activities can severely impact your business operations and credit.

sections.warning: Even if you think you might owe the money back, responding properly can help you avoid additional penalties and set up a manageable payment arrangement.

sections.heading: Financial Impact of Clawbacks
sections.content: Understanding what you may owe helps you plan:

list.title: Principal amount
list.description: The original ERC refund you received.

list.title: Interest
list.description: Interest from the date of the original refund (not the clawback notice date).

list.title: Accuracy penalty
list.description: 20% penalty if the IRS determines you were negligent or substantially understated eligibility.

list.title: OBBBA penalty
list.description: Additional 20% erroneous claim penalty under new legislation.

list.title: Fraud penalty
list.description: 75% penalty if fraud is determined (replaces accuracy penalty).

takeaways: The IRS is demanding repayment of over $1 billion in ERC refunds from 30,000+ businesses
Letter 6577-C gives you only 21 days to respond
You can fight a clawback if you have documentation proving eligibility
Ignoring the notice can lead to immediate collection actions
Interest accrues from the date you received the original refund
content.ctaText: Received a clawback notice? Every day counts with a 21-day deadline. Get expert help defending your ERC refund immediately.

faqs.question: What is an ERC clawback?

faqs.answer: Yes. You can dispute the recapture if your claim was valid, negotiate a settlement, request Appeals review, or set up a payment plan if you can't pay in full.

faqs.question: What happens if I ignore the clawback notice?

faqs.answer: Potentially the original refund amount plus interest (from when you received it), 20% accuracy penalty, 20% OBBBA penalty, and in fraud cases, a 75% fraud penalty.

pages.title: IRS Form 4564: How to Respond to an ERC Document Request
pages.description: Received Form 4564 with your ERC audit notice? Learn what documents the IRS wants and how to respond properly.
pages.h1: IRS Form 4564: Responding to an ERC Information Document Request

content.intro: Form 4564, also known as an Information Document Request (IDR), is how the IRS formally asks for documentation during an ERC audit. If you've received this form, your response will largely determine the outcome of your audit. Here's what the IRS is asking for and how to respond effectively.

sections.heading: What is Form 4564?
sections.content:
Form 4564 is the IRS's standard form for requesting specific documents and information during an audit. For ERC claims, the Form 4564 typically accompanies Letter 6612 and contains detailed questions about your eligibility.

The form itself is short, but the questions often require extensive documentation to answer properly.

sections.heading: Common Questions on ERC Form 4564
sections.content: The ERC version of Form 4564 typically includes 13 multi-part questions covering:

list.title: Government orders
list.description: Which federal, state, or local government orders affected your business? Provide copies and explain the impact.

list.title: Operational impact
list.description: How did these orders suspend or limit your business operations? Provide specific examples.

list.title: Essential business status
list.description: Were you classified as an essential business? If so, explain how operations were still affected.

list.title: Gross receipts decline
list.description: Provide quarterly gross receipts for 2019-2021 and calculations showing the required decline.

list.title: Qualified wages
list.description: Provide payroll records showing wages paid during eligible quarters.

list.title: Employee counts
list.description: How many employees did you have? This affects whether you can claim all wages or only wages during non-working periods.

list.title: PPP coordination
list.description: Did you receive PPP loans? How did you allocate wages between PPP and ERC?

list.title: Related employers
list.description: Are you part of a controlled group or affiliated service group?

list.title: Recovery startup status
list.description: If claiming as a recovery startup business, provide formation documents and gross receipts history.

sections.heading: How to Respond to Form 4564
sections.content: A strong response to Form 4564 requires careful preparation:

steps.title: Read each question carefully
steps.description: Make sure you understand exactly what's being asked before gathering documents.

steps.title: Answer every question
steps.description: Don't skip questions—explain if something doesn't apply or if you don't have certain documentation.

steps.title: Organize your response
steps.description: Number your exhibits to match the questions and provide a clear index.

steps.title: Include a cover letter
steps.description: Summarize your eligibility position and guide the examiner through your response.

steps.title: Keep copies of everything
steps.description: Document what you sent and when you sent it.

steps.title: Meet the deadline
steps.description: Send your response before the due date (typically 30 days from Letter 6612).

sections.heading: What If You Don't Have All the Documents?
sections.content: Missing documentation is a common challenge. Here's how to handle it:

list.title: Reconstruct what you can
list.description: Many documents can be recreated from your business records, payroll provider, or public sources.

list.title: Explain gaps
list.description: If you can't provide something, explain why and offer alternative evidence.

list.title: Don't fabricate documents
list.description: Submitting false documents is a crime. It's better to explain gaps than create false evidence.

list.title: Request an extension
list.description: If you need more time, ask. Extensions are sometimes granted.

sections.warning: Failing to respond to Form 4564 at all typically results in full denial of your ERC claim.

takeaways: Form 4564 is the IRS's formal document request during an ERC audit
It typically contains 13 multi-part questions about your eligibility
Your response significantly impacts whether your claim is approved or denied
Answer every question, even if just to explain why something doesn't apply
Meet the deadline—late responses can result in automatic denial
content.ctaText: Need help responding to Form 4564? Our team can help you prepare a thorough, professional response that protects your ERC claim.

faqs.question: What is Form 4564?
faqs.answer: Form 4564 is an Information Document Request (IDR) the IRS uses to request specific documents during an audit. For ERC claims, it typically includes 13 multi-part questions about your eligibility.

faqs.question: What documents does the IRS request?
faqs.answer: Government orders affecting your business, gross receipts calculations, payroll records, PPP documentation, and evidence of how operations were impacted during COVID-19.

faqs.question: What if I don't have all the documents?
faqs.answer: Provide what you have and explain any gaps. Work with a professional to reconstruct missing documentation from your own records where possible. Don't fabricate documents.

faqs.question: How long do I have to respond?
faqs.answer: The deadline is typically 30 days from the date of Letter 6612 (which usually accompanies Form 4564). Extensions may be available if you need more time.

pages.title: ERC Audit Penalties: What's at Stake
pages.description: ERC audit penalties range from 20% to 75% plus interest. Learn what penalties apply and how to minimize your exposure.
pages.h1: ERC Audit Penalties: Understanding What's at Stake

content.intro: If your ERC claim is disallowed, the financial impact goes far beyond just losing the credit. The IRS can impose penalties ranging from 20% to 75% of the disallowed amount, plus interest that's been accruing since you received the refund. Understanding these penalties helps you assess your risk and make informed decisions about how to proceed.

sections.heading: Types of ERC Penalties
sections.content: The IRS can impose several different penalties on disallowed ERC claims, depending on the circumstances:

list.title: Accuracy-Related Penalty (20%)
list.description: The most common penalty. Applies if you were negligent, disregarded rules, or substantially understated your eligibility. This is 20% of the disallowed credit amount.

list.title: Erroneous Refund Claim Penalty (20%)
list.description: Added by the One Big Beautiful Bill Act (OBBBA). This additional 20% penalty applies specifically to erroneous ERC refund claims filed after January 31, 2024.

list.title: Civil Fraud Penalty (75%)
list.description: If the IRS determines your claim was fraudulent, the penalty jumps to 75% of the underpayment. This replaces (doesn't stack with) the accuracy penalty.

list.title: Failure to Pay Penalty (0.5%/month)
list.description: If you received a refund and don't repay promptly, you'll owe an additional 0.5% per month, up to 25% maximum.

sections.heading: Interest on ERC Disallowances
sections.content: Interest is often the most overlooked cost of an ERC disallowance:

list.title: Interest starts from refund date
list.description: Interest accrues from the date you received the ERC refund, not from the disallowance date. For early filers, this could be 3+ years of interest.

list.title: Current IRS interest rate
list.description: The IRS interest rate is currently 8% annually, compounded daily. This adds up quickly on large claims.

list.title: Interest on penalties
list.description: You also owe interest on any penalties assessed, compounding the total amount due.

sections.warning: On a $500,000 ERC claim received in 2021, interest alone could exceed $150,000 by 2026—before any penalties are added.

sections.heading: Criminal Penalties for ERC Fraud
sections.content: In serious cases, the IRS pursues criminal prosecution:

list.title: 545+ criminal investigations
list.description: The IRS has opened over 545 criminal investigations involving $5.6 billion in potentially fraudulent ERC claims.

list.title: Average prison sentence
list.description: Convicted ERC fraud defendants are receiving an average of 24 months in federal prison.

list.title: Additional criminal fines
list.description: Criminal conviction can include fines up to $250,000 for individuals or $500,000 for corporations, plus restitution.

list.title: Who's at risk
list.description: Criminal prosecution typically targets promoters, preparers, and business owners who knowingly filed false claims or created fraudulent documentation.

sections.heading: How to Minimize Penalties
sections.content: Several strategies can help reduce or eliminate penalties:

list.title: Reasonable cause defense
list.description: If you can show you acted in good faith and had reasonable cause for your position, accuracy penalties may be waived.

list.title: Reliance on professional advice
list.description: Documented reliance on a qualified tax professional's advice can support a reasonable cause argument.

list.title: Voluntary correction
list.description: Coming forward before the IRS contacts you may result in reduced penalties.

list.title: First-time penalty abatement
list.description: If you have a clean compliance history, you may qualify for first-time penalty abatement.

list.title: Withdrawal of pending claims
list.description: For unprocessed claims, withdrawal avoids all penalties entirely.

sections.tip: Document everything. If you relied on a CPA's advice that you qualified, keep records of that advice. Good faith reliance on professional guidance is your strongest penalty defense.

sections.heading: Calculating Your Total Exposure
sections.content: Here's how to estimate your potential liability on a disallowed claim:

list.title: Principal
list.description: The full ERC amount that was disallowed.

list.title: Plus accuracy penalty
list.description: Add 20% of the disallowed amount.

list.title: Plus OBBBA penalty (if applicable)
list.description: Add another 20% if your claim was filed after January 31, 2024.

list.title: Plus interest
list.description: Calculate interest at ~8% annually from the date you received the refund.

list.title: Fraud multiplier
list.description: If fraud is determined, replace the 20% accuracy penalty with 75%.

takeaways: Accuracy-related penalty is 20% of the disallowed credit amount
Fraud penalty jumps to 75%—and can include criminal prosecution
Interest accrues from when you received the refund, not from disallowance
Reasonable cause and reliance on professional advice can eliminate penalties
Withdrawing an unprocessed claim avoids all penalties
content.ctaText: Concerned about penalties on your ERC claim? Get a confidential assessment of your exposure and options for minimizing your liability.

faqs.question: What penalties apply to ERC audits?
faqs.answer: The 20% accuracy-related penalty is most common. Fraud cases face a 75% civil fraud penalty. The OBBBA legislation added an additional 20% erroneous claim penalty for claims filed after January 31, 2024.

faqs.question: Can penalties be waived?
faqs.answer: Yes, with reasonable cause. If you relied on professional advice and had a good faith belief in your eligibility, penalties may be abated. First-time penalty abatement may also be available.

faqs.question: Is criminal prosecution possible?
faqs.answer: Yes. The IRS has opened 545+ criminal investigations covering $5.6 billion in suspected ERC fraud. The average sentence for convicted defendants is 24 months in federal prison.

faqs.question: When does interest start accruing?
faqs.answer: Interest accrues from the date you received the ERC refund, not from when the IRS disallows your claim. This can mean years of accumulated interest on early claims.

pages.title: ERC Mills: How to Know If You Worked With One
pages.description: ERC mills filed billions in fraudulent claims. Learn the warning signs that you may have worked with one.
pages.h1: ERC Mills: Warning Signs You Worked With a Bad Filer

content.intro: During the ERC boom, thousands of companies popped up promising easy refunds with minimal documentation. These 'ERC mills' filed billions of dollars in questionable claims—and many have now disappeared, leaving their clients to face IRS audits alone. If you're wondering whether your ERC preparer was legitimate, here are the warning signs to watch for.

sections.heading: What is an ERC Mill?
sections.content:
An ERC mill is a company that aggressively marketed Employee Retention Credit services using high-pressure tactics and questionable eligibility determinations. These operations prioritized volume over accuracy, filing as many claims as possible with minimal due diligence.

The IRS has identified ERC mills as a major source of improper claims, and claims filed by known mills are being targeted for systematic review.

sections.heading: Warning Signs You Used an ERC Mill
sections.content: Review your experience with your ERC preparer. The more of these signs you recognize, the higher your risk:

list.title: Unsolicited contact
list.description: They reached out to you through cold calls, texts, emails, or social media ads promising guaranteed refunds.

list.title: Claimed everyone qualifies
list.description: They said virtually every business qualifies for ERC without reviewing your specific situation.

list.title: Contingency fees
list.description: They charged a percentage of your refund (often 15-30%) rather than a flat fee for professional services.

list.title: No documentation review
list.description: They didn't ask for or review government orders affecting your business, gross receipts records, or payroll data.

list.title: Quick turnaround promises
list.description: They promised fast refunds without thorough analysis of your eligibility.

list.title: No CPA or attorney involved
list.description: The company had no licensed tax professionals involved in evaluating claims.

list.title: Aggressive revenue decline claims
list.description: They claimed you qualified based on gross receipts decline without proper calculations.

list.title: Vague supply chain arguments
list.description: They used generic 'supply chain disruption' reasoning without specific documentation.

sections.heading: Red Flags in Your Claim
sections.content: Look at the actual claim that was filed on your behalf:

list.title: Claimed all eligible quarters
list.description: Your claim covers every possible quarter (Q2 2020 through Q3 2021) without clear justification for each.

list.title: Maximum wage amounts
list.description: The claim uses the maximum $10,000 per employee per quarter without supporting payroll documentation.

list.title: Generic eligibility language
list.description: The supporting documentation uses boilerplate language not specific to your business.

list.title: No copies provided
list.description: You never received copies of the Forms 941-X filed on your behalf or the supporting documentation.

list.title: Can't reach the company
list.description: The preparer's phone is disconnected, website is down, or emails bounce.

sections.warning: If your preparer has disappeared and you can't get copies of what was filed, you're in a high-risk situation. The IRS holds YOU responsible for your claim, regardless of who prepared it.

sections.heading: What to Do If You Used an ERC Mill
sections.content: If you recognize these warning signs, take action now:

steps.title: Get your IRS transcripts
steps.description: Request your business tax transcripts to see exactly what was filed in your name.

steps.title: Retrieve your documentation
steps.description: Contact your payroll provider for copies of Forms 941-X. Gather any paperwork you still have from the preparer.

steps.title: Assess your actual eligibility
steps.description: Work with a qualified professional to determine if you legitimately qualified for the ERC.

steps.title: Consider withdrawal
steps.description: If your claim is still unprocessed and you don't think you qualified, withdrawal may be your best option.

steps.title: Prepare for audit
steps.description: If you've already received the refund, prepare your defense documentation now—before the IRS contacts you.

sections.heading: IRS Actions Against ERC Mills
sections.content: The IRS is aggressively pursuing ERC mills and their clients:

list.title: Criminal investigations
list.description: Over 545 criminal investigations have been opened, with many targeting mill operators.

list.title: Promoter penalties
list.description: The IRS is pursuing promoter penalties against companies that made false statements about ERC eligibility.

list.title: Client audits
list.description: Claims filed by identified mills are being flagged for automatic audit.

list.title: Civil lawsuits
list.description: The DOJ has filed civil suits to shut down ERC mills and recover fees.

takeaways: ERC mills used aggressive marketing and promised easy refunds without proper eligibility review
Key warning signs: contingency fees, no documentation review, claims that everyone qualifies
Many mills have disappeared, leaving clients to face audits alone
You are legally responsible for your claim regardless of who prepared it
Get your transcripts and assess your eligibility now—before the IRS contacts you
content.ctaText: Worried you may have worked with an ERC mill? Get a confidential assessment of your claim and learn your options before the IRS contacts you.

faqs.question: What is an ERC mill?
faqs.answer: An ERC mill is a company that aggressively marketed ERC services, often claiming every business qualifies, charging contingency fees, and filing claims without proper documentation or eligibility review.

faqs.question: What are the warning signs?
faqs.answer: Cold calls claiming guaranteed refunds, no review of your actual eligibility, percentage-based fees, no documentation provided to you, and the company is now unreachable.

faqs.question: What should I do if I used an ERC mill?
faqs.answer: Request your IRS transcripts and payroll records, assess your actual eligibility with a qualified professional, consider withdrawal if unprocessed, and prepare documentation for potential audit.

faqs.question: Am I responsible even though they prepared my claim?
faqs.answer: Yes. The IRS holds the taxpayer—you—responsible for the accuracy of your tax filings regardless of who prepared them. The preparer may face separate penalties, but you're on the hook for your claim.

pages.title: How to Withdraw Your ERC Claim
pages.description: You can withdraw an unprocessed ERC claim with no penalties. Learn the process and whether it's right for you.
pages.h1: How to Withdraw Your ERC Claim

content.intro: If you've filed an ERC claim and now have concerns about your eligibility, the IRS Withdrawal Program offers a penalty-free exit. Over 7,300 businesses have already withdrawn claims totaling $677 million. Here's everything you need to know about whether withdrawal is right for you and how to do it.

sections.heading: What is ERC Claim Withdrawal?
sections.content:
The IRS ERC Withdrawal Program allows businesses to withdraw their Employee Retention Credit claims before they're processed. When you withdraw, it's as if you never filed the claim—no penalties, no interest, no audit risk from that claim.

This program was created specifically to help businesses that realized they may not qualify or who were misled by aggressive promoters.

sections.heading: Who Can Withdraw?
sections.content: You may be eligible to withdraw your ERC claim if:

list.title: Claim not yet processed
list.description: Your ERC refund has not been paid. Check your IRS transcripts or call the IRS to confirm status.

list.title: Check not cashed
list.description: You received a refund check but haven't cashed or deposited it yet.

list.title: Filed Form 941-X
list.description: Your claim was submitted on Form 941-X (Adjusted Employer's Quarterly Federal Tax Return).

sections.warning: If you've already received and deposited/spent your ERC refund, you cannot use the withdrawal program. You'll need to work with the IRS on repayment if your claim is disallowed.

sections.heading: Benefits of Withdrawing
sections.content: Withdrawal offers significant advantages over waiting for an audit:

list.title: No penalties
list.description: Withdrawal is completely penalty-free. No 20% accuracy penalty, no fraud penalty, no erroneous claim penalty.

list.title: No interest
list.description: You won't owe any interest on the claimed amount.

list.title: Clean slate
list.description: The claim is treated as if it was never filed. No audit, no investigation.

list.title: Peace of mind
list.description: Eliminate the stress of waiting to see if you'll be audited.

list.title: Return uncashed checks
list.description: If you received but didn't cash the check, you can return it as part of withdrawal.

sections.heading: When You Should Consider Withdrawal
sections.content: Withdrawal may be the right choice if:

list.title: You're unsure about eligibility
list.description: After reviewing the actual ERC requirements, you're not confident you qualified.

list.title: Your preparer used aggressive positions
list.description: The eligibility arguments used in your claim seem questionable.

list.title: No supporting documentation
list.description: You don't have documentation to prove government order impact or gross receipts decline.

list.title: Preparer has disappeared
list.description: The company that filed your claim is now unreachable and you can't get your documentation.

list.title: You want to avoid audit risk
list.description: Even if you might qualify, you prefer certainty over audit risk.

sections.tip: If you're genuinely uncertain about eligibility, consult with a qualified tax professional before deciding. They can review your specific situation and advise whether withdrawal or defense makes more sense.

sections.heading: How to Withdraw Your Claim
sections.content: The withdrawal process involves these steps:

steps.title: Confirm your claim is unprocessed
steps.description: Check your IRS transcripts or call (800) 829-4933 to verify no refund has been issued.

steps.title: Gather your information
steps.description: You'll need your EIN, the quarters you claimed, and copies of your Forms 941-X.

steps.title: Complete the withdrawal request
steps.description: Follow IRS procedures for submitting a withdrawal request. A tax professional can help ensure it's done correctly.

steps.title: Return any uncashed checks
steps.description: If you received a check, include it with your withdrawal request.

steps.title: Get confirmation
steps.description: Request written confirmation that your withdrawal was processed.

sections.heading: What Happens After Withdrawal
sections.content: Once your withdrawal is processed:

list.title: Claim is voided
list.description: Your ERC claim is treated as if it was never filed.

list.title: No further action needed
list.description: You won't receive any IRS correspondence about that claim.

list.title: No record of denial
list.description: There's no disallowance or denial on your record.

list.title: Preparer fees
list.description: You may want to pursue recovery of fees paid to the preparer, though this is a separate civil matter.

takeaways: Withdrawal is available for unprocessed claims or uncashed refund checks
Over 7,300 businesses have withdrawn claims totaling $677 million
Withdrawal is penalty-free—no accuracy penalty, no interest, no audit
Once withdrawn, the claim is treated as if it was never filed
Consult a professional to determine if withdrawal or defense is better for your situation

relatedPages.title: Was Your Preparer an ERC Mill?

content.ctaText: Not sure whether to withdraw your claim? Get a confidential eligibility assessment and professional guidance on the best path forward.

faqs.question: Can I withdraw my ERC claim?
faqs.answer: Yes, if your claim hasn't been processed yet or if you received a check but haven't cashed it. Once withdrawn, the IRS treats it as if the claim was never filed.

faqs.question: Are there penalties for withdrawing?
faqs.answer: No. Withdrawal is completely penalty-free. Over 7,300 businesses have withdrawn claims totaling $677 million with no penalties or interest.

faqs.question: How do I withdraw my claim?
faqs.answer: Follow the IRS withdrawal procedures, which require submitting a withdrawal request with your claim information. A tax professional can help ensure it's done correctly.

faqs.question: What if I already received and spent the refund?
faqs.answer: If you've already received and deposited your refund, you cannot use the withdrawal program. You'll need to address eligibility through the audit/appeals process or voluntary repayment.

pages.title: ERC Audit Statute of Limitations: How Long Can the IRS Audit You?
pages.description: The IRS has 5-6 years to audit your ERC claim. Learn the statute of limitations and what it means for your business.
pages.h1: ERC Audit Statute of Limitations: How Long Can the IRS Audit You?

content.intro: If you received an ERC refund, you may be wondering how long you need to worry about an IRS audit. The standard statute of limitations doesn't apply to ERC claims the same way it does to regular tax returns. Here's what you need to know about how long the IRS can come after your ERC claim.

sections.heading: The 5-Year ERC Assessment Period
