sections.content: The Employee Retention Credit has a special extended assessment period:

list.title: 5 years from filing
list.description: The IRS has 5 years from the date you filed your Form 941-X to assess additional tax related to your ERC claim.

list.title: Not the standard 3 years
list.description: Unlike most tax returns which have a 3-year statute, ERC claims specifically have an extended 5-year window.

list.title: Starts from filing date
list.description: The clock starts when you filed your amended return (Form 941-X), not when you received the refund.

sections.heading: When the Statute is Extended
sections.content: In certain situations, the IRS has even longer to audit your claim:

list.title: 25% understatement (6 years)
list.description: If you overstated your ERC by more than 25%, the statute extends to 6 years.

list.title: Fraud (unlimited)
list.description: If the IRS can prove fraud, there is NO statute of limitations. They can audit you indefinitely.

list.title: No return filed (unlimited)
list.description: If you never filed the underlying Form 941, there's no statute of limitations.

list.title: Substantial omission (6 years)
list.description: Omitting more than 25% of gross income extends the period to 6 years.

sections.warning: If you knowingly filed a false ERC claim, the IRS can audit you at any time—there's no statute of limitations for fraud.

sections.heading: What This Means for You
sections.content: The extended statute has practical implications:

list.title: Keep records longer
list.description: You should retain all ERC documentation for at least 6-7 years from filing.

list.title: Audits will continue for years
list.description: Even if you filed in 2021, the IRS has until 2026-2027 to audit you.

list.title: Don't assume you're safe
list.description: Not hearing from the IRS for a year or two doesn't mean you won't be audited.

list.title: IRS is just getting started
list.description: The major ERC audit wave began in late 2023. Most audits are still ahead.

sections.heading: Calculating Your Statute Expiration
sections.content: To determine when your audit window closes:

steps.title: Find your filing date
steps.description: Locate the date you filed your Form 941-X claiming the ERC.

steps.title: Add 5 years
steps.description: For standard claims, add 5 years to get your basic statute expiration.

steps.title: Consider extensions
steps.description: If your claim involved large amounts or questionable positions, assume 6 years or more.

steps.title: Factor in fraud
steps.description: If there's any fraud involved, the statute never expires.

sections.tip: If you filed an ERC claim for Q2 2020 in October 2021, your standard statute runs until October 2026. But if the IRS determines fraud, they could audit you in 2030 or beyond.

sections.heading: How to Protect Yourself
sections.content: Given the extended audit window, take these precautions:

list.title: Preserve all documentation
list.description: Keep copies of government orders, gross receipts calculations, payroll records, and everything supporting your eligibility.

list.title: Document your decision-making
list.description: Keep records of why you believed you qualified, including any professional advice you received.

list.title: Store records securely
list.description: Use both physical and digital backup for important ERC documentation.

list.title: Know your preparer's status
list.description: If your preparer is still in business, confirm they've retained your file.

list.title: Consider professional review
list.description: Having a current professional review your claim can identify weaknesses before the IRS does.

takeaways: The IRS has 5 years from filing to audit your ERC claim (not the standard 3 years)
Statute extends to 6 years for substantial overstatements (25%+)
There is NO statute of limitations for fraudulent claims
Keep all ERC documentation for at least 6-7 years
The major ERC audit wave is ongoing—don't assume you're safe yet

relatedPages.title: If You Receive an Audit Notice

content.ctaText: Want to know where you stand? Get a professional assessment of your ERC claim before the IRS contacts you.

faqs.question: How long can the IRS audit my ERC claim?
faqs.answer: The IRS has 5 years from when you filed your Form 941-X to assess additional tax related to your ERC claim. This can extend to 6 years for substantial overstatements, and there's no limit for fraud.

faqs.question: When does the statute of limitations start?
faqs.answer: The statute begins when you filed your Form 941-X claiming the ERC, not when you received the refund or when the underlying quarter ended.

faqs.question: Is there a statute of limitations for ERC fraud?
faqs.answer: No. If the IRS can prove fraud, there is no statute of limitations. They can audit and assess penalties at any time, even decades later.

faqs.question: How long should I keep my ERC records?
faqs.answer: Keep all ERC documentation for at least 6-7 years from when you filed your claim. If there's any question about your eligibility, keep records indefinitely.

pages.title: Letter 106-C: Your ERC Claim Was Partially Denied
pages.description: Received Letter 106-C? The IRS partially denied your ERC claim. Learn what was allowed, what was denied, and your options.
pages.h1: IRS Letter 106-C: Partial ERC Denial Explained

content.intro: Letter 106-C means the IRS has reviewed your ERC claim and allowed part of it while denying the rest. This is actually better news than a full denial—you'll receive some of your claimed credit. But you still have important decisions to make about whether to accept the partial allowance or appeal for the full amount.

sections.heading: What Letter 106-C Means
sections.content: A Letter 106-C (also called a partial disallowance) indicates that:

list.title: Some credit allowed
list.description: The IRS determined you qualified for part of the ERC you claimed.

list.title: Some credit denied
list.description: The remaining portion was disallowed because the IRS didn't find sufficient documentation or eligibility.

list.title: Breakdown provided
list.description: The letter specifies which quarters and amounts were allowed vs. denied.

list.title: Appeal rights included
list.description: You have 30 days to appeal the denied portion if you disagree.

sections.heading: Common Reasons for Partial Denial
sections.content: The IRS typically allows some quarters while denying others when:

list.title: Mixed eligibility periods
list.description: You clearly qualified for some quarters (e.g., during shutdowns) but not others (e.g., when restrictions lifted).

list.title: Documentation gaps
list.description: You had strong documentation for some periods but weak or missing documentation for others.

list.title: Gross receipts calculation
list.description: Some quarters met the revenue decline test while others didn't.

list.title: Government order timing
list.description: Orders affected your business during specific periods but not the full claim period.

list.title: Wage allocation issues
list.description: Some wages qualified while others were disallowed due to PPP overlap or employee count issues.

sections.heading: Your Options After Receiving Letter 106-C
sections.content: You have several choices when you receive a partial denial:

list.title: Accept the partial allowance
list.description: If the allowed portion seems fair and you're not confident about the denied portion, you can accept the determination.

list.title: Appeal the denied portion
list.description: Within 30 days, you can request an appeal to IRS Appeals for the denied portion only.

list.title: Appeal the entire determination
list.description: If you believe the calculation is wrong, you can appeal the full determination.

list.title: Request audit reconsideration
list.description: If you have new documentation, you may be able to request reconsideration.

sections.tip: Review the letter carefully to understand exactly which quarters and amounts were denied. This helps you decide if an appeal is worth pursuing.

sections.heading: The 30-Day Appeal Deadline
sections.content: Just like a full denial, you have 30 days to respond:

list.title: Clock starts from letter date
list.description: The 30-day period begins from the date on the letter, not when you received it.

list.title: Written protest required
list.description: For denied amounts over $25,000, you need a formal written protest.

list.title: Specify what you're appealing
list.description: Your protest should clearly identify which quarters/amounts you're contesting.

list.title: Include new evidence
list.description: Provide any documentation that supports your eligibility for the denied portion.

sections.warning: Missing the 30-day deadline limits your options. If you're unsure whether to appeal, at least file a protective protest to preserve your rights.

sections.heading: Should You Appeal a Partial Denial?
sections.content: Consider these factors when deciding whether to appeal:

list.title: Strength of your documentation
list.description: Do you have solid evidence supporting eligibility for the denied quarters?

list.title: Amount at stake
list.description: Is the denied portion large enough to justify the time and potential cost of an appeal?

list.title: Reason for denial
list.description: Was the denial based on missing documentation you can now provide, or a fundamental eligibility issue?

list.title: Cost-benefit analysis
list.description: Professional help for appeals has costs—weigh these against potential recovery.

takeaways: Letter 106-C means part of your ERC claim was allowed and part was denied
You have 30 days to appeal the denied portion
Review which specific quarters/amounts were denied before deciding
You can accept the partial allowance or fight for the denied portion
Consider the strength of your documentation before appealing
content.ctaText: Received a partial denial? We can help you evaluate whether to appeal the denied portion and build the strongest possible case.

faqs.question: What does Letter 106-C mean?
faqs.answer: Letter 106-C is a partial disallowance notice. The IRS allowed some of your ERC claim while denying the rest. You'll receive credit for the allowed portion.

faqs.question: Can I appeal a partial denial?
faqs.answer: Yes. You have 30 days to appeal the denied portion to IRS Appeals. You can also accept the partial allowance if you don't want to contest the denied portion.

faqs.question: Why was part of my claim denied?
faqs.answer: Common reasons include weak documentation for certain quarters, eligibility in some periods but not others, gross receipts calculations that didn't meet thresholds, or PPP wage overlap issues.

faqs.question: Do I still get the allowed portion?
faqs.answer: Yes. The allowed portion of your ERC will be paid. Appealing the denied portion doesn't affect the credit you've been allowed.

pages.title: How to Check Your ERC Refund Status
pages.description: There's no online tool to check ERC status. Learn how to call the IRS, read transcripts, and understand where your claim stands.
pages.h1: How to Check Your ERC Refund Status

content.intro: Waiting for your ERC refund is frustrating, especially when you can't easily check the status online. Unlike income tax refunds, there's no 'Where's My Refund?' tool for ERC claims. Here's how to actually find out what's happening with your claim.

sections.heading: Why There's No Online ERC Status Tool
sections.content: The IRS does not offer an online tracking system for ERC refunds because:

list.title: Different processing system
list.description: ERC claims are processed differently from regular tax refunds, using a separate examination process.

list.title: Manual review required
list.description: Since September 2023, all ERC claims receive individual review rather than automated processing.

list.title: Amended return complexity
list.description: Form 941-X is an amended payroll return, which doesn't integrate with the standard refund tracking system.

sections.heading: Option 1: Call the IRS
sections.content: The most direct way to check your ERC status is calling the IRS:

list.title: Phone number
list.description: Call the IRS Business & Specialty Tax Line at (800) 829-4933.

list.title: Hours
list.description: Monday through Friday, 7:00 AM to 7:00 PM local time.

list.title: Information needed
list.description: Have your EIN, the quarters you claimed, and filing dates ready.

list.title: Expect long hold times
list.description: Wait times can exceed an hour. Call early morning or late afternoon for shorter waits.

list.title: Ask specific questions
list.description: Request the current status of your Form 941-X and whether any action is required from you.

sections.tip: Call at 7:00 AM Eastern on Tuesday, Wednesday, or Thursday for the shortest wait times. Mondays and days after holidays are the busiest.

sections.heading: Option 2: Check Your IRS Transcripts
sections.content: Your business tax transcripts can reveal your claim status:

steps.title: Request transcripts
steps.description: Request Form 941 transcripts for the quarters you claimed ERC.

steps.title: Look for transaction codes
steps.description: Certain codes indicate claim status, adjustments, or holds.

steps.title: Check for TC 846
steps.description: Transaction Code 846 indicates a refund has been issued.

steps.title: Watch for TC 971
steps.description: TC 971 with Action Code 123 may indicate your claim is being reviewed.

steps.title: Note any holds
steps.description: Codes like TC 810 indicate a refund freeze that requires resolution.

sections.heading: Option 3: Use a Tax Professional
sections.content: A tax professional with proper authorization can check for you:

list.title: Practitioner Priority Service
list.description: CPAs and Enrolled Agents have access to a dedicated IRS line with shorter wait times.

list.title: Power of Attorney
list.description: With Form 2848 on file, they can discuss your account directly with the IRS.

list.title: E-Services access
list.description: Authorized practitioners can pull your transcripts electronically.

list.title: Interpretation help
list.description: They can explain what the codes and statuses actually mean for your claim.

sections.heading: Understanding Common Status Updates
sections.content: Here's what different status responses typically mean:

list.title: Claim received, processing
list.description: Your Form 941-X was received and is in the general processing queue. No action needed yet.

list.title: Under review/examination
list.description: Your claim has been selected for closer review. You may receive Letter 6612 or other correspondence.

list.title: Additional information requested
list.description: The IRS needs documentation from you. Check your mail and respond promptly.

list.title: Approved, refund issued
list.description: Your claim was approved and a refund has been sent.

list.title: Partially approved
list.description: Some portion was allowed; you'll receive Letter 106-C with details.

list.title: Disallowed
list.description: Your claim was denied. You'll receive Letter 105-C with appeal rights.

sections.warning: If you're told your claim is 'under review,' don't panic. This doesn't necessarily mean you did anything wrong—the IRS is reviewing all claims individually.

takeaways: There's no online tool to check ERC refund status—you must call or check transcripts
Call the IRS Business line at (800) 829-4933 with your EIN and filing information
IRS transcripts show transaction codes that indicate claim status
A tax professional with Power of Attorney can check status more efficiently
If told you're 'under review,' this is normal—all claims are being individually reviewed
content.ctaText: Can't get a clear answer on your ERC status? Our team can check your claim status and advise on next steps.

faqs.question: Is there an online tool to check ERC refund status?
faqs.answer: No. Unlike regular tax refunds, there's no online tracking for ERC claims. You must call the IRS Business line at (800) 829-4933 or check your business tax transcripts.

faqs.question: What number do I call to check ERC status?
faqs.answer: Call the IRS Business & Specialty Tax Line at (800) 829-4933, Monday through Friday, 7:00 AM to 7:00 PM local time. Have your EIN and filing information ready.

faqs.question: How do I read my IRS transcript for ERC status?
faqs.answer: Look for transaction codes on your Form 941 transcript. TC 846 means refund issued. TC 971 with certain action codes indicates review. TC 810 indicates a refund freeze.

faqs.question: What does 'under review' mean for my ERC claim?
faqs.answer: It means your claim is being individually examined, which is now standard for all ERC claims. This doesn't necessarily indicate a problem—but you may receive correspondence requesting documentation.

pages.title: ERC Fraud Penalties: Criminal Prosecution and Civil Fraud
pages.description: ERC fraud can result in 75% civil penalties and criminal prosecution. Learn what constitutes fraud vs. negligence.
pages.h1: ERC Fraud Penalties: What Happens If the IRS Alleges Fraud

content.urgency: If you're being investigated for ERC fraud, contact a tax attorney immediately—not a CPA. You need attorney-client privilege.
content.intro: The IRS is aggressively pursuing ERC fraud, with over 545 criminal investigations opened and convictions resulting in federal prison sentences averaging 24 months. Understanding the difference between negligence and fraud—and knowing the consequences of each—is critical if you're facing scrutiny of your ERC claim.

sections.heading: IRS ERC Fraud Enforcement
sections.content: The IRS has made ERC fraud a top enforcement priority:

list.title: 545+ criminal investigations
list.description: The IRS Criminal Investigation division has opened over 545 cases involving suspected ERC fraud.

list.title: $5.6 billion under investigation
list.description: These investigations cover billions of dollars in potentially fraudulent claims.

list.title: Active prosecutions
list.description: Multiple defendants have already been convicted and sentenced to federal prison.

list.title: Promoter focus
list.description: The IRS is especially targeting ERC mills and promoters who filed false claims on behalf of clients.

sections.heading: Civil Fraud Penalty (75%)
sections.content: The civil fraud penalty is the most severe civil penalty available:

list.title: 75% of underpayment
list.description: If civil fraud is determined, the penalty is 75% of the disallowed credit amount.

list.title: Replaces accuracy penalty
list.description: The fraud penalty replaces (doesn't stack with) the 20% accuracy penalty.

list.title: IRS burden of proof
list.description: The IRS must prove fraud by clear and convincing evidence—a high standard.

list.title: Intent required
list.description: Fraud requires intentional wrongdoing, not just mistakes or negligence.

sections.heading: Criminal Fraud Penalties
sections.content: Criminal prosecution can result in:

list.title: Federal prison
list.description: Convicted ERC fraud defendants are receiving an average of 24 months in federal prison.

list.title: Substantial fines
list.description: Criminal fines can reach $250,000 for individuals or $500,000 for corporations.

list.title: Restitution
list.description: Courts order repayment of the fraudulent amounts in addition to other penalties.

list.title: Supervised release
list.description: After prison, defendants typically face 1-3 years of supervised release.

list.title: Professional consequences
list.description: CPAs, attorneys, and enrolled agents face loss of their professional licenses.

sections.warning: Criminal tax fraud convictions are federal felonies. They result in prison time at federal facilities, not county jail, and cannot be expunged.

sections.heading: Fraud vs. Negligence: The Critical Distinction
sections.content: Not every incorrect claim is fraud. Understanding the distinction is crucial:

list.title: Negligence (20% penalty)
list.description: Failing to make a reasonable attempt to comply with the law, or being careless in record-keeping. Mistakes happen.

list.title: Substantial understatement (20%)
list.description: Understating eligibility by more than 10% or $5,000, without reasonable cause.

list.title: Civil fraud (75%)
list.description: Intentionally filing a false claim with knowledge it was wrong. Requires intent to deceive.

list.title: Criminal fraud (prison)
list.description: Willfully attempting to evade taxes through false statements. Requires willfulness.

sections.tip: The key factor is intent. If you made a good-faith mistake based on reasonable (even if wrong) interpretation of the rules, that's typically not fraud. If you knowingly filed a claim you knew was false, that's fraud.

sections.heading: Who Is Most at Risk for Fraud Charges?
sections.content: Criminal prosecution typically targets the most egregious cases:

list.title: Promoters and preparers
list.description: Companies that systematically filed false claims on behalf of multiple clients.

list.title: Fabricated documentation
list.description: Business owners who created fake government orders, payroll records, or other evidence.

list.title: False business information
list.description: Claims based on businesses that didn't exist or dramatically inflated employee counts.

list.title: Repeat offenders
list.description: Individuals with prior tax fraud convictions or patterns of tax evasion.

list.title: Large dollar amounts
list.description: Claims involving hundreds of thousands or millions of dollars get more scrutiny.

sections.heading: What to Do If You're Under Investigation
sections.content: If you believe you're being investigated for ERC fraud:

steps.title: Contact a tax attorney immediately
steps.description: You need attorney-client privilege, which CPAs cannot provide. Don't discuss the case with anyone else.

steps.title: Don't talk to investigators
steps.description: Politely decline to answer questions without your attorney present.

steps.title: Preserve all documents
steps.description: Don't destroy any records—that's a separate crime.

steps.title: Understand your rights
steps.description: You have the right to remain silent and the right to counsel.

steps.title: Separate from the preparer
steps.description: If your preparer is under investigation, get your own independent representation.

takeaways: The IRS has 545+ active criminal ERC fraud investigations covering $5.6 billion
Civil fraud penalty is 75% of the disallowed amount; criminal fraud means prison
Fraud requires intent—mistakes and negligence are different (20% penalty)
If under investigation, contact a tax attorney immediately for privilege protection
Promoters and those with fabricated documentation face highest risk
content.ctaText: Concerned about potential fraud exposure? Get confidential guidance from professionals who understand both civil and criminal tax matters.

faqs.question: What is the ERC fraud penalty?
faqs.answer: Civil fraud carries a 75% penalty on the disallowed amount. Criminal fraud can result in federal prison (averaging 24 months), fines up to $250,000, and restitution.

faqs.question: How is fraud different from making a mistake?
faqs.answer: Fraud requires intent—knowingly filing a false claim. Mistakes and negligence result in lower penalties (20%). The IRS must prove fraud by clear and convincing evidence.

faqs.question: Can I go to prison for ERC fraud?
faqs.answer: Yes. The IRS has opened 545+ criminal investigations. Convicted defendants are receiving an average of 24 months in federal prison, plus fines and restitution.

faqs.question: What should I do if I'm being investigated?
faqs.answer: Contact a tax attorney immediately—not a CPA. You need attorney-client privilege. Don't discuss the case with anyone else, don't talk to investigators without counsel, and preserve all documents.

pages.title: ERC Voluntary Disclosure Program: What You Need to Know
pages.description: The ERC Voluntary Disclosure Program closed in November 2024. Learn what options remain for correcting problematic claims.
pages.h1: ERC Voluntary Disclosure Program: Options for Correcting Your Claim

content.intro: The IRS offered two ERC Voluntary Disclosure Programs that allowed businesses to repay improperly received credits at a reduced rate. While these programs have closed, understanding what they were and what options remain is important if you have concerns about your ERC claim.

sections.heading: The ERC Voluntary Disclosure Programs
sections.content: The IRS offered two voluntary disclosure programs specifically for ERC:

list.title: First VDP (March-November 2024)
list.description: Allowed repayment of 80% of the ERC received, keeping 20% to cover preparer fees.

list.title: Second VDP (August-November 2024)
list.description: Required repayment of 85% of the ERC received, keeping 15%.

list.title: Benefits
list.description: No penalties, no interest, and the IRS generally didn't pursue audits of participants.

list.title: Participation
list.description: Thousands of businesses participated, returning billions of dollars in improperly claimed credits.

sections.warning: Both ERC Voluntary Disclosure Programs are now closed. The deadlines have passed and the IRS is not accepting new applications.

sections.heading: Why the Programs Closed
sections.content: The IRS closed these programs because:

list.title: Time-limited by design
list.description: The programs were always intended as temporary relief measures with fixed deadlines.

list.title: Sufficient participation
list.description: The IRS achieved its goal of recovering significant improper payments voluntarily.

list.title: Shift to enforcement
list.description: Resources are now focused on auditing and pursuing those who didn't come forward.

sections.heading: Options That Remain
sections.content: Even with the VDP closed, you still have options if you're concerned about your claim:

list.title: Claim withdrawal
list.description: If your claim is still unprocessed, you can withdraw it penalty-free. This is often the best option for pending claims.

list.title: Amended return
list.description: You can file an amended Form 941-X to reduce or eliminate your ERC claim before the IRS contacts you.

list.title: Voluntary repayment
list.description: You can simply repay the credit you received, though you may still owe interest.

list.title: Wait for audit
list.description: If you believe you legitimately qualified, you can prepare your documentation and wait to defend your claim.

list.title: Negotiate during audit
list.description: If audited, you may be able to negotiate a settlement for less than full disallowance plus penalties.

sections.heading: Comparing Your Options
sections.content: Here's how the remaining options compare:

list.title: Withdrawal (best for unprocessed claims)
list.description: Completely penalty-free, no interest, claim treated as never filed. Only works if you haven't received the refund.

list.title: Voluntary repayment
list.description: You pay back the credit, possibly with interest. May still face audit questions but shows good faith.

list.title: Wait and defend
list.description: If you legitimately qualified, waiting may be best. But prepare your documentation now.

sections.tip: The best option depends on your specific situation. A qualified professional can help you evaluate your eligibility and choose the right path.

sections.heading: Will There Be Another VDP?
sections.content: The IRS has not announced plans for another voluntary disclosure program, but:

list.title: No guarantees
list.description: There's no indication the IRS will offer another ERC-specific VDP.

list.title: General VDP exists
list.description: The IRS has a general voluntary disclosure program, but it doesn't offer the same favorable terms as the ERC-specific programs did.

list.title: Enforcement focus
list.description: The IRS is now focused on audits and enforcement rather than voluntary programs.

takeaways: Both ERC Voluntary Disclosure Programs closed in November 2024
The programs allowed repayment of 80-85% with no penalties or interest
Withdrawal is still available for unprocessed claims—penalty-free
You can still file amended returns or voluntarily repay if concerned
No new ERC-specific VDP has been announced
content.ctaText: Missed the Voluntary Disclosure deadline? Let us help you evaluate your remaining options and choose the best path forward.

faqs.question: Is the ERC Voluntary Disclosure Program still open?
faqs.answer: No. Both ERC Voluntary Disclosure Programs closed in November 2024. The IRS is not accepting new applications.

faqs.question: What were the VDP terms?
faqs.answer: The programs allowed businesses to repay 80-85% of their ERC with no penalties or interest. The IRS generally didn't pursue further action against participants.

faqs.question: What options do I have now?
faqs.answer: You can withdraw unprocessed claims (penalty-free), file amended returns, voluntarily repay the credit, or prepare to defend your claim if audited.

faqs.question: Will there be another Voluntary Disclosure Program?
faqs.answer: The IRS has not announced another ERC-specific VDP. The focus has shifted to enforcement and audits.

pages.title: ERC for Restaurants: Eligibility, Audits, and Defense
pages.description: Restaurants were heavily impacted by COVID restrictions. Learn about ERC eligibility, common audit issues, and how to defend your claim.
pages.h1: ERC for Restaurants: Understanding Your Eligibility and Audit Risk

content.intro: Restaurants were among the hardest-hit industries during COVID-19, making many legitimate candidates for the Employee Retention Credit. However, the restaurant industry also saw significant improper claims, making it a focus for IRS audits. Here's what restaurant owners need to know about ERC eligibility and defending their claims.

sections.heading: Why Restaurants Often Qualified for ERC
sections.content: Many restaurants legitimately qualified for the Employee Retention Credit:

list.title: Mandatory shutdowns
list.description: Many jurisdictions ordered restaurants to close entirely during early 2020.

list.title: Indoor dining restrictions
list.description: Bans on indoor dining constituted a partial suspension of operations even when takeout continued.

list.title: Capacity limits
list.description: 50%, 25%, or other capacity restrictions directly impacted restaurant operations.

list.title: Operating hour restrictions
list.description: Curfews and limited hours reduced operational capacity.

list.title: Gross receipts decline
list.description: Many restaurants experienced the 50% (2020) or 20% (2021) revenue decline required for eligibility.

sections.heading: Common Restaurant ERC Audit Issues
sections.content: The IRS is scrutinizing restaurant ERC claims for these issues:

list.title: Takeout vs. dine-in distinction
list.description: If your restaurant continued full takeout operations, the IRS may question whether you had a 'suspension' of operations.

list.title: Partial suspension documentation
list.description: You need to document exactly how capacity limits or restrictions impacted your specific restaurant.

list.title: Multiple location issues
list.description: Different locations may have had different restrictions—claims should reflect this.

list.title: Timeline accuracy
list.description: Claiming ERC for periods when restrictions weren't in effect raises red flags.

list.title: PPP wage overlap
list.description: The same wages can't be used for both PPP forgiveness and ERC.

sections.warning: Claiming you qualified solely because you're a restaurant isn't enough. You need documentation of specific government orders affecting your specific location(s).

sections.heading: Documentation Restaurants Need
sections.content: To defend your restaurant ERC claim, gather:

list.title: Government orders
list.description: Copies of federal, state, county, and city orders that restricted your operations.

list.title: Capacity limit evidence
list.description: Documentation showing specific capacity restrictions and how they affected your seating.

list.title: Revenue comparison
list.description: Quarterly gross receipts for 2019 vs. 2020/2021 showing the decline.

list.title: Operational changes
list.description: Documentation of how you modified operations (reduced hours, limited menu, etc.).

list.title: Employee records
list.description: Payroll records showing wages paid during eligible quarters.

list.title: Timeline documentation
list.description: Clear records of when restrictions began and ended in your jurisdiction.

sections.heading: Defending Your Restaurant ERC Claim
sections.content: If audited, your defense should demonstrate:

list.title: Specific impact
list.description: How the government orders specifically affected YOUR restaurant's operations—not restaurants in general.

list.title: More than nominal
list.description: That the impact was 'more than nominal' (generally more than 10% of operations).

list.title: Accurate timeline
list.description: That you only claimed ERC for quarters when restrictions actually affected you.

list.title: Proper calculations
list.description: That your wage calculations and PPP coordination were done correctly.

sections.tip: Photos of socially distanced seating, reduced table counts, and outdoor-only setups can powerfully demonstrate operational impact.

takeaways: Many restaurants legitimately qualified due to shutdowns, capacity limits, and restrictions
The IRS is scrutinizing whether takeout-only operations constituted 'suspension'
You need documentation of specific orders affecting your specific location(s)
Claiming ERC just because you're a restaurant isn't sufficient
Photos and contemporaneous records strengthen your audit defense
content.ctaText: Restaurant owner facing an ERC audit? We understand the unique issues restaurants face and can help you build your defense.

faqs.question: Did restaurants automatically qualify for ERC?
faqs.answer: No. Restaurants had to meet the same eligibility tests as other businesses: either a government order that suspended operations (fully or partially) or a significant decline in gross receipts.

faqs.question: Does takeout-only count as a suspension?
faqs.answer: It can, if you can demonstrate that the shift to takeout-only represented more than a nominal impact on your operations. The IRS will examine whether you were able to conduct most of your normal business activity.

faqs.question: What documentation do I need for my restaurant ERC claim?
faqs.answer: Government orders affecting your location, capacity limit documentation, revenue comparisons showing decline, payroll records, and evidence of operational changes like reduced seating or hours.

faqs.question: Are restaurant ERC claims being targeted for audit?
faqs.answer: Restaurants are among the industries receiving significant audit attention because many claims were filed and some were improper. However, many restaurant claims are legitimate and defensible.

pages.title: ERC for Medical Practices: Eligibility and Audit Defense
pages.description: Medical practices affected by elective procedure bans may qualify for ERC. Learn eligibility rules and audit defense strategies.
pages.h1: ERC for Medical Practices: Understanding Your Eligibility

content.intro: Medical practices, dental offices, and healthcare providers were significantly impacted by COVID-19 restrictions—particularly bans on elective procedures. Many healthcare businesses legitimately qualify for substantial ERC claims, but the IRS is closely examining these claims. Here's what healthcare providers need to know.

sections.heading: How Medical Practices Qualified for ERC
sections.content: Healthcare providers could qualify through several pathways:

list.title: Elective procedure bans
list.description: State and federal orders banned elective surgeries and non-essential medical procedures during various periods in 2020-2021.

list.title: Office closures
list.description: Many medical offices were ordered to close for in-person visits except emergencies.

list.title: Capacity restrictions
list.description: Limits on patient volume, waiting room capacity, and appointment scheduling.

list.title: PPE and supply disruptions
list.description: Some practices couldn't operate due to inability to obtain required protective equipment.

list.title: Gross receipts decline
list.description: Many practices saw significant revenue drops due to cancelled appointments and procedures.

sections.heading: The Elective Procedure Question
sections.content: For many medical practices, the key eligibility issue is elective procedures:

list.title: What counts as elective?
list.description: Procedures that could be delayed without immediate health consequences—routine checkups, cosmetic procedures, non-urgent surgeries.

list.title: Partial suspension
list.description: If elective procedures were more than a nominal part of your practice, banning them created a partial suspension.

list.title: More than nominal test
list.description: If elective procedures represented more than 10% of your operations, the ban likely qualifies.

list.title: Documentation needed
list.description: You should document what percentage of your practice was elective procedures.

sections.tip: A dermatology practice that primarily did cosmetic procedures had a clear partial suspension. A trauma center that continued emergency operations may have a weaker claim.

sections.heading: Common Audit Issues for Healthcare ERC
sections.content: The IRS examines healthcare claims for:

list.title: Essential business classification
list.description: Healthcare was often deemed 'essential'—but essential businesses can still qualify if operations were partially suspended.

list.title: Telehealth continuation
list.description: If you shifted to telehealth, the IRS may question whether operations were truly suspended.

list.title: Definition of elective
list.description: You need to show that banned procedures were a significant part of your practice.

list.title: Timeline accuracy
list.description: Elective procedure bans varied by state and time period—your claim should match actual restriction periods.

sections.heading: Documentation for Medical Practice ERC
sections.content: Healthcare providers should gather:

list.title: State health department orders
list.description: Orders banning elective procedures or limiting healthcare operations.

list.title: CMS guidance
list.description: Federal guidance on procedure postponement.

list.title: Practice revenue breakdown
list.description: What percentage of revenue came from procedures that were banned.

list.title: Appointment records
list.description: Showing cancelled procedures and reduced patient volume.

list.title: Operational changes
list.description: Documentation of how you modified practice operations.

sections.heading: Potential ERC Amounts for Medical Practices
sections.content: Healthcare providers can claim significant credits:

list.title: Per employee limits
list.description: $5,000 per employee for 2020, up to $28,000 per employee for 2021.

list.title: Example: 50 employees
list.description: A practice with 50 employees could potentially claim up to $1.65 million total.

list.title: Multiple quarters
list.description: If you qualified for multiple quarters, the credit compounds significantly.

takeaways: Medical practices affected by elective procedure bans often qualified for ERC
Being deemed 'essential' doesn't disqualify you if operations were partially suspended
Document what percentage of your practice involved banned procedures
The IRS examines whether telehealth continuation negates suspension claims
Healthcare claims can be substantial—up to $1.65M for a 50-employee practice
content.ctaText: Healthcare provider with ERC questions? We understand medical practice operations and can help evaluate or defend your claim.

faqs.question: Did medical practices qualify for ERC?
faqs.answer: Many did. Bans on elective procedures, office closures, and capacity restrictions created partial suspensions that qualified practices for the ERC, even though healthcare was often deemed 'essential.'

faqs.question: Does telehealth count against ERC eligibility?
faqs.answer: The IRS may examine whether telehealth allowed you to continue most operations. If a significant portion of your practice couldn't be done via telehealth (procedures, exams), you may still qualify.

faqs.question: How much can a medical practice claim?
faqs.answer: Up to $5,000 per employee for 2020 and up to $28,000 per employee for 2021. A practice with 50 employees could potentially claim up to $1.65 million total.

faqs.question: What documentation do I need?
faqs.answer: State health orders, CMS guidance on procedure bans, revenue breakdowns showing elective procedure percentage, appointment records, and documentation of operational changes.

pages.title: ERC for Construction Companies: Eligibility and Audit Risks
pages.description: Construction companies face IRS skepticism on ERC claims. Learn about supply chain eligibility and documentation requirements.
pages.h1: ERC for Construction Companies: What You Need to Know

content.intro: Construction companies filed many ERC claims based on supply chain disruptions, project delays, and operational restrictions. However, the IRS is particularly skeptical of construction ERC claims, making thorough documentation essential. Here's what construction businesses need to understand about eligibility and audit risk.

sections.heading: How Construction Companies Claimed ERC
sections.content: Construction businesses typically claimed eligibility through:

list.title: Work site shutdowns
list.description: Some jurisdictions ordered construction sites to close during early COVID restrictions.

list.title: Supply chain disruption
list.description: Inability to get materials due to supplier shutdowns caused by government orders.

list.title: Customer/client closures
list.description: Commercial projects were delayed when clients' businesses were shut down.

list.title: Gross receipts decline
list.description: Reduced project volume leading to significant revenue decline.

list.title: Crew restrictions
list.description: Limitations on crew sizes or on-site personnel.

sections.heading: Why the IRS is Skeptical of Construction Claims
sections.content: Construction ERC claims face extra scrutiny because:

list.title: Often deemed essential
list.description: Construction was frequently classified as essential work, meaning many sites remained open.

list.title: Supply chain claims are harder to prove
list.description: You must show that YOUR supplier was closed due to a government order, not just general supply tightness.

list.title: Outdoor work continued
list.description: Many construction operations continued normally since work was outdoors.

list.title: Aggressive promoter claims
list.description: ERC mills heavily marketed to construction companies using questionable eligibility arguments.

sections.warning: Generic claims that 'supply chains were disrupted' are not sufficient. You need to document specific suppliers who were closed due to specific government orders.

sections.heading: Supply Chain Eligibility Requirements
sections.content: To qualify for ERC based on supply chain disruption, you must prove:

list.title: Specific supplier identification
list.description: Name the actual supplier(s) that couldn't provide materials.

list.title: Government order affecting supplier
list.description: Identify the specific government order that caused your supplier to suspend operations.

list.title: Impact on your operations
list.description: Show that you couldn't continue normal operations without those supplies.

list.title: No readily available alternatives
list.description: Demonstrate that you couldn't simply source from alternative suppliers.

list.title: More than nominal impact
list.description: The disruption must have affected more than 10% of your operations.

sections.tip: The best supply chain claims involve suppliers who were actually ordered to close—not suppliers who chose to close or who had supply issues unrelated to government orders.

sections.heading: Documentation Construction Companies Need
sections.content: To defend a construction ERC claim, gather:

list.title: Supplier correspondence
list.description: Emails or letters from suppliers explaining they couldn't fulfill orders due to COVID closures.

list.title: Supplier shutdown orders
list.description: The actual government orders that caused your supplier(s) to close.

list.title: Project delay documentation
list.description: Evidence of projects delayed or cancelled due to supply issues.

list.title: Alternative supplier research
list.description: Documentation showing you tried to find alternative sources.

list.title: Revenue impact
list.description: Financial records showing the impact on your business.

sections.heading: Stronger vs. Weaker Construction Claims
sections.content: Some construction ERC claims are more defensible than others:

list.title: Stronger: Actual site closures
list.description: If your jurisdiction ordered construction sites closed, this is clear eligibility.

list.title: Stronger: Direct supplier closures
list.description: If you can name a supplier who was ordered to close and this stopped your work.

list.title: Weaker: General supply tightness
list.description: Higher material costs or longer lead times don't equal suspension.

list.title: Weaker: Deemed essential but claimed anyway
list.description: If construction was essential in your area and you continued normal operations.

list.title: Weaker: Generic promoter arguments
list.description: Boilerplate supply chain claims without specific documentation.

takeaways: Construction ERC claims face heightened IRS scrutiny
Supply chain claims require documentation of specific suppliers closed by government orders
Being deemed 'essential' doesn't automatically disqualify you, but it weakens the claim
Generic supply chain arguments without specific supplier documentation are high-risk
Site closure orders provide the strongest basis for construction ERC claims
content.ctaText: Construction company facing ERC audit? We can help evaluate your supply chain documentation and build your defense strategy.

faqs.question: Did construction companies qualify for ERC?
faqs.answer: Some did. Those with documented site closures, specific supplier shutdowns due to government orders, or significant gross receipts decline may qualify. General supply chain disruption without specific documentation is not sufficient.

faqs.question: What about supply chain disruption?
faqs.answer: Supply chain eligibility requires documenting that a specific supplier was ordered to close by a government order, that you couldn't get materials from alternatives, and that this impacted more than 10% of your operations.

faqs.question: Why is the IRS skeptical of construction ERC claims?
faqs.answer: Construction was often deemed essential, many operations continued normally, and ERC promoters heavily marketed questionable supply chain arguments to construction companies.

faqs.question: What documentation do construction companies need?
faqs.answer: Supplier correspondence about closures, government orders affecting suppliers, project delay documentation, evidence of alternative supplier research, and financial records showing impact.

pages.title: ERC for Hotels & Hospitality | Audit Defense Guide
pages.description: Hotels and hospitality businesses face unique ERC audit challenges. Learn how to defend your claim with occupancy data, travel ban evidence, and event cancellations.
pages.h1: ERC Audit Defense for Hotels & Hospitality Businesses

content.intro: The hotel and hospitality industry was among the hardest hit by COVID-19 government orders. From travel bans and occupancy restrictions to convention cancellations and quarantine requirements, hotels faced unprecedented operational disruptions. Now the IRS is scrutinizing hospitality ERC claims with particular intensity. This guide explains how hotels, resorts, and hospitality businesses can defend their Employee Retention Credit claims during an audit.

sections.heading: Why Hotels Face Heightened ERC Audit Scrutiny
sections.content:
The IRS has flagged the hospitality sector for increased ERC audits due to the large claim amounts and complexity of operations. Hotels often claimed ERC across multiple quarters, citing various government orders that impacted different aspects of their business—from room occupancy to restaurant service to event hosting.

Auditors are specifically looking for claims where businesses conflated general economic downturns with actual government order impacts, or where they failed to properly allocate wages between suspended and operating business segments.

list.title: Multi-department complexity
list.description: Hotels with restaurants, spas, event spaces, and room service face questions about which operations were actually suspended vs. merely reduced

list.title: Occupancy vs. closure
list.description: The IRS distinguishes between mandatory closures and voluntary capacity reductions due to reduced demand

list.title: Essential service carve-outs
list.description: Many hotels remained open for essential workers, complicating full or partial suspension claims

list.title: Revenue test complications
list.description: PPP loans, government assistance, and changing business models affect gross receipts calculations

sections.warning: Simply experiencing reduced bookings or lower occupancy rates does NOT qualify a hotel for ERC. You must demonstrate a government order directly suspended or limited your operations.

sections.heading: Government Orders That Impacted Hotels
sections.content: Successful ERC defense requires documenting specific government orders that caused operational suspensions. Hotels were subject to numerous orders at federal, state, and local levels that restricted normal operations.

list.title: International and domestic travel bans
list.description: Executive orders restricting travel from specific countries or between states directly reduced the guest pool hotels could serve

list.title: Occupancy percentage limits
list.description: Many states limited hotel occupancy to 25-50% of capacity, forcing closure of floors or wings

list.title: Quarantine and isolation requirements
list.description: Some jurisdictions required 14-day quarantines for travelers, effectively eliminating leisure travel

list.title: Convention and event prohibitions
list.description: Bans on large gatherings eliminated weddings, conferences, and events—often a hotel's most profitable revenue stream

list.title: Restaurant and bar closures
list.description: On-site food and beverage operations were subject to separate dining restrictions

list.title: Pool, spa, and fitness center closures
list.description: Amenity closures affected the full hospitality experience hotels could offer

sections.tip: Gather copies of every government order that affected your operations, including amendments and extensions. Create a timeline matching each order to specific operational changes at your property.

sections.heading: Documenting Partial Suspension for Hotels
sections.content:
Most hotels did not close entirely but experienced partial suspension of operations. The IRS requires you to demonstrate that a government order caused "more than a nominal" impact on your business—generally interpreted as affecting more than 10% of revenue or operations.

For hotels, this often means documenting which revenue-generating activities were suspended while others continued.

list.title: Event and banquet department suspension
list.description: If government orders prohibited gatherings over certain sizes, your event business may have been fully suspended even while rooms remained open

list.title: Food and beverage restrictions
list.description: Restaurant closures, bar shutdowns, and room service limitations each represent suspendable operations with allocable wages

list.title: Amenity closures
list.description: Pools, spas, fitness centers, and business centers subject to closure orders represent additional suspended operations

list.title: Capacity-limited room operations
list.description: If orders required closing entire floors or limiting rooms available, document the percentage reduction

sections.warning: You cannot claim ERC for wages paid to employees who continued working in non-suspended operations. Proper wage allocation between suspended and active departments is critical.

sections.heading: Evidence Hotels Should Prepare for Audits
sections.content: The IRS will request extensive documentation to verify your ERC claim. Hotels should proactively organize the following evidence to demonstrate their eligibility and wage calculations.

list.title: Government order documentation
list.description: Copies of all federal, state, county, and municipal orders affecting hotel operations, with effective dates and expiration dates

list.title: Occupancy records and PMS data
list.description: Property management system reports showing room inventory available vs. occupied, demonstrating capacity restrictions

list.title: Event cancellation records
list.description: Banquet event orders (BEOs) for cancelled events, refund documentation, and rebooking records

list.title: Department-level P&L statements
list.description: Financial statements broken down by rooms, F&B, events, spa, and other departments to show revenue impact by segment

list.title: Employee schedules and payroll allocation
list.description: Records showing which employees worked in which departments during claim periods

list.title: Operational memos and communications
list.description: Internal documents showing how management responded to government orders, including floor closures, reduced staffing, and modified services

sections.tip: Hotels using major property management systems like Opera, Maestro, or Cloudbeds should export detailed reports now—these systems track capacity and occupancy data essential for audit defense.

sections.heading: Common Audit Challenges for Hospitality Businesses
sections.content: IRS auditors examining hotel ERC claims typically focus on several key issues. Understanding these challenges helps you prepare effective responses.

list.title: Distinguishing demand decline from government suspension
list.description: Auditors will question whether reduced occupancy resulted from government orders or simply reduced travel demand. You must show orders directly restricted your operations.

list.title: Essential business exceptions
list.description: Hotels that remained open for essential workers, first responders, or quarantine housing may face questions about whether they truly experienced suspension

list.title: Inconsistent wage allocation
list.description: Hotels claiming ERC for front desk staff while those same employees continued serving remaining guests face allocation scrutiny

list.title: Aggregation rule complications
list.description: Hotel management companies and franchise groups may need to aggregate employees across properties for the 100/500 employee threshold

list.title: Gross receipts calculation errors
list.description: Room revenue, F&B revenue, and ancillary income must be properly calculated, especially with deferred revenue from cancelled events

sections.heading: Defending Your Hotel's ERC Claim
sections.content: When facing an IRS audit, hotels need a strategic defense that clearly connects government orders to operational suspensions and wage calculations. Work with an ERC audit defense attorney who understands hospitality industry operations and can present your case effectively.

list.title: Build a chronological narrative
list.description: Create a clear timeline showing which orders affected which departments in which quarters, with corresponding wage allocations

list.title: Quantify the suspension impact
list.description: Calculate the percentage of normal operations suspended using metrics like RevPAR, F&B covers, event revenue, and amenity usage

list.title: Separate departments clearly
list.description: Treat each revenue-generating department as a distinct operation that may have experienced different suspension levels

list.title: Address auditor concerns proactively
list.description: Anticipate questions about essential status, demand vs. orders, and wage allocation—provide explanations before being asked

sections.tip: If you used an ERC promoter who filed aggressive claims without proper documentation, consider whether voluntary disclosure or amended returns may reduce penalties before the audit progresses.

takeaways: Hotels must demonstrate specific government orders caused operational suspensions—not just reduced travel demand
Document suspension separately for rooms, F&B, events, and amenities since each may have different eligibility periods
Proper wage allocation between suspended and operating departments is critical for audit defense
Gather PMS data, event cancellation records, and department-level financials before responding to IRS inquiries
Work with counsel experienced in hospitality ERC claims to present a clear, documented defense

relatedPages.title: When to Hire an ERC Audit Attorney

content.ctaText: Is Your Hotel Facing an ERC Audit? Get Expert Defense

faqs.question: Can hotels claim ERC if they remained open during COVID-19?
faqs.answer: Yes, hotels that remained open can still qualify for ERC through partial suspension. If government orders closed or restricted specific operations like restaurants, event spaces, pools, or limited room capacity, those suspended operations may support an ERC claim even if the hotel never fully closed.

faqs.question: How should hotels allocate wages between suspended and active operations?
faqs.answer: Hotels should allocate wages based on the department or function where each employee worked. Employees exclusively assigned to suspended operations (like banquet servers when events were prohibited) may fully qualify. Employees working across departments require proportional allocation based on time spent in suspended vs. active operations.

faqs.question: Do travel bans qualify hotels for ERC?
faqs.answer: Travel bans can support ERC eligibility, but hotels must show the ban directly impacted their specific guest base. A hotel near an international airport serving primarily international travelers has a stronger case than a roadside hotel serving domestic drivers. Document your typical guest origin and how specific travel restrictions affected that population.

faqs.question: What if our hotel was designated for essential worker housing?
faqs.answer: Hotels used for essential worker housing, healthcare overflow, or quarantine facilities may still claim partial suspension for other operations. The rooms serving essential purposes may not qualify, but suspended event spaces, restaurants, and amenities could still support claims if government orders restricted those specific operations.

pages.title: ERC for Gyms & Fitness Centers | Audit Defense Guide
pages.description: Gyms and fitness centers face IRS scrutiny on ERC claims. Learn how to defend your credit with closure orders, capacity limits, and class restriction documentation.
pages.h1: ERC Audit Defense for Gyms & Fitness Centers

content.intro: Gyms, fitness centers, and health clubs experienced some of the most direct and prolonged government-ordered closures during COVID-19. From complete shutdowns to capacity restrictions to bans on specific activities like group classes and locker room use, fitness businesses faced clear operational suspensions. However, the IRS is now closely examining ERC claims from this industry. This guide explains how gyms and fitness centers can defend their Employee Retention Credit claims during an audit.

sections.heading: Why the IRS is Targeting Gym ERC Claims
sections.content: While gyms had clear government-ordered closures in many jurisdictions, the IRS is scrutinizing fitness industry claims for several reasons. Many gyms claimed ERC for extended periods, and some continued paying employees during closures who performed little or no work—raising questions about whether wages were truly for employment retention or simply unemployment alternatives.

list.title: Extended closure claims
list.description: Some gyms claimed full suspension for many quarters, raising IRS questions about whether all claimed periods truly involved government orders

list.title: Employee activity during closures
list.description: Auditors examine whether paid employees performed any work (maintenance, virtual classes, equipment delivery) that might affect qualification

list.title: Reopening date disputes
list.description: The IRS may challenge when suspension actually ended, especially in jurisdictions with phased reopening

list.title: Capacity limit interpretation
list.description: Questions about whether capacity restrictions constituted partial suspension or merely reduced demand

sections.warning: Gyms that paid employees during closures but had those employees perform work must properly document the nature and extent of that work—it affects ERC calculations.

sections.heading: Government Orders Affecting Gyms and Fitness Centers
sections.content: Fitness facilities were subject to extensive government restrictions that evolved through multiple phases. Document each order and its specific provisions to demonstrate how your operations were suspended.

list.title: Complete facility closures
list.description: Many states ordered gyms completely closed during initial lockdowns, with some closures extending for months

list.title: Capacity percentage limits
list.description: Reopening often came with 10%, 25%, or 50% capacity restrictions that limited how many members could use the facility

list.title: Group fitness class prohibitions
list.description: Many jurisdictions banned group classes even when individual workouts were permitted, suspending a major revenue stream

list.title: Locker room and shower closures
list.description: Amenity restrictions affected the full service gyms could offer and changed member behavior

list.title: Equipment spacing requirements
list.description: Six-foot distancing rules between equipment effectively reduced capacity beyond stated percentage limits

list.title: Mask requirements during exercise
list.description: While not closures, mask mandates during cardio exercise affected gym usability and member willingness to attend

list.title: Pool and sauna closures
list.description: Water features and high-humidity areas often faced separate closure orders

sections.tip: Create a detailed timeline mapping each government order to specific changes in your gym's operations. Include the date, order citation, and specific provisions affecting your business.

sections.heading: Documenting Full vs. Partial Suspension
sections.content: Gyms experienced both full suspension periods (complete closure) and partial suspension periods (limited operations). Each requires different documentation approaches.

list.title: Full suspension documentation
list.description: Gather closure orders, member communications announcing closure, utility reduction records, and security-only staffing schedules

list.title: Partial suspension for capacity limits
list.description: Document your normal member capacity, the government-mandated limit, and actual attendance during restricted periods

list.title: Service-specific suspensions
list.description: If group classes were banned while individual workouts continued, document class schedules before and during restrictions

list.title: Outdoor-only periods
list.description: Some gyms could operate outdoors only—document the equipment you could offer vs. your full indoor facility

list.title: Appointment-only requirements
list.description: Mandatory reservation systems that limited spontaneous visits represent operational modifications worth documenting

sections.warning: The IRS distinguishes between government-ordered restrictions and voluntary operational choices. If you closed your pool voluntarily out of caution rather than government order, that doesn't support ERC eligibility.

sections.heading: Evidence Gyms Should Prepare for Audits
sections.content: Fitness centers should compile comprehensive documentation demonstrating eligibility and proper wage calculations. The IRS will request evidence supporting your claim.

list.title: Government orders with effective dates
list.description: Every federal, state, county, and municipal order affecting gym operations, with clear effective and end dates

list.title: Member access records
list.description: Check-in data from your access control system showing usage before, during, and after restrictions

list.title: Class schedules and cancellations
list.description: Group fitness schedules showing cancelled classes during prohibition periods

list.title: Employee schedules and assignments
list.description: Payroll records showing which employees worked, their hours, and assigned duties during each period

list.title: Membership communications
list.description: Emails, texts, and social media posts announcing closures, reopenings, and modified operations

list.title: Facility modification records
list.description: Photos, invoices, or work orders showing equipment spacing, capacity signage, and safety modifications

list.title: Revenue comparisons
list.description: Monthly revenue data showing impact on membership dues, personal training, and class revenue

sections.tip: Access control systems (key fobs, app check-ins) provide excellent objective data. Export detailed reports showing member visits by date—this proves actual capacity limitations.

sections.heading: Special Considerations for Different Gym Types
sections.content: The fitness industry includes various business models, each with unique ERC considerations. Your specific business type affects how you should document and defend your claim.

list.title: Large commercial gyms
list.description: Multi-location operations may need to aggregate employees and document orders affecting different locations differently

list.title: Boutique fitness studios
list.description: Class-based businesses like yoga, cycling, or HIIT studios may have stronger full suspension arguments when classes were prohibited

list.title: Personal training studios
list.description: One-on-one training may have been permitted when group activities weren't—document any distinctions in applicable orders

list.title: CrossFit and functional fitness
list.description: Outdoor workout options and tight-knit communities may have allowed some continued operations worth documenting

list.title: Franchise operations
list.description: Franchisees must determine whether they're separate employers or aggregated with the franchisor for ERC purposes

list.title: Gym chains with common ownership
list.description: Related gyms under common control may need to aggregate for employee count thresholds

sections.heading: Responding to IRS Audit Inquiries
sections.content: When the IRS audits your gym's ERC claim, respond strategically with organized documentation and clear explanations. An ERC audit attorney can help present your case effectively.

list.title: Lead with government orders
list.description: Start your response by clearly identifying each government order that suspended your operations, with citations and effective dates

list.title: Demonstrate direct impact
list.description: Connect each order to specific operational changes—don't just list orders, explain what you had to stop doing

list.title: Quantify the suspension
list.description: Use member check-in data, revenue figures, and class cancellation counts to show the magnitude of impact

list.title: Explain employee retention decisions
list.description: Document why you kept employees on payroll (to retain trained staff, fulfill membership obligations, prepare for reopening)

list.title: Address any continued operations
list.description: If employees performed some work during closures (maintenance, virtual classes), explain what they did and how wages were allocated

sections.tip: If your ERC claim was prepared by a promoter who didn't gather proper documentation, work with defense counsel to build the evidentiary record now—before submitting audit responses.

takeaways: Gyms had clear government-ordered closures but must still document specific orders and their direct operational impact
Distinguish between full suspension (complete closure) and partial suspension (capacity limits, class prohibitions) in your documentation
Access control and check-in data provides objective evidence of capacity restrictions and operational changes
Different gym business models face different audit considerations—boutique studios differ from large commercial gyms
Proactively organize government orders, member data, and employee records before responding to IRS inquiries

relatedPages.title: When to Hire an ERC Audit Attorney

faqs.question: Can gyms claim ERC for periods when they offered outdoor or virtual workouts?
faqs.answer: Yes, gyms can potentially claim ERC for periods when indoor operations were suspended even if they offered outdoor or virtual alternatives. However, wages must be properly allocated. If instructors taught virtual classes, those wages may not qualify, while wages for staff unable to perform their normal duties might. Document what each employee did during these periods.

faqs.question: How do capacity limits support gym ERC claims?
faqs.answer: Capacity limits can support partial suspension claims if they represented more than a nominal impact on operations (generally interpreted as more than 10%). A gym limited to 25% capacity has a strong argument for 75% partial suspension. Document your normal capacity, the mandated limit, and actual attendance to quantify the impact.

faqs.question: What if members could cancel memberships during closure?
faqs.answer: Member cancellations during government-ordered closures don't disqualify ERC claims—the credit is based on government orders suspending operations, not on revenue loss from member choices. However, your gross receipts calculations should accurately reflect actual revenue received during claim periods.

faqs.question: Do gym franchise owners need to aggregate with the franchisor for ERC?
faqs.answer: It depends on the franchise relationship. Franchisees operating as genuinely independent businesses typically don't aggregate with the franchisor. However, if common ownership, shared management, or other factors create a controlled group relationship, aggregation may be required. Review your franchise agreement and ownership structure with counsel.

pages.title: ERC for Retail Stores | Audit Defense Guide
pages.description: Retail stores face complex ERC audit questions about essential status, capacity limits, and curbside operations. Learn how to defend your retail ERC claim.
pages.h1: ERC Audit Defense for Retail Stores

content.intro: Retail businesses faced a complex patchwork of COVID-19 restrictions that varied dramatically by store type, location, and time period. While some retailers were deemed essential and remained open, others faced complete closures or were limited to curbside pickup. The IRS is now scrutinizing retail ERC claims, particularly questioning whether stores truly experienced suspension of operations or simply adapted to new operating conditions. This guide helps retail businesses understand and defend their ERC audit positions.

sections.heading: The Retail ERC Audit Challenge
sections.content:
Retail businesses face unique ERC audit challenges because government orders treated different types of retail very differently. Essential retailers like grocery stores and pharmacies often remained open, while non-essential retail faced closures. Even within those categories, specific restrictions varied by jurisdiction and changed frequently.

The IRS is particularly focused on retailers who claimed ERC while continuing significant operations, and on businesses that may have conflated reduced consumer demand with government-ordered suspensions.

list.title: Essential vs. non-essential classification
list.description: Auditors examine whether your store type was actually subject to closure orders in your jurisdiction

list.title: Curbside and delivery operations
list.description: The IRS questions whether curbside pickup constitutes full suspension or merely modified operations

list.title: Capacity limit impact
list.description: Retailers must demonstrate that capacity restrictions caused more than nominal operational impact

list.title: Mall and shopping center closures
list.description: Stores in closed malls may have different eligibility than standalone locations with the same restrictions

sections.warning: Being classified as 'non-essential' does not automatically qualify your retail store for ERC. You must demonstrate specific government orders that suspended your particular operations.

sections.heading: Government Orders Affecting Retail Businesses
sections.content: Retail restrictions took many forms across different jurisdictions and time periods. Document the specific orders that applied to your store type in your location.

list.title: Non-essential business closures
list.description: Orders closing stores selling non-essential goods like clothing, electronics, furniture, and specialty items

list.title: Mall and shopping center closures
list.description: Even if your store type could operate standalone, mall closures may have prevented your specific location from opening

list.title: Capacity percentage limits
list.description: Limits on customer occupancy (often 25-50% of fire code capacity) restricted how many shoppers could enter

list.title: Curbside-only requirements
list.description: Periods when customers could not enter stores and all sales required outside pickup

list.title: Fitting room closures
list.description: Apparel retailers faced restrictions on fitting room use even when stores could open

list.title: Operating hour restrictions
list.description: Reduced hours and curfews that limited when stores could be open

list.title: County and municipal variations
list.description: Different jurisdictions imposed different restrictions—a chain might have had different rules for different locations

sections.tip: For multi-location retailers, document orders separately for each store location. Different jurisdictions may have had different restrictions at different times.

sections.heading: Essential Retailers and Partial Suspension
sections.content: Retailers classified as essential—grocery stores, pharmacies, hardware stores—often remained open throughout the pandemic. However, these businesses may still qualify for ERC if government orders suspended specific aspects of their operations.

list.title: In-store service departments
list.description: Deli counters, bakeries, prepared food sections, and other service areas within stores may have been suspended

list.title: Specialty departments
list.description: Garden centers, optical departments, and other ancillary operations may have faced separate restrictions

list.title: Product category restrictions
list.description: Some jurisdictions prohibited sales of 'non-essential' items even in essential stores (garden supplies, clothing sections)

list.title: Customer service functions
list.description: Returns, exchanges, and in-person customer service may have been suspended or severely limited

list.title: Capacity impacts on operations
list.description: Even essential retailers with capacity limits may argue partial suspension if the limits significantly impacted operations

sections.warning: Essential retailers must be careful not to overstate suspension claims. The fact that you had to implement safety measures or that sales declined does not itself establish suspension of operations.

sections.heading: Documenting Retail ERC Eligibility
sections.content: Retail businesses need comprehensive documentation connecting government orders to specific operational suspensions. Gather evidence now rather than trying to reconstruct it during an audit.

list.title: Government orders by location
list.description: For each store location, collect all applicable federal, state, county, and municipal orders with effective dates

list.title: Store closure and reopening records
list.description: Internal records documenting when each location closed, reopened, or modified operations

list.title: Point-of-sale data
list.description: Transaction data showing sales volume, transaction counts, and operational patterns during restriction periods

list.title: Inventory and merchandising changes
list.description: Records of sections closed, inventory pulled, or departments shuttered due to orders

list.title: Staffing schedules and payroll
list.description: Documentation of which employees worked in which capacities during different restriction phases

list.title: Customer communications
list.description: Signage, emails, and social media posts explaining closure, curbside procedures, or capacity limits to customers

list.title: Landlord communications
list.description: For mall tenants, documentation of center closures and access restrictions

sections.tip: POS systems contain valuable data for audit defense. Export detailed reports showing transaction counts, ticket sizes, and operational hours for comparison across periods.

sections.heading: Curbside Operations and ERC Eligibility
sections.content: Many retailers operated curbside pickup during periods when indoor shopping was prohibited. The IRS examines whether curbside operations constitute suspension of normal operations or merely a modified operating model.

list.title: Full suspension argument
list.description: Retailers can argue that curbside-only represents full suspension of their normal in-store retail operation, with curbside being a different business activity

list.title: Partial suspension approach
list.description: Alternatively, curbside may represent partial operations, with wages allocated between suspended in-store activities and continued curbside functions

list.title: Employee role changes
list.description: Document how employee duties changed—sales associates becoming order pickers, for example—to support either argument

list.title: Revenue impact
list.description: Quantify how curbside-only operations affected sales volume compared to normal in-store operations

list.title: Customer experience differences
list.description: Document how the customer experience fundamentally changed when indoor shopping was prohibited

sections.warning: If you claim full suspension while operating curbside, be prepared to explain why curbside represented a fundamentally different operation rather than modified retail. Inconsistent positions can undermine credibility.

sections.heading: Common Retail Audit Issues and Responses
sections.content: Understanding typical IRS audit questions helps you prepare effective responses. Retail ERC audits commonly focus on these issues.

list.title: Demand decline vs. government orders
list.description: Auditors distinguish between reduced shopping (not qualifying) and government-ordered closures (potentially qualifying). Focus on orders, not economic conditions.
