list.title: Essential classification disputes
list.description: The IRS may argue your store was essential and not subject to closure. Have documentation ready showing the specific orders that applied to your store type.

list.title: Aggregation for retail chains
list.description: Multi-location retailers with common ownership may need to aggregate employees for threshold calculations. Understand your corporate structure.

list.title: Gross receipts calculations
list.description: Ensure accurate revenue calculations that properly account for returned merchandise, gift cards, and deferred revenue.

list.title: Wage allocation methodology
list.description: If claiming partial suspension, document your methodology for allocating wages between suspended and continuing operations.

sections.tip: Work with an ERC audit attorney who understands retail operations. The distinction between modified operations and suspended operations often determines audit outcomes.

takeaways: Retail ERC eligibility depends on specific government orders affecting your store type and location—not on reduced demand or voluntary closures
Essential retailers may still claim partial suspension if specific departments or services were suspended by government order
Curbside-only operations require careful analysis—document whether you're claiming full or partial suspension and why
Multi-location retailers should document orders separately for each location as restrictions varied by jurisdiction
POS data, employee schedules, and customer communications provide valuable audit defense evidence

relatedPages.title: When to Hire an ERC Audit Attorney

content.ctaText: Is Your Retail Business Facing an ERC Audit? Get Expert Help

faqs.question: Can retail stores that were deemed essential claim ERC?
faqs.answer: Essential retailers may qualify for ERC if government orders suspended specific aspects of their operations. For example, a grocery store may claim partial suspension if deli counters, bakeries, or in-store dining areas were closed by government order. The key is identifying specific orders that suspended particular operations, not just implementing safety measures.

faqs.question: How does curbside pickup affect retail ERC eligibility?
faqs.answer: Curbside-only periods can support ERC claims if you demonstrate that government orders prohibited indoor shopping. You can argue either full suspension (curbside is a different operation) or partial suspension (curbside represents limited operations). Document your approach clearly and allocate wages accordingly.

faqs.question: Do retail chains need to combine all locations for ERC employee counts?
faqs.answer: Retail chains under common ownership typically must aggregate employees across all locations for the 100/500 employee thresholds. However, separately owned franchise locations may be treated independently. Review your corporate structure and ownership to determine the correct treatment.

faqs.question: What if my retail store closed voluntarily due to safety concerns?
faqs.answer: Voluntary closures do not qualify for ERC—the credit requires government orders that suspended operations. If your store closed before a government order required closure, only the period after the order became effective would potentially qualify. Document the exact effective date of applicable orders.

pages.title: ERC for Churches & Nonprofits | Audit Defense Guide
pages.description: Churches and nonprofits face unique ERC audit challenges including gathering restrictions and special nonprofit rules. Learn how to defend your organization's ERC claim.
pages.h1: ERC Audit Defense for Churches & Nonprofits

content.intro: Churches, religious organizations, and nonprofit entities faced significant operational disruptions during COVID-19, with government orders restricting gatherings at the core of many organizations' missions. From worship service limitations to program cancellations to fundraising event prohibitions, nonprofits experienced clear operational suspensions. However, the IRS is now examining ERC claims from tax-exempt organizations with particular scrutiny. This guide explains how churches and nonprofits can defend their Employee Retention Credit claims during an audit.

sections.heading: Special ERC Rules for Tax-Exempt Organizations
sections.content: While most ERC rules apply equally to for-profit and nonprofit employers, several provisions specifically affect churches and tax-exempt organizations. Understanding these special rules is essential for audit defense.

list.title: No gross receipts test for nonprofits
list.description: Tax-exempt organizations under IRC Section 501(c) can only qualify for ERC through the government order test, not the gross receipts decline test

list.title: All operations count
list.description: For nonprofits, all activities in furtherance of the organization's mission constitute operations for suspension analysis—not just revenue-generating activities

list.title: Qualified wages definition
list.description: Wages paid to employees count regardless of whether the employee's position is funded by donations, grants, or earned revenue

list.title: Group exemption considerations
list.description: Churches and affiliates operating under group exemptions must determine whether they're separate employers or aggregated for ERC purposes

sections.warning: Because nonprofits cannot use the gross receipts test, documenting government orders that suspended operations is absolutely critical. Without clear government order documentation, nonprofit ERC claims fail.

sections.heading: Government Orders Affecting Churches and Nonprofits
sections.content: Churches and nonprofits were subject to gathering restrictions that directly impacted their core operations. Document every order that affected your organization's ability to conduct normal activities.

list.title: Indoor gathering prohibitions
list.description: Complete bans on indoor gatherings of any size suspended worship services, support groups, and educational programs

list.title: Capacity percentage limits
list.description: Restrictions limiting gatherings to 10, 25, or 50 people—or percentages of building capacity—significantly impacted larger organizations

list.title: Singing and group activity bans
list.description: Specific prohibitions on singing, chanting, or group activities affected worship formats even when some gatherings were permitted

list.title: Food service restrictions
list.description: Soup kitchens, food pantries, and community meal programs faced separate food service regulations

list.title: Youth program suspensions
list.description: Childcare, youth groups, vacation bible school, and after-school programs faced specific restrictions

list.title: Event and fundraising prohibitions
list.description: Galas, concerts, festivals, and fundraising events were prohibited under gathering restrictions

sections.tip: Religious organizations may have faced different treatment than secular gatherings in some jurisdictions. Document the specific orders that applied to religious services in your state and locality.

sections.heading: Documenting Suspension for Religious Organizations
sections.content: Churches must demonstrate that government orders prevented them from conducting their normal religious and community activities. Document how each aspect of your ministry was affected.

list.title: Worship service changes
list.description: Document service cancellations, outdoor-only periods, capacity-limited services, and any periods when in-person worship was fully suspended

list.title: Religious education impacts
list.description: Sunday school, bible study, confirmation classes, and other education programs that were cancelled or moved online

list.title: Pastoral care restrictions
list.description: Hospital visitation bans, restrictions on home visits, and funeral attendance limits affected core pastoral functions

list.title: Community programs suspended
list.description: Food pantries, homeless services, counseling programs, and support groups that couldn't operate normally

list.title: Lifecycle events affected
list.description: Weddings, funerals, baptisms, and other ceremonies subject to attendance restrictions

list.title: Music ministry limitations
list.description: Choir cancellations, restrictions on congregational singing, and worship team limitations

sections.warning: Voluntary decisions to cancel services for safety reasons don't qualify for ERC. You must show government orders required the suspension—even if you would have made the same choice voluntarily.

sections.heading: Evidence Churches and Nonprofits Should Prepare
sections.content: Nonprofit ERC audits require documentation demonstrating government orders suspended normal operations. Gather comprehensive evidence connecting orders to specific operational changes.

list.title: Government orders with citations
list.description: All federal, state, county, and municipal orders affecting gatherings, religious services, and nonprofit operations

list.title: Congregational communications
list.description: Bulletins, emails, website announcements, and social media posts explaining service changes and program cancellations

list.title: Program schedules before and during
list.description: Calendars showing normal programming compared to suspended or modified schedules during restriction periods

list.title: Attendance records
list.description: If you track attendance, data showing pre-pandemic capacity compared to restriction-period attendance

list.title: Staff assignments and duties
list.description: Documentation of what employees did during suspension periods—whether continuing modified duties or unable to perform normal functions

list.title: Board meeting minutes
list.description: Meeting records discussing operational changes in response to government orders

list.title: Denominational guidance
list.description: Communications from denominational bodies interpreting government orders for member churches

sections.tip: Churches that livestreamed services during closures should document this as evidence of suspension—the need to livestream demonstrates in-person gatherings were prohibited, not that operations continued normally.

sections.heading: Special Considerations for Different Organization Types
sections.content: Different types of religious and nonprofit organizations face different ERC audit considerations based on their specific activities and structures.

list.title: Multi-site churches
list.description: Churches with multiple campuses may need to aggregate employees and document orders affecting different locations differently

list.title: Religious schools
list.description: Schools attached to churches face both gathering restrictions and education-specific orders—document both

list.title: Charitable organizations
list.description: Food banks, homeless shelters, and service organizations may have been deemed essential but still experienced partial suspension

list.title: Membership organizations
list.description: Clubs, associations, and membership nonprofits facing meeting restrictions should document event cancellations and membership activity changes

list.title: Grant-funded programs
list.description: Programs funded by government grants may face additional scrutiny about whether ERC duplicates other COVID relief

list.title: Denominational structures
list.description: Churches part of denominations should understand whether they're treated as independent employers or aggregated with the denomination

sections.heading: Responding to IRS Audit Inquiries
sections.content: When your church or nonprofit receives an ERC audit notice, respond strategically with well-organized documentation. Consider working with counsel experienced in both ERC and nonprofit tax law.

list.title: Explain your mission and operations
list.description: Help the auditor understand what your organization does—don't assume they understand church or nonprofit operations

list.title: Connect orders to mission suspension
list.description: Clearly explain how gathering restrictions prevented you from conducting your core religious or charitable mission

list.title: Address any continuing operations
list.description: If staff conducted some work during closures (online services, phone counseling), explain what was modified vs. suspended

list.title: Document employee retention rationale
list.description: Explain why you retained staff during suspension—to be ready for reopening, to maintain community connections, to provide modified services

list.title: Show you didn't use gross receipts test
list.description: Confirm your eligibility rests on government orders, not revenue decline, since nonprofits can't use that test

sections.tip: Many IRS auditors are less familiar with nonprofit operations than for-profit businesses. Take time to educate the examiner about how your organization works and why gathering restrictions were so impactful.

takeaways: Tax-exempt organizations can only qualify for ERC through government orders—not gross receipts decline
Gathering restrictions directly suspended core operations for most churches and many nonprofits
Document all government orders affecting religious services, programs, and charitable activities
Livestreaming and online services demonstrate suspension, not continuation, of normal operations
Work with counsel who understands both ERC rules and nonprofit tax law for optimal audit defense

relatedPages.title: When to Hire an ERC Audit Attorney

content.ctaText: Is Your Church or Nonprofit Facing an ERC Audit? Get Expert Help

faqs.question: Can churches claim ERC if they held outdoor services during closures?
faqs.answer: Yes, churches can claim ERC for periods when indoor worship was prohibited even if outdoor services occurred. The suspension of normal indoor operations supports eligibility. Document the government orders prohibiting indoor gatherings and how outdoor services represented a modified—not normal—operation.

faqs.question: Do nonprofits need to show revenue decline to claim ERC?
faqs.answer: No. Tax-exempt organizations under IRC Section 501(c) cannot use the gross receipts test for ERC eligibility. Nonprofits must qualify solely through the government order test by demonstrating operations were fully or partially suspended by COVID-19 government orders.

faqs.question: Can nonprofit staff who worked remotely qualify for ERC wages?
faqs.answer: It depends on whether the remote work represented suspended operations or continued operations in a different format. Staff who couldn't perform their normal duties (like children's ministry leaders with no children's programs) may qualify even if doing some other work. Staff whose jobs continued largely unchanged remotely may not qualify.

faqs.question: How do religious school closures affect church ERC claims?
faqs.answer: If your church operates a school, school closure orders may support ERC claims for wages paid to school employees. Document the specific orders closing schools in your jurisdiction. If the church and school are treated as one employer, school suspension contributes to the overall suspension analysis.

pages.title: ERC for Manufacturing | Audit Defense Guide
pages.description: Manufacturing businesses face complex ERC audit questions about essential status, supply chain disruptions, and workforce restrictions. Learn how to defend your manufacturing ERC claim.
pages.h1: ERC Audit Defense for Manufacturing Businesses

content.intro: Manufacturing businesses present complex ERC eligibility questions. Many manufacturers were deemed essential and continued operations, yet faced significant disruptions from supply chain interruptions, workforce restrictions, and customer order suspensions. The IRS is examining manufacturing ERC claims carefully, distinguishing between legitimate government-order suspensions and general pandemic economic effects. This guide helps manufacturers understand their audit position and prepare effective defense strategies.

sections.heading: The Manufacturing ERC Challenge
sections.content:
Manufacturers face unique ERC audit scrutiny because their eligibility often depends on nuanced interpretations of government orders. While many manufacturers remained open as essential businesses, various orders still affected their operations in ways that may qualify for ERC.

The IRS is particularly focused on manufacturers who claimed ERC for supply chain disruptions or reduced customer orders—situations that may not qualify under the government order test.

list.title: Essential designation complexity
list.description: Most manufacturers were deemed essential, but essential status doesn't automatically disqualify ERC claims if specific operations were suspended

list.title: Supply chain disruption limits
list.description: The IRS has stated that supply chain issues from supplier government orders generally don't qualify—only orders directly affecting your operations count

list.title: Customer order cancellations
list.description: Lost orders because customers were shut down typically don't qualify, even if the customer's closure was government-ordered

list.title: Workforce restrictions
list.description: Social distancing requirements, capacity limits, and quarantine orders that affected production may support claims

sections.warning: Supply chain disruptions—even those caused by government orders affecting suppliers—generally do not qualify manufacturers for ERC. The IRS requires government orders to directly affect YOUR operations.

sections.heading: Government Orders That May Affect Manufacturers
sections.content: While manufacturers often continued operations, various government orders may have directly suspended or limited specific manufacturing activities. Document orders that specifically affected your facility.

list.title: Facility capacity restrictions
list.description: Orders limiting the number of workers in a facility or requiring specific square footage per worker

list.title: Workforce quarantine requirements
list.description: Mandatory quarantine periods for workers exposed to COVID-19 or returning from travel

list.title: Shift and scheduling limitations
list.description: Orders restricting operating hours, requiring shift separations, or limiting workforce size

list.title: Product category restrictions
list.description: Orders prohibiting production of non-essential goods while permitting essential manufacturing

list.title: Visitor and contractor restrictions
list.description: Bans on outside personnel that affected maintenance, installation, or inspection activities

list.title: On-site activity limitations
list.description: Restrictions on meetings, training, quality inspections, or other activities beyond direct production

sections.tip: Focus on orders that directly restricted activities at your facility—not orders affecting your suppliers or customers. The direct impact requirement is strictly interpreted.

sections.heading: Essential Manufacturers and Partial Suspension
sections.content: Manufacturers designated as essential can still qualify for ERC if government orders suspended specific aspects of their operations. The key is identifying particular functions or activities that were directly restricted.

list.title: Non-production functions
list.description: Even if production continued, government orders may have suspended R&D activities, sales functions, training programs, or administrative operations

list.title: Specific product lines
list.description: Orders may have restricted production of non-essential products while permitting essential manufacturing

list.title: Support operations
list.description: Cafeterias, on-site childcare, fitness centers, and other employee support functions may have been suspended

list.title: Customer-facing activities
list.description: Showrooms, demonstration facilities, and customer training operations may have been restricted

list.title: Quality and compliance functions
list.description: If third-party inspections, certifications, or audits were suspended, related activities may qualify

sections.warning: Claiming partial suspension for non-production functions requires demonstrating those functions represented more than nominal operations—generally more than 10% of business activity.

sections.heading: Documenting Manufacturing ERC Eligibility
sections.content: Manufacturing ERC claims require meticulous documentation connecting government orders to specific operational suspensions. Generic claims about pandemic impacts won't survive audit scrutiny.

list.title: Government orders with facility impact
list.description: Orders specifically affecting your facility, workforce, or production activities—with clear effective dates

list.title: Capacity and workforce records
list.description: Documentation showing normal workforce levels compared to government-restricted maximums

list.title: Production records
list.description: Data showing output before, during, and after restriction periods—broken down by product line if relevant

list.title: Employee scheduling documentation
list.description: Records showing shift modifications, quarantine-related absences, and workforce limitations

list.title: Internal communications
list.description: Memos, emails, and announcements explaining operational changes in response to government orders

list.title: Function-specific suspension records
list.description: Documentation of suspended training, cancelled sales activities, or restricted support operations

sections.tip: Manufacturing ERP systems contain valuable audit defense data. Export production reports, workforce scheduling, and capacity utilization data for claim periods.

sections.heading: Supply Chain Issues and ERC Eligibility
sections.content: The IRS has specifically addressed supply chain disruptions in ERC guidance. Understanding these rules helps manufacturers assess their positions and prepare for audit questions.

list.title: Supplier government orders don't qualify
list.description: If your supplier was shut down by a government order, that doesn't qualify YOU for ERC—the order must directly affect your operations

list.title: Material shortage exceptions
list.description: If a government order caused materials to be unavailable (not just from one supplier but generally), a stronger argument may exist

list.title: Customer closure effects
list.description: Customer shutdowns causing order cancellations don't qualify—the government order must restrict YOUR operations, not your market

list.title: Secondary effects generally fail
list.description: The IRS interprets the direct impact requirement narrowly; indirect or secondary effects of orders typically don't qualify

list.title: Documentation requirements
list.description: If you believe supply chain issues do qualify, document the specific government order, how it caused general unavailability, and how this directly prevented your operations

sections.warning: Many manufacturers claimed ERC based on supply chain disruptions following aggressive promoter advice. These claims face significant audit risk. Review your claim basis carefully and consider whether amended returns may be appropriate.

sections.heading: Defending Manufacturing ERC Claims
sections.content: When facing an IRS audit, manufacturers need clear documentation and persuasive arguments connecting government orders directly to operational suspensions. Work with counsel who understands both ERC law and manufacturing operations.

list.title: Focus on direct government orders
list.description: Lead with orders that directly restricted your facility, workforce, or production activities—not supply chain or customer issues

list.title: Quantify workforce restrictions
list.description: If capacity limits or social distancing requirements reduced your operational workforce, calculate and document the percentage impact

list.title: Document non-production suspensions
list.description: If claiming partial suspension for support functions, document those activities' significance to overall operations

list.title: Address essential status proactively
list.description: Acknowledge essential designation but explain which specific operations were still suspended by government orders

list.title: Prepare supply chain defense carefully
list.description: If your claim relies on supply chain issues, prepare detailed documentation and acknowledge the challenging legal position

sections.tip: If your ERC claim was based primarily on supply chain disruptions or customer cancellations, consult with an ERC audit attorney before responding to IRS inquiries. These claims face heightened scrutiny.

takeaways: Essential manufacturer designation doesn't disqualify ERC claims if specific operations were directly suspended by government orders
Supply chain disruptions from supplier government orders generally do NOT qualify manufacturers for ERC
Focus on government orders that directly affected your facility, workforce, or production activities
Document workforce restrictions, capacity limits, and specific function suspensions with quantified impact
Manufacturing ERC claims based on supply chain or customer issues face significant audit risk—review with counsel

relatedPages.title: When to Hire an ERC Audit Attorney

content.ctaText: Is Your Manufacturing Business Facing an ERC Audit? Get Expert Defense

faqs.question: Can essential manufacturers claim ERC?
faqs.answer: Yes, manufacturers deemed essential can still qualify for ERC if government orders suspended specific aspects of their operations. Capacity limits, workforce restrictions, quarantine requirements, or suspension of non-production functions (R&D, training, sales) may support partial suspension claims even if production continued.

faqs.question: Do supply chain disruptions qualify manufacturers for ERC?
faqs.answer: Generally no. The IRS has stated that supply chain disruptions from government orders affecting suppliers don't qualify manufacturers for ERC. The government order must directly affect YOUR operations. Claims based primarily on supply chain issues face significant audit risk.

faqs.question: What if our customers cancelled orders because they were shut down?
faqs.answer: Customer closures causing order cancellations typically don't qualify manufacturers for ERC. The government order must suspend YOUR operations, not your customers' operations. Reduced demand due to customer shutdowns is distinguishable from government orders directly restricting your activities.

faqs.question: How should manufacturers calculate partial suspension impact?
faqs.answer: Calculate the percentage of normal operations that were suspended by government orders. This could be measured by workforce capacity restrictions, production output limitations, or the portion of business activities (including non-production functions) that were suspended. Document your methodology clearly.

pages.title: ERC for Auto Dealers | Audit Defense Guide
pages.description: Auto dealers face unique ERC audit challenges including showroom closures and service department distinctions. Learn how to defend your dealership's ERC claim.
pages.h1: ERC Audit Defense for Auto Dealers

content.intro: Auto dealerships experienced a unique combination of COVID-19 restrictions. While service departments often remained open as essential businesses, showrooms and sales operations faced closures or severe limitations. This split created complex ERC eligibility questions that the IRS is now scrutinizing. Whether you operate a franchised dealership, independent used car lot, or specialty vehicle retailer, this guide explains how to defend your ERC claim during an audit.

sections.heading: The Auto Dealer ERC Complexity
sections.content: Auto dealerships present unique ERC challenges because they typically operate multiple distinct business functions under one roof. Sales showrooms, service departments, parts operations, body shops, and finance offices each faced different government restrictions—and may require separate eligibility analysis.

list.title: Showroom vs. service distinctions
list.description: Many jurisdictions closed showrooms while permitting service operations to continue—creating partial suspension scenarios

list.title: Essential service classification
list.description: Vehicle repair was often deemed essential, but vehicle sales may not have been—orders varied by jurisdiction

list.title: Test drive and delivery restrictions
list.description: Even when sales were technically permitted, restrictions on customer interactions affected how business could be conducted

list.title: Multi-department wage allocation
list.description: Dealerships must properly allocate wages between suspended and operating departments for accurate ERC calculations

sections.warning: Claiming ERC for your entire dealership when only the showroom was closed overstates your claim. Proper department-level analysis and wage allocation is essential.

sections.heading: Government Orders Affecting Auto Dealers
sections.content: Dealerships were subject to various orders that affected different aspects of their operations. Document all orders applicable to your business, including those that may have permitted some activities while restricting others.

list.title: Showroom closure orders
list.description: Many states closed auto showrooms as non-essential retail while permitting other dealership operations

list.title: By-appointment-only requirements
list.description: Orders restricting walk-in customers and requiring scheduled appointments changed showroom operations

list.title: Test drive restrictions
list.description: Limitations on vehicle occupancy or test drive activities affected the sales process

list.title: Delivery and paperwork limitations
list.description: Restrictions on in-person financing discussions, title work, and vehicle delivery processes

list.title: Capacity limits
list.description: Percentage-based capacity restrictions affecting how many customers could be in showrooms or waiting areas

list.title: Parts counter restrictions
list.description: Retail parts sales to walk-in customers may have been restricted while wholesale operations continued

sections.tip: Auto industry associations and manufacturer bulletins often compiled COVID-19 orders affecting dealerships. Check resources from NADA, state dealer associations, and your manufacturer for order compilations.

sections.heading: Analyzing Suspension by Department
sections.content: Effective ERC analysis for dealerships requires examining each operational area separately. Different departments may have experienced different levels of suspension.

list.title: New vehicle sales
list.description: Showroom closures most directly affected new car sales—document when showrooms were closed vs. permitted to operate with restrictions

list.title: Used vehicle sales
list.description: Used car operations may have faced similar or different restrictions than new car sales—analyze separately if orders distinguished them

list.title: Service department
list.description: Typically operated as essential, but may have faced restrictions on waiting areas, customer interactions, or certain service types

list.title: Parts operations
list.description: Wholesale parts to repair shops often continued while retail parts counter sales may have been restricted

list.title: Body shop
list.description: Collision repair typically continued but may have faced restrictions on customer vehicle pickup/dropoff processes

list.title: Finance and insurance
list.description: F&I operations depend on vehicle sales—analyze whether restrictions on sales meetings affected these operations

sections.warning: If your service department operated normally throughout the pandemic, wages for service technicians, advisors, and support staff likely don't qualify for ERC—even if showroom staff wages do.

sections.heading: Documentation Auto Dealers Should Prepare
sections.content: Dealerships have robust record-keeping systems that can support ERC audit defense. Organize the following documentation to demonstrate eligibility and proper wage calculations.

list.title: Government orders affecting dealerships
list.description: All orders specifically addressing auto sales, vehicle repair, or retail operations in your jurisdiction

list.title: DMS transaction data
list.description: Dealer management system reports showing sales, service, and parts transactions by date—demonstrating operational changes

list.title: Department-level financial statements
list.description: P&L statements breaking out gross profit and expenses by new sales, used sales, service, parts, and body shop

list.title: Employee department assignments
list.description: Payroll records showing which employees worked in which departments during claim periods

list.title: Showroom traffic and appointment data
list.description: CRM records showing customer visits, appointments, and sales activity during restriction periods

list.title: Manufacturer communications
list.description: Bulletins from OEMs regarding dealership operations, delivery processes, and COVID protocols

list.title: Operational change documentation
list.description: Internal memos, employee communications, and policy documents showing how operations changed

sections.tip: Dealer management systems like CDK, Reynolds & Reynolds, and Dealertrack contain detailed transaction-level data. Export comprehensive reports for claim periods showing activity by department.

sections.heading: Wage Allocation for Dealerships
sections.content: Proper wage allocation between suspended and operating departments is critical for dealership ERC claims. The IRS will scrutinize whether wages claimed actually correspond to suspended operations.

list.title: Sales staff allocation
list.description: Salespeople primarily working the showroom likely qualify during showroom closure periods; those who transitioned to online/phone sales require analysis

list.title: Service staff typically excluded
list.description: Service advisors, technicians, and support staff working in operating service departments generally don't qualify

list.title: Parts staff analysis
list.description: Parts staff serving continuing wholesale operations don't qualify; those working suspended retail counters may qualify

list.title: Administrative staff proration
list.description: Administrative employees supporting multiple departments require proportional allocation based on time or function

list.title: Management considerations
list.description: General managers and multi-department supervisors require allocation analysis based on their responsibilities

list.title: Detail and delivery staff
list.description: If vehicle delivery processes were suspended, staff dedicated to those functions may qualify

sections.warning: Don't claim ERC for your entire payroll if only sales operations were suspended. The IRS specifically looks for over-claiming when some business operations continued normally.

sections.heading: Defending Your Dealership's ERC Claim
sections.content: When facing an IRS audit, auto dealers need to present a clear picture of which operations were suspended and how wages were allocated. Work with counsel experienced in both ERC and dealership operations.

list.title: Department-by-department analysis
list.description: Present your claim with clear separation of suspended sales operations from continuing service/parts operations

list.title: Transaction data evidence
list.description: Use DMS data to demonstrate dramatic reduction in sales activity during suspension periods while service continued

list.title: Wage allocation methodology
list.description: Document exactly how you determined which wages qualified—by department, by employee, or by proportional allocation

list.title: Address hybrid arrangements
list.description: If sales staff performed some activities during closures (online leads, phone sales), explain how wages were allocated

list.title: Manufacturer and industry support
list.description: Reference industry guidance, manufacturer bulletins, and dealer association resources supporting your eligibility position

sections.tip: Dealership fixed operations (service, parts, body shop) often represent significant portions of total gross profit. Don't claim ERC based on total dealership revenue decline if fixed operations remained strong.

takeaways: Auto dealers must analyze ERC eligibility separately for sales, service, parts, and other departments
Showroom closures may support ERC for sales staff while service department wages typically don't qualify
Proper wage allocation between suspended and operating departments is critical for audit defense
DMS transaction data provides powerful evidence of operational changes during restriction periods
Work with counsel who understands both ERC rules and dealership operations for optimal defense

relatedPages.title: When to Hire an ERC Audit Attorney

content.ctaText: Is Your Dealership Facing an ERC Audit? Get Expert Defense Help

faqs.question: Can auto dealers claim ERC if the service department stayed open?
faqs.answer: Yes, dealers can claim ERC for suspended sales operations even if service continued. However, only wages allocable to suspended operations qualify. Service department employees generally don't qualify if that department operated normally. Proper department-level wage allocation is essential.

faqs.question: How should dealerships allocate wages between sales and service?
faqs.answer: Allocate based on employee department assignments and actual duties. Sales staff assigned exclusively to suspended showroom operations may fully qualify. Employees working across departments (like general managers) require proportional allocation. Document your methodology clearly.

faqs.question: Do online or phone vehicle sales affect ERC eligibility?
faqs.answer: If your showroom was closed but you conducted sales via phone or online, analyze whether this represents continuation of sales operations in modified form or a fundamentally different activity. Wages for employees conducting remote sales may or may not qualify depending on how you characterize the operational change.

faqs.question: Should multi-location dealer groups aggregate for ERC?
faqs.answer: Dealership groups under common ownership typically must aggregate employees across all locations for the 100/500 employee thresholds. However, eligibility analysis is still done location-by-location based on orders affecting each specific dealership. Consult with counsel on your specific corporate structure.

pages.title: ERC for Salons & Spas | Audit Defense Guide
pages.description: Salons, spas, and personal service businesses face ERC audit scrutiny on closure dates and reopening phases. Learn how to defend your salon or spa ERC claim.
pages.h1: ERC Audit Defense for Salons & Spas

content.intro: Salons, spas, and personal service businesses experienced among the clearest government-ordered closures during COVID-19. Personal services requiring close contact were explicitly shut down by government orders in most jurisdictions, often for extended periods. Despite this clear suspension, the IRS is scrutinizing ERC claims from this industry—particularly questioning claim periods, partial suspension calculations during phased reopening, and wage allocations. This guide helps salon and spa owners defend their ERC claims during an audit.

sections.heading: Clear Closure Orders for Personal Services
sections.content:
Unlike some industries where ERC eligibility requires nuanced analysis, salons and spas generally faced explicit government orders suspending their operations. Personal services requiring close contact were specifically identified and prohibited during lockdown periods.

However, the IRS still requires proper documentation and is examining whether claimed periods accurately match actual government orders—particularly during phased reopening when different services may have resumed at different times.

list.title: Explicit closure orders
list.description: Most states specifically named hair salons, nail salons, spas, and personal care services in closure orders—document these specific citations

list.title: Extended closure periods
list.description: Personal services often remained closed longer than other retail, creating extended but well-defined claim periods

list.title: Phased reopening complexity
list.description: Many jurisdictions reopened different services at different times—hair before nails, massage last—requiring careful period tracking

list.title: Capacity and service restrictions
list.description: Even after reopening, limitations on services, capacity, and operations may support continued partial suspension claims

sections.tip: Your state cosmetology or barber board likely documented COVID closure orders affecting licensed services. These regulatory board communications provide authoritative documentation of closure periods.

sections.heading: Government Orders Affecting Salons and Spas
sections.content: Document every government order that affected your specific services. Different services within a salon or spa may have faced different restrictions at different times.

list.title: Complete closure orders
list.description: Initial lockdowns typically closed all personal service businesses—document the specific order and effective dates

list.title: Service-specific reopening
list.description: Some jurisdictions permitted hair services before nail services, or prohibited certain treatments (facials, beard trims) longer than others

list.title: Capacity percentage limits
list.description: Reopening often came with 25-50% capacity restrictions limiting how many clients could be served

list.title: Station spacing requirements
list.description: Six-foot spacing between stations effectively reduced capacity beyond stated percentage limits

list.title: Treatment-specific restrictions
list.description: Services requiring face-to-face contact (facials, brow services) or mask removal faced extended prohibition

list.title: Waiting area closures
list.description: Requirements eliminating waiting areas changed scheduling and client flow

list.title: Sanitation time requirements
list.description: Mandated cleaning time between clients reduced appointment capacity

sections.warning: Different services within your business may have had different restriction timelines. A spa offering massage, facials, and nail services likely saw each service resume at different dates—track them separately.

sections.heading: Documenting Suspension for Different Service Types
sections.content: Salons and spas offering multiple services must document suspension periods for each service category, as government orders often distinguished between service types.

list.title: Hair services
list.description: Cutting, coloring, styling, and barbering typically reopened earlier than other services—document specific reopening date

list.title: Nail services
list.description: Manicures and pedicures often had separate reopening dates from hair services—track these distinctions

list.title: Esthetic services
list.description: Facials, waxing, brow services, and skin treatments requiring close face contact faced extended closure

list.title: Massage therapy
list.description: Massage services often were among the last to reopen—document licensing board communications about reopening

list.title: Spa amenities
list.description: Saunas, steam rooms, pools, and locker facilities faced separate restrictions from treatment services

list.title: Retail operations
list.description: Product sales may have been permitted (curbside or limited) while services were suspended

sections.tip: Create a calendar showing when each service type could legally resume in your jurisdiction. This service-by-service timeline is essential for accurate ERC calculations.

sections.heading: Evidence Salons and Spas Should Prepare
sections.content: Salon and spa ERC audits require documentation demonstrating when your specific services were suspended by government order. Organize comprehensive evidence supporting your claim periods and wage calculations.

list.title: Government orders with service citations
list.description: Orders specifically naming personal care services, salons, spas, or cosmetology services with effective dates

list.title: State board communications
list.description: Guidance from cosmetology, barber, massage, or esthetics licensing boards regarding closure and reopening

list.title: Appointment system records
list.description: Scheduling software data showing appointment volume before, during, and after closures

list.title: Service category revenue
list.description: Financial records broken down by service type (hair, nails, spa, massage) showing when each resumed

list.title: Employee schedules and certifications
list.description: Records showing which employees provided which services and their corresponding suspension periods

list.title: Client communications
list.description: Emails, texts, or social posts announcing closures, reopenings, and service availability

list.title: Sanitation protocol documentation
list.description: Records of implemented safety protocols that affected capacity and scheduling

sections.tip: Salon scheduling software like Vagaro, Mindbody, or Square Appointments contains detailed appointment data. Export reports showing booking volume by service type across claim periods.

sections.heading: Wage Allocation for Multi-Service Businesses
sections.content: Spas and full-service salons offering multiple service categories must allocate wages properly between services with different suspension periods. This allocation affects your ERC calculation.

list.title: Service provider allocation
list.description: Stylists, nail technicians, estheticians, and massage therapists should be allocated to their specific service categories

list.title: Support staff proration
list.description: Receptionists, cleaning staff, and management supporting multiple service areas require proportional allocation

list.title: Continuing operations wages
list.description: If retail sales or some services continued, wages supporting those operations may not qualify

list.title: Cross-trained employees
list.description: Staff licensed for multiple services (hair and nails, for example) require analysis of which services they provided during partial reopening

list.title: Commission and hourly structures
list.description: Whether employees earned hourly wages or commissions may affect how to calculate qualified wages during partial operation

sections.warning: If you claimed ERC for all employees during periods when some services were operating, you may have over-claimed. Proper wage allocation is essential for audit defense.

sections.heading: Defending Your Salon or Spa ERC Claim
sections.content: Salon and spa ERC claims benefit from clear government order documentation and straightforward suspension analysis. Present your case with organized evidence and precise timeline documentation.

list.title: Lead with clear closure orders
list.description: Personal service closures were explicit—cite the specific orders naming your service type

list.title: Present service-by-service timeline
list.description: Show exactly when each service category was suspended and when it resumed

list.title: Demonstrate wage allocation methodology
list.description: Explain how you allocated wages between suspended and operating services during partial reopening

list.title: Quantify capacity restrictions
list.description: Calculate how station spacing and sanitation requirements reduced capacity even after reopening

list.title: Address any retail or product sales
list.description: If product sales continued, explain how you treated wages for employees handling retail during service closure

sections.tip: If your ERC claim period extends beyond the actual service closure period, review your documentation. Claiming ERC after services fully resumed exposes you to audit challenges.

takeaways: Salons and spas generally had clear government orders explicitly suspending personal services—document these specific citations
Different services (hair, nails, massage, facials) often had different suspension and reopening dates requiring separate tracking
Wage allocation between suspended and operating services is critical for multi-service businesses
Scheduling software provides valuable evidence of service suspension and capacity restrictions
Phased reopening creates partial suspension scenarios requiring careful period-by-period analysis

relatedPages.title: When to Hire an ERC Audit Attorney

content.ctaText: Is Your Salon or Spa Facing an ERC Audit? Get Expert Defense Help

faqs.question: Can salons claim ERC for the entire closure period?
faqs.answer: Salons can claim ERC for periods when government orders suspended their operations. The claim period should match the actual closure dates in your jurisdiction. If you reopened before the general restriction lifted (following local variance, for example), your claim period should reflect your actual suspension dates.

faqs.question: How should spas handle different reopening dates for different services?
faqs.answer: Spas should track reopening dates separately for each service category (massage, facials, nails, etc.) and allocate wages accordingly. Employees providing services that remained suspended qualify longer than those whose services resumed earlier. Document the specific orders governing each service type.

faqs.question: Do capacity restrictions after reopening support continued ERC claims?
faqs.answer: Capacity restrictions may support partial suspension claims if they reduced operations by more than a nominal amount (generally more than 10%). Calculate your normal capacity versus restricted capacity, factoring in station spacing and sanitation time requirements. Document how restrictions limited your operational capacity.

faqs.question: What if my salon sold products during the service closure?
faqs.answer: Product sales during service closure don't disqualify your ERC claim, but wages for employees conducting retail sales during this period may not qualify. If your retail operations continued while services were suspended, allocate wages appropriately between qualifying and non-qualifying work.

pages.title: ERC for Daycare & Childcare | Audit Defense Guide
pages.description: Daycare and childcare centers face unique ERC audit challenges including capacity restrictions and essential worker exceptions. Learn how to defend your childcare ERC claim.
pages.h1: ERC Audit Defense for Daycare & Childcare Centers

content.intro: Daycare centers and childcare providers faced complex and evolving COVID-19 restrictions. While some facilities closed completely, others were designated essential to serve healthcare workers and first responders—but often with dramatically reduced capacity and strict group size limits. The IRS is examining childcare ERC claims closely, questioning whether facilities that remained partially open experienced genuine suspension of operations. This guide helps daycare and childcare providers understand and defend their ERC claims during an audit.

sections.heading: The Childcare ERC Challenge
sections.content: Childcare providers face unique ERC audit scrutiny because many facilities operated throughout the pandemic under essential worker exemptions. However, severe capacity restrictions, group size limits, and enrollment drops often meant these facilities operated far below normal capacity—potentially qualifying for partial suspension claims.

list.title: Essential designation complexity
list.description: Many daycares remained open for essential workers' children, but with dramatically restricted capacity and operations

list.title: Capacity and ratio restrictions
list.description: Group size limits, classroom ratios, and overall capacity restrictions fundamentally changed how facilities could operate

list.title: Enrollment vs. capacity issues
list.description: The IRS distinguishes between government-imposed capacity limits and voluntary enrollment declines from parents keeping children home

list.title: State licensing variations
list.description: Childcare licensing requirements and COVID modifications varied significantly by state, affecting eligibility analysis

sections.warning: Reduced enrollment because parents chose to keep children home does NOT qualify daycares for ERC. You must demonstrate government orders restricted your operations—not parent decisions.

sections.heading: Government Orders Affecting Childcare Providers
sections.content: Childcare facilities were subject to various government orders that modified their operations. Document all orders from state licensing agencies, health departments, and local governments that affected your specific operation.

list.title: Complete closure orders
list.description: Some jurisdictions completely closed non-essential childcare during initial lockdowns—document if this applied to you

list.title: Essential-only operation requirements
list.description: Orders limiting enrollment to children of essential workers, healthcare providers, or first responders

list.title: Group size reductions
list.description: Licensing modifications reducing classroom group sizes from, for example, 20 children to 10 children

list.title: Ratio modifications
list.description: Changes to adult-to-child ratios that reduced operational capacity

list.title: Classroom cohort requirements
list.description: Rules prohibiting mixing of groups, eliminating combined activities, or requiring separate outdoor times

list.title: Facility capacity limits
list.description: Overall facility enrollment caps reduced below licensed capacity

list.title: Program suspensions
list.description: Orders prohibiting before/after school programs, summer camps, or drop-in care

sections.tip: State childcare licensing agencies issued emergency regulations and waivers that significantly affected operations. Request copies of all emergency orders from your licensing agency.

sections.heading: Documenting Capacity Restrictions
sections.content: For daycares that remained open with restrictions, demonstrating ERC eligibility requires documenting how government orders reduced your operational capacity—not just your actual enrollment.

list.title: Licensed capacity vs. allowed capacity
list.description: Document your normal licensed capacity and the government-imposed reduced capacity during different periods

list.title: Classroom-by-classroom analysis
list.description: Show how group size restrictions affected each classroom's capacity and your total operational capability

list.title: Program-specific suspensions
list.description: If before/after school programs, summer camps, or drop-in care were suspended, document these separately

list.title: Staffing ratio impacts
list.description: Changed ratios may have limited enrollment even if facility capacity wasn't directly restricted

list.title: Cohort restrictions
list.description: Requirements preventing mixing of groups may have prevented efficient use of staff and space

sections.warning: Focus on government-imposed capacity limits, not enrollment declines from parent choices. If your facility could have served 100 children but government orders limited you to 50, that's suspension. If parents simply didn't enroll, that's not.

sections.heading: Evidence Childcare Providers Should Prepare
sections.content: Daycare ERC audits require documentation demonstrating government orders restricted operations. Compile evidence showing capacity limits, program suspensions, and operational changes mandated by government orders.

list.title: Licensing agency orders
list.description: All emergency regulations, capacity modifications, and operational requirements from state licensing

list.title: Health department directives
list.description: Local and county health department orders affecting childcare operations

list.title: Licensed capacity documentation
list.description: Your normal licensing showing room-by-room and total facility capacity

list.title: Enrollment records
list.description: Enrollment data showing capacity before restrictions and during restriction periods

list.title: Classroom assignment records
list.description: Documentation of group assignments, cohort separations, and capacity by room

list.title: Staffing schedules
list.description: Employee schedules showing which staff worked with which groups during restrictions

list.title: Parent communications
list.description: Notices to parents about capacity restrictions, program changes, and enrollment limitations

list.title: Program cancellation records
list.description: Documentation of suspended before/after school programs, summer camps, or other activities

sections.tip: Childcare management software contains enrollment and classroom data. Export detailed reports showing capacity utilization, enrollment by classroom, and program attendance across claim periods.

sections.heading: Essential Worker Exceptions and ERC
sections.content: Many daycares operated throughout the pandemic serving essential workers' children. This essential operation doesn't disqualify ERC claims if government orders still restricted your capacity or suspended specific programs.

list.title: Capacity-limited essential operation
list.description: Operating for essential workers at 50% capacity still represents partial suspension of your normal 100% operation

list.title: Program suspensions during essential operation
list.description: Even while providing essential childcare, after-school programs, camps, or other activities may have been suspended

list.title: Enrollment restrictions
list.description: If you could only enroll essential workers' children, that government restriction limited your normal enrollment pool

list.title: Wage allocation for partial operation
list.description: Allocate wages between continuing essential operations and suspended normal operations

sections.warning: If you operated at full capacity serving essential workers, you likely don't have a suspension claim—even if your normal client base was different. The question is whether government orders restricted your operations.

sections.heading: Defending Your Childcare ERC Claim
sections.content: Childcare providers facing ERC audits should present clear documentation connecting government orders to operational restrictions. Work with counsel who understands both ERC rules and childcare licensing regulations.

list.title: Quantify capacity reductions
list.description: Calculate the percentage reduction in your operational capacity due to government-imposed limits vs. your normal licensed capacity

list.title: Separate program suspension claims
list.description: If specific programs (camps, after-school, drop-in) were suspended while core childcare continued, document these separately

list.title: Address essential operation clearly
list.description: Acknowledge if you operated as essential but explain how capacity restrictions still partially suspended normal operations

list.title: Distinguish enrollment from capacity
list.description: Clarify that you're claiming suspension based on government capacity limits, not voluntary enrollment declines

list.title: Document licensing agency requirements
list.description: Show that restrictions came from licensing requirements, not voluntary operational choices

sections.tip: Childcare ERC claims are stronger when based on documented licensing capacity reductions rather than enrollment declines. Focus your evidence on government-imposed operational limits.

takeaways: Childcare ERC eligibility depends on government-ordered capacity restrictions—not enrollment declines from parent choices
Daycares operating for essential workers may still claim partial suspension if capacity was government-limited
Document licensing agency capacity modifications, group size restrictions, and program suspensions
Separate analysis may be needed for different programs (core childcare vs. camps vs. after-school)
Focus evidence on government-imposed operational limits rather than voluntary enrollment changes

relatedPages.title: When to Hire an ERC Audit Attorney

content.ctaText: Is Your Childcare Center Facing an ERC Audit? Get Expert Defense Help

faqs.question: Can daycares that stayed open as essential claim ERC?
faqs.answer: Yes, daycares that operated as essential can still claim ERC if government orders restricted their capacity below normal levels. If you were limited to 50% capacity serving essential workers, that 50% reduction may qualify as partial suspension. Document the specific licensing modifications that restricted your operations.

faqs.question: Does reduced enrollment qualify daycare for ERC?
faqs.answer: Reduced enrollment alone does not qualify daycares for ERC. The credit requires government orders that suspended or limited operations. If parents chose to keep children home but you could have served them, that's not suspension. However, if government orders limited your capacity and that's why enrollment dropped, the capacity limit—not the enrollment decline—supports your claim.

faqs.question: How should daycares calculate partial suspension?
faqs.answer: Calculate partial suspension based on government-imposed capacity reductions. Compare your normal licensed capacity to the maximum you were permitted to serve under emergency orders. If your license allowed 100 children and emergency orders limited you to 50, you may have experienced 50% suspension. Document the specific orders creating these limits.

faqs.question: What about summer camp and after-school program suspensions?
faqs.answer: Suspended summer camps, after-school programs, and other activities may support ERC claims even if core daycare operations continued. Document the specific orders that prohibited these programs and allocate wages appropriately between continuing core operations and suspended supplementary programs.

pages.title: ERC Gross Receipts Test: Understanding the 50% and 20% Decline Requirements
pages.description: Learn the ERC gross receipts test requirements: 50% decline for 2020, 20% decline for 2021. Calculate eligibility and avoid audit red flags.
pages.h1: ERC Gross Receipts Test: The 50% and 20% Decline Requirements Explained

content.intro: The gross receipts test is one of two primary pathways to ERC eligibility. To qualify under this test, your business must demonstrate a significant decline in quarterly gross receipts compared to the same quarter in 2019. The thresholds differ by year: 50% decline for 2020 quarters and 20% decline for 2021 quarters. Understanding exactly how to calculate and document this test is critical for surviving an IRS audit.

sections.heading: Understanding the Gross Receipts Test Thresholds
sections.content: The gross receipts test provides an objective, numbers-based path to ERC eligibility. Unlike the government order test, which can be subjective, the gross receipts test relies on your financial records:

list.title: 2020 Quarters (50% Test)
list.description: Your gross receipts must be less than 50% of gross receipts for the same quarter in 2019. You remain eligible until the quarter following the quarter where receipts exceed 80% of 2019 levels.

list.title: 2021 Quarters (20% Test)
list.description: Your gross receipts must be less than 80% of the same quarter in 2019 (i.e., a decline of more than 20%). This lower threshold made more businesses eligible in 2021.

list.title: Alternative Prior Quarter Election
list.description: For 2021, you can alternatively compare to the immediately preceding quarter instead of 2019, which may be beneficial for some businesses.

list.title: New Businesses
list.description: If you weren't in business for all of 2019, special rules apply for establishing your comparison baseline.

sections.heading: What Counts as Gross Receipts?
sections.content: The IRS follows IRC Section 448(c) for defining gross receipts, which includes more than just sales revenue:

list.title: Total sales
list.description: All revenue from goods and services, net of returns and allowances.

list.title: Investment income
list.description: Interest, dividends, royalties, rents, and capital gains (but not gross receipts from sales of capital assets).

list.title: PPP loan proceeds
list.description: Critically, forgiven PPP loans are NOT included in gross receipts for ERC purposes.

list.title: EIDL advances
list.description: Economic Injury Disaster Loan advances should not be included in gross receipts.

list.title: Grants and subsidies
list.description: Most government grants received during COVID should be included unless specifically excluded.

sections.tip: Ensure consistency in how you calculate gross receipts across all comparison quarters. The IRS will scrutinize any calculation methodology changes.

sections.heading: How to Calculate the Decline Correctly
sections.content: Accurate calculation is essential. The IRS has denied many claims due to calculation errors:

list.title: Use accrual or cash method consistently
list.description: You must use the same accounting method you used for your tax returns. Don't switch between accrual and cash basis.

list.title: Compare full quarters
list.description: You cannot cherry-pick months within a quarter. The entire quarter must be compared.

list.title: Include all entities
list.description: For controlled groups and affiliated service groups, you must aggregate gross receipts of all members.

list.title: Document your sources
list.description: Be prepared to show exactly where your numbers came from—tax returns, financial statements, or accounting records.

sections.warning: If your numbers are close to the threshold (within 5%), expect intense IRS scrutiny. Document every line item meticulously.

sections.heading: Documentation Requirements for Audit Defense
sections.content: When the IRS audits your ERC claim under the gross receipts test, they will request extensive documentation:

list.title: Quarterly financial statements
list.description: Profit and loss statements for each quarter of 2019, 2020, and 2021 showing gross receipts calculations.

list.title: Tax returns
list.description: Form 941s, annual income tax returns, and any amended returns for the relevant periods.

list.title: General ledger detail
list.description: Supporting detail for every revenue account included in your gross receipts calculation.

list.title: Accounting method documentation
list.description: Evidence that you used consistent accounting methods across all comparison periods.

list.title: Workpapers showing calculations
list.description: Clear, organized calculations demonstrating how you arrived at your eligibility determination.

sections.tip: Create a reconciliation that ties your gross receipts calculation directly to your tax return revenue figures. This makes the IRS examiner's job easier and increases credibility.

sections.heading: Common Gross Receipts Test Mistakes
sections.content: These errors frequently lead to audit problems and claim denials:

list.title: Including PPP forgiveness
list.description: Forgiven PPP loans should NOT be counted as gross receipts. Including them can disqualify you or reduce your decline percentage.

list.title: Inconsistent accounting methods
list.description: Switching from cash to accrual (or vice versa) between comparison periods creates audit red flags.

list.title: Ignoring aggregation rules
list.description: Related entities must combine their gross receipts under controlled group rules. Claiming separately for each entity is improper.

list.title: Using wrong comparison quarters
list.description: Comparing Q2 2020 to Q1 2019 instead of Q2 2019 is a fundamental error that will result in denial.

list.title: Calculation arithmetic errors
list.description: Simple math mistakes happen more often than you'd think and are easily caught by IRS examiners.

takeaways: 2020 requires a 50% gross receipts decline; 2021 requires only a 20% decline compared to 2019
PPP loan forgiveness does NOT count as gross receipts for ERC calculations
You must use the same accounting method (cash or accrual) across all comparison periods
Related entities under common control must aggregate their gross receipts
Keep detailed documentation tying your calculations directly to tax returns and financial statements
content.ctaText: Need help documenting your gross receipts decline for an IRS audit? Get expert assistance preparing your response.

faqs.question: What is the ERC gross receipts test?
faqs.answer: The gross receipts test is one of two ways to qualify for the Employee Retention Credit. For 2020, your quarterly gross receipts must be less than 50% of the same quarter in 2019. For 2021, they must be less than 80% of the same quarter in 2019 (a 20%+ decline).

faqs.question: Does PPP loan forgiveness count as gross receipts for ERC?
faqs.answer: No. Forgiven PPP loans are specifically excluded from the gross receipts calculation for ERC eligibility purposes. This is a common mistake that can incorrectly disqualify businesses or reduce their apparent decline percentage.

faqs.question: What if my business started in 2019 or 2020?
faqs.answer: If you weren't in business for all of 2019, special rules apply. Generally, you use the quarters you were in business during 2019, or if you started in 2020, you may compare to your first full quarter of operations.

faqs.question: Can I use the gross receipts test even if I also had a government order impact?
faqs.answer: Yes. You can qualify through either test. Many businesses document both pathways to strengthen their position in case the IRS challenges one basis for eligibility.

pages.title: ERC Government Order Test: Proving Suspension of Operations
pages.description: Understand the ERC government order test. Learn what qualifies as a suspension of operations and how to document eligibility for IRS audit defense.
pages.h1: ERC Government Order Test: How to Prove Your Operations Were Suspended

content.intro: The government order test is one of two primary pathways to ERC eligibility, and it's also the most frequently disputed. To qualify, your business operations must have been fully or partially suspended due to a governmental order related to COVID-19. The IRS has taken an increasingly narrow view of what constitutes a qualifying suspension, making proper documentation essential for audit defense.

sections.heading: What is a Qualifying Government Order?
sections.content: Not every government action during COVID-19 creates ERC eligibility. A qualifying government order must meet specific criteria:

list.title: Issued by a government authority
list.description: The order must come from a federal, state, or local governmental authority—not just recommendations from health organizations or industry associations.

list.title: Related to COVID-19
list.description: The order must have been issued specifically in response to the COVID-19 pandemic.

list.title: Limited commerce, travel, or group meetings
list.description: The order must have placed restrictions on how businesses could operate, not just general public health guidance.

list.title: Applied to your business
list.description: The order must have specifically affected your type of business—general stay-at-home orders don't automatically qualify every business.

sections.tip: Keep copies of the actual government orders, including effective dates and any subsequent modifications or extensions. You'll need these for audit documentation.

sections.heading: Full vs. Partial Suspension
sections.content: The nature of your suspension affects both eligibility and documentation requirements:

list.title: Full suspension
list.description: Your business was completely prohibited from operating. This is relatively rare and typically applied to specific industries like indoor entertainment venues during complete shutdowns.

list.title: Partial suspension
list.description: A government order had more than a nominal effect on your business operations, even if you could still operate in some capacity. This is the more common scenario.

list.title: Nominal effect exclusion
list.description: If the order only had a small or trivial impact on your operations, you don't qualify. The IRS considers impacts under 10% of operations to be nominal.

list.title: Supplier/customer impacts
list.description: You may qualify if your suppliers or customers were subject to government orders that prevented them from providing goods or services to you, or from purchasing from you.

sections.heading: Documenting the Business Impact
sections.content: The IRS requires proof that the government order actually impacted your specific business operations. This is where many claims fail:

list.title: Copy of the government order
list.description: You must have the actual order, proclamation, or emergency declaration—not just news articles about it.

list.title: Connection to your business
list.description: Documentation showing why and how this specific order applied to your business type or location.

list.title: Operational impact evidence
list.description: Records showing what operations were suspended: reduced hours, capacity limits, service modifications, closed facilities.

list.title: Timeline documentation
list.description: Evidence of when the suspension began and ended, corresponding to the quarters you're claiming.

list.title: Employee and operational records
list.description: Schedules, communications, or policy changes showing how you modified operations in response to the order.

sections.warning: Simply stating that COVID-19 affected your business is not sufficient. You must show a direct connection between a specific government order and a specific operational suspension.

sections.heading: Common Government Order Test Failures
sections.content: These issues frequently lead to IRS denials of government order-based ERC claims:

list.title: No actual order identified
list.description: Claims that cite general pandemic conditions without pointing to a specific government mandate are denied.

list.title: Essential business without proof of partial suspension
list.description: If your business was classified as essential, you need additional evidence showing how you were still partially suspended.

list.title: Order didn't apply to your business type
list.description: A restaurant closure order doesn't create eligibility for an accounting firm, even if both were in the same city.

list.title: Voluntary closures
list.description: Choosing to close or reduce operations for safety reasons doesn't qualify—there must be a government mandate.

list.title: Indirect economic effects
list.description: Lost customers because people were staying home is not the same as being ordered to suspend operations.

sections.heading: Supply Chain Disruption as Suspension
sections.content: Some businesses qualify based on government orders that affected their suppliers, even if no order directly targeted their business:

list.title: Critical supplier suspension
list.description: If a government order suspended operations of a supplier you relied on for critical goods or materials, you may qualify.

list.title: No alternative suppliers available
list.description: You must show that you couldn't obtain the goods or services from other suppliers not affected by government orders.

list.title: More than nominal impact
list.description: The supply disruption must have affected more than 10% of your business operations.

list.title: Documentation requirements
list.description: You need evidence of your supplier relationship, the government order affecting them, and how it impacted your operations.

sections.tip: If relying on supply chain disruption, gather documentation from your affected suppliers confirming the government orders they were subject to and how it impacted their ability to serve you.

takeaways: A qualifying government order must be an actual mandate, not general health guidance or recommendations
You must document both the order AND how it specifically suspended your business operations
Essential businesses can still qualify but must prove partial suspension with additional evidence
Voluntary closures or economic hardship from reduced demand don't qualify under the government order test
Supply chain disruptions may qualify if a government order suspended a critical supplier
content.ctaText: Facing an IRS audit of your government order eligibility? Get expert help documenting your business impact.

faqs.question: What counts as a government order for ERC purposes?
faqs.answer: A qualifying government order is a mandate from a federal, state, or local authority related to COVID-19 that limited commerce, travel, or group meetings. It must be an actual order or proclamation, not guidance or recommendations from health organizations.

faqs.question: Can I qualify if my business was considered essential?
faqs.answer: Yes, but you must prove partial suspension. Essential businesses can qualify if government orders still limited their operations—such as capacity restrictions, modified service requirements, or prohibition of certain business activities even while the business remained open.

faqs.question: What if I closed voluntarily due to COVID-19 concerns?
faqs.answer: Voluntary closures do not qualify under the government order test. There must be a government mandate requiring the suspension. However, you may still qualify under the gross receipts test if your revenue declined sufficiently.

faqs.question: How do I prove the government order affected my specific business?
faqs.answer: Document the specific order, show how it applied to your business type or location, and provide evidence of operational changes you made in response. This can include reduced hours, capacity limits, service modifications, employee schedules, and internal communications about compliance.

pages.title: Can Essential Businesses Claim ERC? What You Need to Prove
pages.description: Were you an essential business during COVID-19? Learn how essential businesses can still qualify for ERC through partial suspension documentation.
pages.h1: Essential Business ERC Eligibility: How to Qualify and What to Document

content.intro: Being classified as an essential business during COVID-19 does not automatically disqualify you from the Employee Retention Credit. However, the IRS scrutinizes essential business claims more heavily because these businesses were allowed to continue operating. To qualify, you must prove that government orders still resulted in a partial suspension of your operations—and this requires specific, detailed documentation.

sections.heading: The Essential Business Exception Explained
sections.content: Many businesses assume that because they were deemed essential, they can't claim ERC. This is incorrect, but the qualification requirements are more stringent:

list.title: Essential designation doesn't equal exemption
list.description: Being classified as essential allowed you to remain open, but doesn't mean government orders had no effect on your operations.

list.title: Partial suspension still applies
list.description: If government orders forced you to modify how you operate—even while remaining open—you may have experienced a partial suspension.

list.title: The nominal effect threshold
