list.description: To qualify, the suspension must have affected more than a nominal portion of your business (generally considered more than 10% of operations).

list.title: Multiple orders may apply
list.description: Essential businesses were often subject to various restrictions: capacity limits, social distancing requirements, modified service protocols, and more.

sections.heading: How Essential Businesses Can Qualify
sections.content: Essential businesses typically qualify through partial suspension in these scenarios:

list.title: Capacity restrictions
list.description: If government orders limited how many customers could be in your facility, reducing your ability to serve customers at normal levels.

list.title: Modified service requirements
list.description: Requirements to change how you delivered services—curbside only, no indoor dining, appointment-only operations.

list.title: Suspended business segments
list.description: If your business had multiple functions and one was suspended (e.g., a grocery store with an in-store cafe that was closed).

list.title: Supplier suspensions
list.description: If your essential business couldn't operate normally because non-essential suppliers were suspended by government orders.

list.title: Customer-facing restrictions
list.description: Requirements that limited customer interactions: no fitting rooms, no product testing, reduced operating hours.

sections.tip: The key is demonstrating that while you were allowed to operate, you could not operate at full capacity or in your normal manner due to specific government orders.

sections.heading: Documenting Partial Suspension as an Essential Business
sections.content: Essential business claims require more robust documentation than non-essential business claims:

list.title: Government orders with restrictions
list.description: Copies of the specific orders that imposed operational restrictions, even while allowing you to remain open.

list.title: Before and after operational data
list.description: Evidence comparing your operations before COVID-19 restrictions to operations under restrictions: hours, capacity, services offered.

list.title: Revenue by service line
list.description: If specific business segments were suspended, financial records showing the proportion of business affected.

list.title: Compliance documentation
list.description: Internal policies, employee training materials, or customer communications showing how you modified operations to comply with orders.

list.title: Contemporaneous records
list.description: Records created at the time showing the impact—not documents created later for the ERC claim.

sections.warning: The IRS is especially skeptical of essential business claims. Generic assertions that COVID affected your business will not suffice. You need specific, quantified evidence of operational suspension.

sections.heading: Industries Under Heavy IRS Scrutiny
sections.content: Certain essential business categories face heightened audit risk because they were allowed to operate throughout the pandemic:

list.title: Healthcare providers
list.description: While some elective procedures were suspended, many healthcare businesses operated continuously. Partial suspension claims require showing specific services that were halted.

list.title: Grocery and food retail
list.description: Remained open but may qualify if in-store services (delis, bakeries, prepared foods) were suspended or capacity limits applied.

list.title: Manufacturing
list.description: Many manufacturers were essential. Qualification depends on whether supply chain disruptions or workforce restrictions affected operations.

list.title: Construction
list.description: Often deemed essential but may have faced project suspensions, inspection delays, or supply shortages due to government orders.

list.title: Financial services
list.description: Generally operated throughout COVID but may qualify if in-person services were suspended while branches remained open for limited functions.

sections.heading: What Won't Qualify as Partial Suspension
sections.content: Not every operational change creates ERC eligibility. The IRS specifically rejects these arguments:

list.title: Voluntary safety measures
list.description: Choosing to implement safety protocols beyond what was required by government orders doesn't count as a government-mandated suspension.

list.title: Reduced customer demand
list.description: Fewer customers due to fear of COVID-19 is not a government order suspending your operations.

list.title: Staffing challenges
list.description: Difficulty finding workers or employees calling in sick is not a government-ordered suspension.

list.title: Economic hardship generally
list.description: Business losses due to the pandemic economy don't qualify unless tied to a specific government order affecting operations.

list.title: Nominal operational changes
list.description: Changes affecting less than 10% of your business operations don't meet the partial suspension threshold.

takeaways: Essential businesses CAN qualify for ERC through partial suspension
You must prove government orders restricted your operations, not just that you were affected by COVID generally
The partial suspension must affect more than a nominal portion (10%+) of your business
Document specific operational changes: capacity limits, service modifications, suspended business segments
Voluntary safety measures and reduced customer demand do NOT create eligibility
content.ctaText: Essential business facing an ERC audit? Get expert help documenting your partial suspension and protecting your claim.

faqs.question: Can essential businesses claim the Employee Retention Credit?
faqs.answer: Yes. Essential businesses can claim ERC if they experienced a partial suspension of operations due to government orders. Being allowed to remain open doesn't mean you weren't subject to operational restrictions that created eligibility.

faqs.question: What counts as partial suspension for essential businesses?
faqs.answer: Partial suspension for essential businesses can include capacity restrictions, modified service requirements (like curbside only), suspended business segments (like a dine-in section of a grocery store), or inability to operate normally due to supplier suspensions.

faqs.question: Why are essential business ERC claims audited more often?
faqs.answer: The IRS scrutinizes essential business claims because these businesses were allowed to operate during COVID-19. The IRS wants to ensure there was actually a government-ordered suspension of operations, not just general COVID-related challenges that affected all businesses.

faqs.question: What documentation do essential businesses need for ERC audits?
faqs.answer: Essential businesses need copies of government orders imposing restrictions, evidence of operational changes (before and after comparisons), revenue breakdowns if specific segments were suspended, and contemporaneous records showing compliance with restrictions.

pages.title: Recovery Startup Business ERC: Special Qualification Requirements
pages.description: Learn the Recovery Startup Business (RSB) ERC requirements. Started after Feb 15, 2020? Special eligibility rules and $50K/quarter limits apply.
pages.h1: Recovery Startup Business ERC: Qualification Rules and Documentation

content.intro: Recovery Startup Businesses (RSBs) represent a special category of ERC eligibility designed for businesses that began operations during the pandemic. If your business started after February 15, 2020, you may qualify for up to $50,000 in ERC per quarter—even without proving a suspension of operations or decline in gross receipts. However, RSB claims face intense IRS scrutiny and have specific documentation requirements.

sections.heading: What is a Recovery Startup Business?
sections.content: A Recovery Startup Business is defined under the American Rescue Plan Act with specific criteria:

list.title: Started after February 15, 2020
list.description: Your business must have begun carrying on a trade or business after February 15, 2020. This is the key date that distinguishes RSBs from other employers.

list.title: Average annual gross receipts limit
list.description: Your average annual gross receipts for the three-year period ending in the year prior to the calendar quarter cannot exceed $1 million. For newer businesses, a shorter period is used.

list.title: Not otherwise eligible
list.description: You cannot also qualify under the government order suspension test or the gross receipts decline test for the same quarter. RSB is a separate eligibility pathway.

list.title: Only for Q3 and Q4 2021
list.description: RSB eligibility only applies to the third and fourth quarters of 2021. There is no RSB category for 2020 quarters.

sections.tip: The $1 million gross receipts test is based on average annual receipts, not current quarter receipts. Calculate this carefully based on your actual operating history.

sections.heading: The $50,000 Per Quarter Limitation
sections.content: Unlike standard ERC claims, Recovery Startup Businesses face a hard cap on the credit:

list.title: Maximum credit of $50,000 per quarter
list.description: Regardless of how many employees you have or how much in qualified wages you paid, your ERC is capped at $50,000 per quarter.

list.title: Standard calculation still applies
list.description: You still calculate qualified wages and multiply by 70% (the 2021 credit rate), but the result cannot exceed $50,000.

list.title: Two quarters available
list.description: With Q3 and Q4 2021 both eligible, the maximum RSB ERC is $100,000 total across both quarters.

list.title: All qualified wages count
list.description: Unlike large employers (over 500 employees), RSBs can claim credit on all employee wages, not just wages paid while not providing services.

sections.heading: Proving You Started After February 15, 2020
sections.content: The IRS will closely examine whether your business actually began operations after the critical date:

list.title: Date of first revenue
list.description: When did you first receive payment for goods or services? This is often the clearest indicator of when you began carrying on a trade or business.

list.title: Business registration documents
list.description: State filings, business licenses, and incorporation documents showing dates of formation.

list.title: First payroll records
list.description: When did you first pay employees? Payroll records establish when business activity began.

list.title: First contracts or customer relationships
list.description: Documentation of your first business transactions.

list.title: Pre-existing business issues
list.description: If you operated a similar business before and 'restarted' after February 15, 2020, the IRS may argue this isn't a new business.

sections.warning: The IRS is scrutinizing RSB claims for businesses that may have existed in some form before February 15, 2020, and later reorganized or rebranded. Be prepared to prove your business genuinely began operations after that date.

sections.heading: Calculating Average Annual Gross Receipts
sections.content: Meeting the $1 million average annual gross receipts threshold requires proper calculation:

list.title: Three-year lookback period
list.description: Normally, you average gross receipts over the three tax years ending with the year before the quarter you're claiming.

list.title: Shorter periods for new businesses
list.description: If you haven't been in business for three full years, average over the tax years you have been in business.

list.title: Annualized for partial years
list.description: If you were only in business for part of a year, annualize that year's receipts for the average calculation.

list.title: Aggregation rules apply
list.description: If you're part of a controlled group or affiliated service group, combine gross receipts of all related entities.

sections.tip: For a business that started in late 2020, you might be calculating based on only a few months of actual operations. Make sure to properly annualize and document your calculation methodology.

sections.heading: Common RSB Documentation Failures
sections.content: The IRS has denied many RSB claims for these reasons:

list.title: Business existed before February 15, 2020
list.description: The business or a substantially similar predecessor operated before the cutoff date, even if under a different name or structure.

list.title: Also qualified under other tests
list.description: RSB status is only for businesses that don't otherwise qualify. If you had a gross receipts decline or suspension, you should claim under those provisions instead.

list.title: Gross receipts exceeded $1 million average
list.description: The business grew quickly and exceeded the annual gross receipts threshold, disqualifying it from RSB status.

list.title: Claiming wrong quarters
list.description: RSB only applies to Q3 and Q4 2021. Claims for earlier quarters under RSB status are invalid.

list.title: Insufficient documentation of start date
list.description: Unable to prove when business operations actually began with contemporaneous records.

takeaways: RSB eligibility requires starting your business after February 15, 2020
Average annual gross receipts must not exceed $1 million
Maximum credit is $50,000 per quarter, only available for Q3 and Q4 2021
You cannot claim RSB status if you also qualify under the suspension or gross receipts tests
Document your business start date with formation documents, first revenue, and first payroll
content.ctaText: Claiming ERC as a Recovery Startup Business? Get expert help ensuring your documentation meets IRS requirements.

faqs.question: What is a Recovery Startup Business for ERC purposes?
faqs.answer: A Recovery Startup Business (RSB) is an employer that began carrying on a trade or business after February 15, 2020, and has average annual gross receipts of $1 million or less. RSBs can claim up to $50,000 in ERC per quarter for Q3 and Q4 2021, even without proving operational suspension or gross receipts decline.

faqs.question: What quarters can Recovery Startup Businesses claim ERC?
faqs.answer: RSB eligibility only applies to the third quarter (July-September) and fourth quarter (October-December) of 2021. There is no RSB category for 2020 quarters. The maximum total credit is $100,000 ($50,000 per quarter).

faqs.question: Can I claim as both an RSB and under the regular ERC tests?
faqs.answer: No. If you qualify under the government order suspension test or gross receipts decline test for a quarter, you should claim under those provisions and cannot also claim as an RSB. RSB status is specifically for businesses that don't meet other eligibility criteria.

faqs.question: How does the IRS determine when my business started?
faqs.answer: The IRS looks at when you began carrying on a trade or business, which typically means when you first had revenue, customers, or employees. They examine business formation documents, first payroll records, initial contracts, and first income—not just when you formed an LLC or filed paperwork.

pages.title: PPP and ERC Coordination: Wage Allocation Rules Explained
pages.description: Learn how PPP loans and ERC interact. Wages used for PPP forgiveness cannot be used for ERC. Understand allocation rules and avoid audit issues.
pages.h1: PPP and ERC Coordination: How to Allocate Wages Correctly

content.intro: If your business received a Paycheck Protection Program (PPP) loan and also claimed the Employee Retention Credit, you need to understand the wage coordination rules. The fundamental principle is simple: the same wages cannot be used for both PPP forgiveness and ERC. However, the details of proper allocation are complex, and errors in this area are one of the most common reasons for ERC claim denials and audit adjustments.

sections.heading: The Basic Rule: No Double-Dipping
sections.content: Congress specifically prohibited using the same wages for both PPP and ERC. Here's what this means in practice:

list.title: Wages used for PPP forgiveness
list.description: Any wages you included in your PPP loan forgiveness application cannot also be claimed as qualified wages for the Employee Retention Credit.

list.title: Choose where to allocate wages
list.description: If you have wages that could count for either program, you must decide which program to apply them to. You cannot claim both.

list.title: Historical context
list.description: Initially, businesses couldn't claim both PPP and ERC at all. The rules changed in late 2020 to allow both, but with this strict coordination requirement.

list.title: Applies to all PPP loans
list.description: Whether you had a first draw or second draw PPP loan, the same coordination rules apply.

sections.warning: The IRS has identified PPP/ERC overlap as a major audit focus area. Improper wage coordination is one of the top reasons for ERC denials.

sections.heading: How to Properly Allocate Wages
sections.content: Proper wage allocation requires careful planning and documentation:

list.title: Maximize benefit strategy
list.description: Generally, you should allocate the minimum wages necessary to PPP forgiveness and maximize ERC-eligible wages, since ERC is typically worth more per dollar of wages.

list.title: PPP covered period wages first
list.description: Identify which wages were used for your PPP forgiveness application—these are automatically excluded from ERC.

list.title: Remaining wages for ERC
list.description: Only wages NOT used for PPP forgiveness can be counted as qualified wages for ERC purposes.

list.title: Healthcare costs coordination
list.description: Healthcare costs follow the same rules. You can use healthcare costs for PPP or ERC, but not both.

list.title: Consider timing
list.description: If your PPP covered period and ERC-eligible quarters overlap, careful allocation is especially important.

sections.tip: Work backward from your PPP forgiveness application. The wages you reported there are locked in—you cannot change that allocation now. Your ERC claim must use different wages.

sections.heading: Documentation Requirements for Audit Defense
sections.content: When the IRS audits your ERC claim, they will specifically ask about PPP coordination:

list.title: PPP loan forgiveness application
list.description: A copy of your SBA Form 3508 or 3508-EZ showing exactly which wages were used for forgiveness.

list.title: Payroll records for covered period
list.description: Detailed payroll records for the PPP covered period showing all wages paid.

list.title: ERC wage calculation workpapers
list.description: Documentation showing how you determined which wages were available for ERC after excluding PPP wages.

list.title: Timeline reconciliation
list.description: A clear reconciliation showing PPP covered period dates vs. ERC-eligible quarters and how wages were allocated.

list.title: Employee-level detail
list.description: For complex situations, employee-by-employee wage allocation may be necessary.

sections.heading: Common Coordination Mistakes
sections.content: These errors frequently trigger audit adjustments and claim denials:

list.title: Using same wages for both
list.description: The most basic error: claiming the same wages on both your PPP forgiveness application and Form 941-X for ERC.

list.title: Forgetting about second draw PPP
list.description: Businesses with two PPP loans sometimes coordinate wages with the first loan but overlook the second draw.

list.title: Healthcare cost overlap
list.description: Using the same employer-paid health insurance costs for both PPP and ERC.

list.title: Not reducing ERC after forgiveness
list.description: If you claimed ERC before PPP forgiveness was approved, you may need to reconcile after forgiveness.

list.title: Timing confusion
list.description: Misunderstanding which quarters overlap with your PPP covered period.

sections.warning: If you used an ERC mill or promoter, they may not have properly coordinated with your PPP loan. Review your claim carefully.

sections.heading: What If You Made Coordination Errors?
sections.content: If you discover PPP/ERC coordination errors in your claim, you have options:

list.title: IRS Voluntary Disclosure Program
list.description: The IRS offered a program for businesses to voluntarily correct improper ERC claims, potentially reducing penalties.

list.title: ERC Withdrawal Program
list.description: If your claim hasn't been processed, you may be able to withdraw it entirely and refile correctly.

list.title: Amend Form 941-X
list.description: You can file an amended employment tax return to correct errors, though this may trigger IRS review.

list.title: Proactive correction before audit
list.description: Fixing errors before the IRS finds them generally results in better outcomes than being caught in an audit.

list.title: Seek professional guidance
list.description: Given the complexity and potential penalties, professional help is strongly recommended for coordination errors.

takeaways: The same wages CANNOT be used for both PPP loan forgiveness and Employee Retention Credit
Identify wages used in your PPP forgiveness application first—these are excluded from ERC
Generally, maximize ERC wages since the credit is typically more valuable per dollar than PPP
Keep detailed documentation showing how you allocated wages between programs
If you made coordination errors, voluntary correction programs may reduce penalties
content.ctaText: Worried about PPP and ERC coordination in your claim? Get a professional review before the IRS audits your business.

faqs.question: Can I claim both PPP and ERC?
faqs.answer: Yes, but the same wages cannot be used for both. Wages included in your PPP loan forgiveness application are excluded from the Employee Retention Credit. You must allocate wages to one program or the other, not both.

faqs.question: Which should I use wages for—PPP or ERC?
faqs.answer: Generally, ERC is more valuable per dollar of wages (up to $7,000 credit per employee per quarter in 2021) compared to PPP. The optimal strategy is typically to use the minimum wages necessary for PPP forgiveness and maximize wages for ERC.

faqs.question: How do I know if my ERC claim properly coordinated with PPP?
faqs.answer: Review your PPP forgiveness application to identify exactly which wages were used. Then compare to your Form 941-X ERC claims. The qualified wages on your ERC claim should not include any wages reported on your PPP forgiveness application.

faqs.question: What happens if I used the same wages for both PPP and ERC?
faqs.answer: This is improper and will result in ERC denial or clawback if discovered. If you identify this error, consider the IRS Voluntary Disclosure Program or withdrawing your ERC claim. Voluntary correction before an audit generally results in better outcomes.

pages.title: Full vs Partial Suspension: ERC Eligibility Differences Explained
pages.description: Understand the difference between full and partial suspension for ERC eligibility. Learn the 10% nominal threshold and documentation requirements.
pages.h1: Full vs Partial Suspension: Critical Distinctions for ERC Eligibility

content.intro: Understanding the difference between full and partial suspension is crucial for ERC eligibility under the government order test. While full suspension cases are relatively straightforward, most ERC claims are based on partial suspension—and these claims face intense IRS scrutiny. The distinction affects not only whether you qualify, but how you must document your eligibility and what the IRS will demand during an audit.

sections.heading: Defining Full Suspension
sections.content: Full suspension occurs when a government order completely prohibits your business from operating:

list.title: Complete shutdown required
list.description: Your business was not allowed to operate at all—doors closed, no services provided, no sales made.

list.title: Relatively rare situation
list.description: True full suspension applied to relatively few businesses: certain indoor entertainment venues, gyms during specific periods, some dine-in restaurants.

list.title: Clear documentation
list.description: Full suspension is easier to document because the government order explicitly prohibited operations.

list.title: Duration matters
list.description: Full suspension typically didn't last entire quarters. Businesses often transitioned to partial suspension when they could reopen with restrictions.

sections.heading: Understanding Partial Suspension
sections.content: Partial suspension is the basis for most ERC claims and requires more nuanced analysis:

list.title: Operations modified, not halted
list.description: Your business could operate but was forced to change how it operated due to government orders.

list.title: More than nominal threshold
list.description: The modification must have affected more than a nominal portion of your business—generally considered more than 10% of operations.

list.title: Multiple qualifying scenarios
list.description: Partial suspension can result from capacity limits, service modifications, operating hour restrictions, or prohibited activities within an otherwise operating business.

list.title: Most common eligibility basis
list.description: The majority of ERC claims under the government order test rely on partial suspension rather than full suspension.

sections.tip: If you're claiming partial suspension, think about what specific operations you couldn't perform—not just that you were generally affected by COVID-19.

sections.heading: The 10% Nominal Effect Threshold
sections.content: The IRS uses a nominal effect threshold to determine whether a partial suspension qualifies:

list.title: More than nominal required
list.description: A government order must have had more than a nominal effect on your business operations. Effects at or below 10% are generally considered nominal.

list.title: Multiple measurement methods
list.description: You can measure the effect by revenue impact, portion of operations affected, percentage of employees impacted, or reduction in service capacity.

list.title: IRS guidance unclear
list.description: The IRS has not provided precise rules for calculating the 10% threshold, leading to disputes during audits.

list.title: Document your calculation
list.description: Whatever methodology you use, document it clearly and be prepared to defend it.

sections.warning: If your suspension affected less than 10% of operations, you likely don't qualify under the government order test. Consider whether you qualify under the gross receipts test instead.

sections.heading: Documenting Partial Suspension for Audit Defense
sections.content: Partial suspension claims require robust documentation because they're inherently subjective:

list.title: Specific government orders
list.description: Identify the exact orders that imposed restrictions, including effective dates and the specific provisions that affected your business.

list.title: Operational impact analysis
list.description: Document what you couldn't do before the order and what changed after. Be specific about services, capacity, hours, or activities.

list.title: Quantification of impact
list.description: Calculate the percentage of your business affected. Use revenue data, operational metrics, or employee hours as support.

list.title: Before and after comparisons
list.description: Show how your operations differed during the suspension period compared to normal operations.

list.title: Contemporaneous records
list.description: Records created at the time (emails, policy changes, customer notices) are more credible than documents created later for the ERC claim.

sections.heading: Common Partial Suspension Audit Issues
sections.content: The IRS frequently challenges partial suspension claims on these grounds:

list.title: No direct connection to government order
list.description: Claiming COVID affected your business generally, without citing a specific government order that imposed specific restrictions on your operations.

list.title: Essential business without additional proof
list.description: Being essential doesn't disqualify you, but you need to show which specific operations were suspended despite being allowed to remain open.

list.title: Voluntary measures vs. mandated changes
list.description: Safety protocols you chose to implement aren't the same as restrictions imposed by government orders.

list.title: Nominal effect not overcome
list.description: The IRS may determine that the actual operational impact was less than 10% and therefore doesn't qualify.

list.title: Inadequate documentation
list.description: Generic claims without specific evidence of what operations changed and how significantly.

takeaways: Full suspension means complete shutdown; partial suspension means modified operations—most claims are partial
Partial suspension must affect more than a nominal portion of operations (generally more than 10%)
Document the specific government order AND how it specifically changed your operations
Essential businesses can qualify for partial suspension but face heightened documentation requirements
Voluntary safety measures don't count—only government-mandated restrictions create eligibility
content.ctaText: Claiming partial suspension for ERC? Ensure your documentation meets IRS standards before an audit.

faqs.question: What is the difference between full and partial suspension for ERC?
faqs.answer: Full suspension means a government order completely prohibited your business from operating. Partial suspension means you could operate but government orders forced you to modify how you operated in a way that affected more than a nominal portion of your business.

faqs.question: What does 'more than nominal' mean for partial suspension?
faqs.answer: The IRS considers effects of 10% or less to be nominal. For partial suspension to qualify for ERC, the government order must have affected more than 10% of your business operations, whether measured by revenue, services, capacity, or employee activities.

faqs.question: How do I prove partial suspension?
faqs.answer: Document the specific government order that imposed restrictions, explain how it applied to your business, and quantify the impact on your operations. Include before and after comparisons, operational metrics, and contemporaneous records showing how you modified operations in compliance with the order.

faqs.question: Can I claim partial suspension if I was an essential business?
faqs.answer: Yes. Essential businesses can qualify for ERC based on partial suspension if government orders still restricted their operations—such as capacity limits, service modifications, or suspension of certain business activities within the otherwise operating business. You need additional documentation proving these restrictions.

pages.title: Already Received ERC? What to Do If You're Now Worried About Audits
pages.description: Received your ERC refund but now worried about IRS audits? Learn warning signs, how to self-assess your claim, and steps to protect your business.
pages.h1: Already Received Your ERC? Here's What to Do If You're Worried

content.intro: You claimed the Employee Retention Credit, received your refund, and moved on—until you started hearing about IRS audits, clawbacks, and ERC mills. Now you're wondering if your claim was legitimate and what might happen next. You're not alone. Millions of businesses are in the same position, and there are concrete steps you can take right now.

sections.heading: Why Businesses Are Worried Now
sections.content: The ERC landscape has changed dramatically since you filed your claim. Several developments have many business owners concerned:

list.title: Massive IRS enforcement
list.description: The IRS has sent over 84,000 audit letters (Letter 6612) and disallowed over 22,000 claims. This is just the beginning of their enforcement efforts.

list.title: ERC mill scrutiny
list.description: The IRS is specifically targeting claims filed by aggressive promoters who used questionable eligibility criteria.

list.title: New penalties
list.description: Recent legislation added a 20% erroneous refund claim penalty on top of interest and other penalties.

list.title: Criminal investigations
list.description: The IRS Criminal Investigation division has opened hundreds of cases involving fraudulent ERC claims.

sections.heading: Red Flags That Suggest Your Claim May Have Issues
sections.content: Not all ERC claims are at equal risk. Review your situation for these warning signs:

list.title: You claimed through an ERC mill
list.description: If a company promised you a large refund, charged contingency fees, and did minimal due diligence, your claim may be problematic.

list.title: You can't explain why you qualified
list.description: If you don't know whether you claimed under government orders or gross receipts decline, that's a red flag.

list.title: Your business operated normally
list.description: If you had no meaningful operational impact from COVID, your claim may not hold up.

list.title: You claimed for essential business operations
list.description: Essential businesses face extra scrutiny and need to prove partial suspension or supply chain disruption.

list.title: You claimed all eligible quarters at maximum
list.description: Blanket claims for every quarter at maximum amounts attract IRS attention.

sections.warning: If multiple red flags apply to your situation, consider proactive steps before the IRS contacts you.

sections.heading: How to Self-Assess Your ERC Claim
sections.content: Before you panic, objectively evaluate whether your claim was legitimate:

steps.title: Review your eligibility basis
steps.description: Did you claim based on government orders (partial or full suspension) or gross receipts decline? Can you identify the specific basis?

steps.title: Find the government orders
steps.description: If you claimed under government orders, locate the specific federal, state, or local orders that affected your operations.

steps.title: Document the impact
steps.description: How did those orders actually affect your business operations? What couldn't you do that you normally did?

steps.title: Check your gross receipts math
steps.description: If you claimed under gross receipts decline, verify the quarterly comparisons to 2019 meet the required thresholds.

steps.title: Verify PPP coordination
steps.description: Confirm that wages used for ERC weren't also used for PPP forgiveness.

sections.tip: If you can clearly answer these questions with documentation, your claim is likely defensible. If you struggle to answer them, you have legitimate reasons for concern.

sections.heading: Your Options If You're Concerned
sections.content: Depending on your self-assessment, you have several paths forward:

list.title: Do nothing and wait
list.description: If your claim is solid, you may simply prepare your documentation and wait. Not every claim will be audited.

list.title: Get a professional review
list.description: Have a tax attorney or CPA who specializes in ERC review your claim and identify vulnerabilities before the IRS does.

list.title: Voluntary disclosure program
list.description: The IRS may offer settlement programs for businesses that come forward voluntarily. These typically reduce penalties.

list.title: ERC withdrawal program
list.description: For claims that haven't been paid yet, the IRS allows you to withdraw without penalty. This doesn't apply if you've already received funds.

list.title: Amend your returns
list.description: If you know your claim was wrong, you can file amended returns and repay the credit, potentially reducing penalties.

sections.heading: Preparing for a Potential Audit
sections.content: Whether or not you're worried, every business that received ERC should prepare for possible IRS examination:

list.title: Organize your documentation
list.description: Gather all government orders, gross receipts records, payroll data, and PPP documentation in one place.

list.title: Create an eligibility narrative
list.description: Write down exactly why you believed you qualified and what impact you experienced.

list.title: Preserve all records
list.description: Keep everything related to your ERC claim for at least 7 years from the filing date.

list.title: Know who prepared your claim
list.description: Document the preparer's name, company, and the information they provided about eligibility.

list.title: Identify professional representation
list.description: Know who you'll call if you receive an audit letter—don't scramble when the 30-day deadline starts.

takeaways: Many businesses that received ERC are now legitimately concerned about audits
Self-assess your claim by identifying your eligibility basis and supporting documentation
Red flags include ERC mill involvement, inability to explain eligibility, and claiming maximum amounts for all quarters
Options include waiting, getting professional review, voluntary disclosure, or amending returns
Every ERC recipient should organize documentation and prepare for potential examination
content.ctaText: Worried about your ERC claim? Get a confidential professional review before the IRS contacts you.

faqs.question: What are the chances my ERC claim will be audited?
faqs.answer: The IRS has sent over 84,000 audit letters and is prioritizing claims with high-risk indicators: large amounts, ERC mill preparers, claims for all quarters, and essential businesses. While not every claim will be audited, the rate is significant and increasing.

faqs.question: Can I return the ERC money voluntarily?
faqs.answer: Yes. You can file amended payroll tax returns (Form 941-X) to correct your claim and repay the credit. Doing so voluntarily may reduce penalties and demonstrates good faith if the IRS later reviews your file.

faqs.question: Should I be worried if I used an ERC mill?
faqs.answer: You should be cautious but not necessarily worried. Many ERC mill claims are legitimate, but the aggressive tactics of some mills mean their claims face heightened scrutiny. Have your claim professionally reviewed to assess your specific situation.

faqs.question: What documentation should I have ready?
faqs.answer: At minimum, you should have: the government orders you relied on, evidence of how those orders impacted your operations, gross receipts records for relevant quarters and 2019, payroll records for qualified wages, and PPP loan and forgiveness documentation.

pages.title: ERC Promoter Scam: What to Do If You Were Victimized by an ERC Mill
pages.description: Victimized by an aggressive ERC promoter or mill? Learn how to assess your exposure, your legal options, and how to protect yourself from IRS enforcement.
pages.h1: ERC Mill Victim? What to Do If You Were Scammed by a Promoter

content.intro: You received a cold call, email, or mailer promising a huge ERC refund. The company made it sound easy—they'd handle everything, you just had to sign. Now you've learned that ERC mills have filed thousands of fraudulent claims, and the IRS is coming after businesses that received them. If this sounds familiar, you may be an ERC promoter victim. Here's what you need to know and do.

sections.heading: How to Know If You Were Victimized
sections.content: Not all ERC preparers are mills, and not all mill claims are fraudulent. But certain patterns indicate problematic promoters:

list.title: Unsolicited contact
list.description: They reached out to you first through cold calls, mass emails, or mailings promising large refunds.

list.title: Contingency fees
list.description: They charged a percentage of your refund (often 20-30%) rather than a flat fee for services.

list.title: Guaranteed results
list.description: They promised specific refund amounts or guaranteed approval before reviewing your situation.

list.title: Minimal due diligence
list.description: They asked few questions about your actual business operations and COVID impact.

list.title: They signed your returns
list.description: They may have signed Form 941-X themselves or had you sign without full explanation.

list.title: Supply chain claims
list.description: They claimed you qualified through supply chain disruption or similar broad arguments.

list.title: They've disappeared
list.description: The company is now unreachable, out of business, or not returning calls.

sections.warning: If multiple indicators apply, you were likely dealing with a problematic ERC mill. This doesn't automatically mean your claim was fraudulent, but it requires immediate attention.

sections.heading: You Are Still Responsible
sections.content: This is the hardest truth for ERC mill victims: even if you were misled, you are responsible for the accuracy of your tax returns. The IRS will hold your business accountable, not the promoter. This means:

list.title: You must repay erroneous credits
list.description: If your claim was improper, you'll owe the money back regardless of who prepared the claim.

list.title: Penalties apply to you
list.description: Accuracy and erroneous refund penalties are assessed against your business.

list.title: Interest accrues on your account
list.description: Interest has been building since you received the refund.

list.title: Your business faces collection
list.description: The IRS will pursue your business assets, not the promoter's.

sections.tip: While you're responsible to the IRS, you may have legal claims against the promoter. These are separate issues that require different strategies.

sections.heading: Immediate Steps to Take
sections.content: If you believe you were victimized by an ERC mill, take these steps now:

steps.title: Gather all documentation
steps.description: Collect every email, contract, form, and communication with the promoter. You'll need this for both IRS defense and potential legal action.

steps.title: Get a copy of what was filed
steps.description: Obtain copies of the Form 941-X returns filed on your behalf. Review what eligibility basis was claimed.

steps.title: Assess your actual eligibility
steps.description: Have a qualified professional (not the promoter) evaluate whether you legitimately qualified for any ERC.

steps.title: Report the promoter
steps.description: File IRS Form 14242 to report suspected abusive tax promoters. This creates a record that you were a victim.

steps.title: Consult a tax attorney
steps.description: You need professional guidance on both defending against the IRS and potential claims against the promoter.

sections.heading: Your Options Going Forward
sections.content: Depending on your situation, several paths may be available:

list.title: ERC Voluntary Disclosure Program
list.description: The IRS may offer settlement programs allowing you to repay credits with reduced penalties if you come forward before being contacted.

list.title: Amended returns
list.description: File corrected Form 941-X returns to reduce or eliminate improper claims before the IRS assesses penalties.

list.title: Claim withdrawal
list.description: If your refund hasn't been paid yet, you may be able to withdraw the claim entirely without penalty.

list.title: Prepare for audit
list.description: If you believe part of your claim was legitimate, prepare documentation to defend the valid portion.

list.title: Pursue the promoter legally
list.description: You may have claims against the promoter for malpractice, fraud, or breach of contract to recover losses.

sections.heading: Legal Action Against the Promoter
sections.content: While your first priority is managing IRS exposure, you may have claims against the ERC mill:

list.title: Professional malpractice
list.description: If the promoter was a CPA or tax professional, they may be liable for failing to meet professional standards.

list.title: Fraud and misrepresentation
list.description: If they made false statements about your eligibility or the safety of the claim.

list.title: Breach of contract
list.description: If they failed to provide the services promised or violated engagement terms.

list.title: Consumer protection violations
list.description: Many states have laws against deceptive business practices that may apply.

list.title: Class action participation
list.description: Some law firms are organizing class actions against major ERC mills.

sections.tip: Document your damages carefully, including: fees paid to the promoter, amounts you must repay to the IRS, penalties and interest, professional fees for remediation, and business disruption costs.

takeaways: Signs of an ERC mill include contingency fees, minimal due diligence, unsolicited contact, and guaranteed results
Even as a victim, you're responsible to the IRS for the accuracy of your returns
Immediate steps: gather documentation, get copies of filed returns, assess actual eligibility, report the promoter
Options include voluntary disclosure, amended returns, claim withdrawal, or preparing for audit
You may have legal claims against the promoter, but IRS issues must be addressed first
content.ctaText: Victimized by an ERC mill? Get confidential help understanding your exposure and options before the IRS contacts you.

faqs.question: Can I blame the ERC promoter and avoid repaying the IRS?
faqs.answer: No. The IRS holds taxpayers responsible for the accuracy of their returns regardless of who prepared them. You must address your IRS liability first. You may separately pursue the promoter for damages, but that won't eliminate your obligation to the IRS.

faqs.question: Should I report the ERC mill to the IRS?
faqs.answer: Yes. File Form 14242 (Report of Suspected Abusive Tax Promoter or Preparer) with the IRS. This creates a record that you were a victim of an abusive promoter, which may help in penalty abatement requests and demonstrates good faith.

faqs.question: What if the ERC company has gone out of business?
faqs.answer: Unfortunately, this is common. You can still file Form 14242, preserve documentation for potential future legal action, and focus on managing your IRS exposure. Some attorneys are pursuing principals of defunct mills personally.

faqs.question: Will I face criminal prosecution?
faqs.answer: Criminal prosecution typically requires willful fraud, not just an erroneous claim. If you genuinely believed you qualified based on what the promoter told you, criminal charges are unlikely. However, if you knew the claim was false, the risk is higher. Consult with a tax attorney if you're concerned.

pages.title: ERC 2020 vs 2021: Key Differences in Employee Retention Credit Rules
pages.description: Understand the critical differences between 2020 and 2021 ERC rules. Credit amounts, eligibility thresholds, and qualification requirements changed significantly.
pages.h1: ERC 2020 vs 2021: What Changed and Why It Matters

content.intro: The Employee Retention Credit rules changed significantly between 2020 and 2021. These differences affect everything from how much credit you could claim to which quarters you qualified for to how the credit interacts with PPP loans. Understanding these distinctions is critical for defending your claim if the IRS questions which rules applied to your situation.

sections.heading: Credit Amount Differences
sections.content: The most significant change between 2020 and 2021 was the credit amount itself:

list.title: 2020 Credit Rate
list.description: 50% of qualified wages, up to $10,000 in wages PER EMPLOYEE for the entire year. Maximum credit: $5,000 per employee for all of 2020.

list.title: 2021 Credit Rate
list.description: 70% of qualified wages, up to $10,000 in wages PER EMPLOYEE PER QUARTER. Maximum credit: $7,000 per employee per quarter, or $21,000-$28,000 per employee for 2021.

sections.tip: The 2021 credit was significantly more generous—both a higher percentage (70% vs 50%) and per-quarter caps instead of annual caps. This is why many businesses had much larger 2021 claims.

sections.heading: Gross Receipts Test Changes
sections.content: The threshold for qualifying based on decline in gross receipts changed:

list.title: 2020 Threshold
list.description: Gross receipts must be less than 50% of the same quarter in 2019. This was a steep decline—businesses had to lose more than half their revenue.

list.title: 2021 Threshold
list.description: Gross receipts must be less than 80% of the same quarter in 2019. This 20% decline threshold made many more businesses eligible.

list.title: Alternative comparison for 2021
list.description: For 2021, businesses could also use the immediately preceding quarter's comparison to 2019 if it was more favorable.

sections.warning: When defending your claim, make sure you applied the correct threshold for the year. A 30% decline qualifies you for 2021 but not 2020.

sections.heading: Employee Count Thresholds
sections.content: The definition of large employer changed, affecting which wages qualified:

list.title: 2020 Large Employer
list.description: More than 100 full-time employees. Large employers could only claim wages for employees NOT providing services.

list.title: 2021 Large Employer
list.description: More than 500 full-time employees. This expanded who counted as a small employer.

list.title: Small employer advantage
list.description: Small employers could claim wages for ALL employees during eligible quarters, whether they were working or not.

sections.tip: A business with 200 employees was a large employer in 2020 (limiting qualified wages) but a small employer in 2021 (expanding qualified wages). This dramatically affected claim amounts.

sections.heading: PPP Coordination Rules
sections.content: Perhaps the most confusing change involved how ERC interacted with PPP loans:

list.title: Original 2020 Rule
list.description: If you received a PPP loan, you could NOT claim ERC at all. These programs were mutually exclusive.

list.title: December 2020 Change
list.description: The Consolidated Appropriations Act (CAA) changed the rules retroactively. PPP recipients COULD now claim ERC, but not on the same wages used for PPP forgiveness.

list.title: Wage allocation requirement
list.description: You must show that wages claimed for ERC were NOT included in your PPP forgiveness application. This requires careful documentation.

list.title: Common audit issue
list.description: The IRS frequently questions whether proper wage allocation occurred between PPP and ERC. This is a major audit focus.

sections.warning: If you received PPP and claimed ERC, be prepared to show exactly which wages went to each program. Double-dipping on the same wages is a top audit target.

sections.heading: Eligible Quarters and Timing
sections.content: The quarters during which you could claim ERC differed by year:

list.title: 2020 Eligible Quarters
list.description: Q2 2020 (March 13-June 30), Q3 2020, and Q4 2020. The credit started mid-March when COVID was declared a national emergency.

list.title: 2021 Eligible Quarters
list.description: Q1 2021, Q2 2021, Q3 2021, and for recovery startup businesses only, Q4 2021.

list.title: Infrastructure Act limitation
list.description: For most businesses, ERC eligibility ended after Q3 2021 due to the Infrastructure Investment and Jobs Act—except for recovery startup businesses.

list.title: Startup special rules
list.description: Recovery startup businesses (new businesses started after February 15, 2020) had unique eligibility for Q3 and Q4 2021 even without government order impact or gross receipts decline.

takeaways: 2021 credit was 70% (vs 50%) and capped per quarter (vs per year), making it significantly more valuable
Gross receipts threshold was 50% decline for 2020 but only 20% decline for 2021
Large employer threshold was 100 employees in 2020 but 500 in 2021
PPP coordination rules changed in December 2020—retroactively allowing dual claims with wage allocation
Most businesses could only claim through Q3 2021; Q4 2021 was limited to recovery startups
content.ctaText: Not sure which rules applied to your claim? Get a professional eligibility assessment to understand your audit exposure.

faqs.question: Why was the 2021 ERC credit larger than 2020?
faqs.answer: Congress expanded the credit in December 2020 through the Consolidated Appropriations Act. The credit rate increased from 50% to 70%, and the cap changed from $10,000 per employee per year to $10,000 per employee per quarter, making the 2021 credit potentially 4-5 times larger.

faqs.question: Could I claim ERC if I had a PPP loan?
faqs.answer: Originally no, but the rules changed in December 2020. After that change, you could claim both, but you could not use the same wages for both programs. You must show clear allocation of which wages went to PPP forgiveness vs. ERC.

faqs.question: What was the gross receipts threshold for ERC eligibility?
faqs.answer: For 2020, gross receipts had to decline more than 50% compared to the same quarter in 2019. For 2021, the threshold was relaxed to a 20% decline. Additionally, 2021 allowed you to use the prior quarter's comparison if more favorable.

faqs.question: When did ERC eligibility end?
faqs.answer: For most businesses, ERC eligibility ended after Q3 2021 when the Infrastructure Investment and Jobs Act was passed. Only recovery startup businesses (started after February 15, 2020, with under $1M average gross receipts) could claim for Q4 2021.

pages.title: Infrastructure Act ERC Changes: How the 2021 Law Ended the Credit Early
pages.description: The Infrastructure Investment and Jobs Act ended ERC for most businesses after Q3 2021. Learn what changed, exceptions, and how this affects your claim.
pages.h1: How the Infrastructure Act Changed ERC Eligibility

content.intro: In November 2021, the Infrastructure Investment and Jobs Act (IIJA) made a significant change to the Employee Retention Credit that caught many businesses off guard. The law retroactively ended ERC eligibility for most employers after the third quarter of 2021—meaning Q4 2021 claims were suddenly invalid for nearly everyone. If you claimed ERC for Q4 2021 and you're not a recovery startup business, your claim may be at risk.

sections.heading: What the Infrastructure Act Changed
sections.content: The American Rescue Plan had originally extended ERC through the end of 2021. Businesses were planning to claim for all four quarters. Then on November 15, 2021, President Biden signed the Infrastructure Investment and Jobs Act, which included this provision:

list.title: Early termination
list.description: ERC was terminated after September 30, 2021 (Q3 2021) for most employers—not December 31, 2021 as previously planned.

list.title: Retroactive application
list.description: The change applied retroactively, meaning Q4 2021 wages that businesses thought qualified suddenly didn't.

list.title: Recovery startup exception
list.description: Only recovery startup businesses could still claim ERC for Q4 2021.

list.title: Budget offset
list.description: Congress ended ERC early to help pay for infrastructure spending—the ERC savings were estimated at $8.6 billion.

sections.warning: If you claimed ERC for Q4 2021 and are NOT a recovery startup business, your claim was invalid from the moment you filed it. The IRS is specifically targeting these claims.

sections.heading: The Recovery Startup Business Exception
sections.content: Recovery startup businesses are the ONLY employers eligible for Q4 2021 ERC. To qualify, you must meet ALL of these requirements:

list.title: Business start date
list.description: You must have started carrying on a trade or business AFTER February 15, 2020.

list.title: Average gross receipts limit
list.description: Your average annual gross receipts for the three prior tax years must be $1 million or less. New businesses use shorter periods.

list.title: No other eligibility route
list.description: Originally, you also couldn't be eligible under the government order or gross receipts tests—though this was relaxed for Q3-Q4 2021.

list.title: Credit cap
list.description: Recovery startups are limited to $50,000 in ERC per quarter—far less than other eligible employers could claim.

sections.tip: Being a new business isn't enough. Many businesses that started in 2020 or 2021 don't meet the strict definition because they evolved from existing businesses, had related-party predecessor entities, or exceeded the gross receipts threshold.

sections.heading: Why This Matters for Your Claim
sections.content: The Infrastructure Act change has significant implications:

list.title: Q4 2021 claims under scrutiny
list.description: The IRS knows many businesses filed Q4 2021 claims that don't qualify. These are prime audit targets.

list.title: ERC mills often ignored this
list.description: Many aggressive promoters continued filing Q4 2021 claims for businesses that clearly weren't recovery startups.

list.title: Timing confusion
list.description: Businesses that filed before November 2021 based on the original rules may not have amended their claims after the law changed.

list.title: Interest and penalties
list.description: If you received a Q4 2021 refund you weren't entitled to, interest has been accruing since payment.

sections.heading: What to Do If You Claimed Q4 2021 ERC
sections.content: If you claimed ERC for Q4 2021, take these steps to assess your situation:

steps.title: Verify your recovery startup status
steps.description: Did your business actually start after February 15, 2020? Review formation documents and when you first had business activity.

steps.title: Check gross receipts history
steps.description: Were average annual gross receipts under $1 million for the relevant prior period?

steps.title: Review what was claimed
steps.description: Did you claim more than $50,000 for Q4 2021? If so, even if you're a recovery startup, the excess isn't valid.

steps.title: Consider voluntary correction
steps.description: If you claimed Q4 2021 and don't qualify as a recovery startup, filing an amended return to repay may reduce penalties.

steps.title: Prepare for audit
steps.description: If you legitimately qualify as a recovery startup, gather documentation proving your start date and gross receipts history.

sections.heading: Timeline of ERC Legislation
sections.content: Understanding how ERC evolved helps explain the confusion:

list.title: March 2020 - CARES Act
list.description: ERC created for Q2-Q4 2020. Originally, PPP recipients couldn't claim ERC at all.

list.title: December 2020 - CAA
list.description: ERC extended through Q2 2021. Credit increased to 70%. PPP recipients could now claim ERC with proper wage allocation.

list.title: March 2021 - ARP
list.description: ERC extended through Q4 2021. Recovery startup business category created.

list.title: November 2021 - IIJA
list.description: ERC terminated after Q3 2021 for most employers. Only recovery startups could claim Q4 2021.

takeaways: The Infrastructure Act retroactively ended ERC after Q3 2021 for most businesses
Only recovery startup businesses could claim ERC for Q4 2021
Recovery startup requires: business started after Feb 15, 2020 AND average gross receipts under $1M
Q4 2021 claims are a major IRS audit target because many were filed improperly
If you claimed Q4 2021 and aren't a recovery startup, consider voluntary correction
content.ctaText: Claimed Q4 2021 ERC? Find out if you actually qualify as a recovery startup business before the IRS contacts you.

faqs.question: When did the Infrastructure Act end ERC?
faqs.answer: The Infrastructure Investment and Jobs Act, signed November 15, 2021, retroactively ended ERC eligibility for most employers after September 30, 2021 (Q3 2021). Only recovery startup businesses could claim for Q4 2021.

faqs.question: What is a recovery startup business?
faqs.answer: A recovery startup business is one that: (1) began carrying on a trade or business after February 15, 2020, and (2) has average annual gross receipts of $1 million or less for the three prior tax years. These are the only businesses that could claim ERC for Q4 2021.

faqs.question: I claimed Q4 2021 ERC—what should I do?
faqs.answer: First, verify whether you qualify as a recovery startup business. If you do, gather documentation of your business start date and gross receipts history. If you don't qualify, consider filing an amended return to repay the credit before the IRS contacts you, which may reduce penalties.

faqs.question: Why did Congress end ERC early?
faqs.answer: Congress used the ERC termination to help offset the cost of infrastructure spending. The estimated savings from ending ERC early was $8.6 billion, which was applied toward funding provisions in the Infrastructure Investment and Jobs Act.
